print(f"Combined activity scores created for {combined_count} users")

# Get top 5 most active users
top_users = r.zrevrange("user:activity:combined", 0, 4, withscores=True)
for user, score in top_users:
    print(f"  {user}: {score:.0f} points")
Combined activity scores created for 47 users user:alice: 850 points user:bob: 720 points user:charlie: 680 points user:diana: 590 points user:eve: 520 points
Example 2: Merge skill assessments with weighted scoring
# Combine technical and soft skills with different weights
# Technical skills weighted 70%, soft skills 30%
combined_skills = r.zunionstore(
    "candidate:123:total_score",
    {
        "candidate:123:technical_skills": 0.7,
        "candidate:123:soft_skills": 0.3
    }
)
print(f"Combined skill assessment complete for {combined_skills} skills")

# Get candidate's top skills
top_skills = r.zrevrange("candidate:123:total_score", 0, 2, withscores=True)
for skill, weighted_score in top_skills:
    print(f"  {skill}: {weighted_score:.2f}")
Combined skill assessment complete for 8 skills python_programming: 8.40 problem_solving: 7.80 communication: 6.90

ZINTERSTORE

Docs: Redis ZINTERSTORE Documentation

Computes the intersection of multiple sorted sets and stores the result in a new sorted set.

Examples with Django models

Example 1: Find users active in multiple areas
# Find users who are active in both forums AND have made purchases
active_buyers = r.zinterstore(
    "users:active_buyers",
    ["users:forum_activity", "users:purchase_activity"]
)
print(f"Active buyers (forum + purchase): {active_buyers}")

# Get their combined engagement scores
engaged_buyers = r.zrevrange("users:active_buyers", 0, 4, withscores=True)
for user, combined_score in engaged_buyers:
    print(f"  {user}: {combined_score:.0f} engagement points")
Active buyers (forum + purchase): 23 user:alice: 145 engagement points user:charlie: 120 engagement points user:eve: 98 engagement points user:george: 87 engagement points user:ian: 76 engagement points
Example 2: Match candidates with overlapping skills
# Find candidates who have skills that match job requirements
matching_candidates = r.zinterstore(
    "job:1:matching_candidates",
    ["job:1:required_skills", "candidates:available_skills"],
    aggregate="MIN"  # Use minimum score to ensure quality threshold
)
print(f"Candidates with matching skills: {matching_candidates}")

# Get best matches (highest minimum skill levels)
best_matches = r.zrevrange("job:1:matching_candidates", 0, 2, withscores=True)
for candidate, min_skill_score in best_matches:
    print(f"  {candidate}: minimum skill level {min_skill_score:.1f}")
Candidates with matching skills: 8 candidate:sarah: minimum skill level 8.5 candidate:mike: minimum skill level 7.8 candidate:anna: minimum skill level 7.2

ZREMRANGEBYLEX

Docs: Redis ZREMRANGEBYLEX Documentation

Removes members from a sorted set within a lexicographical range when all members have the same score.

Full description

ZREMRANGEBYLEX removes members between lexicographical min and max when all members have identical scores. Perfect for cleaning up name ranges, removing obsolete entries, or batch deleting sorted string data in Django applications.

Syntax

ZREMRANGEBYLEX key min max

Parameters

  • key: The name of the sorted set to remove from.
  • min: Minimum lexicographical value. Use "[value" for inclusive, "(value" for exclusive.
  • max: Maximum lexicographical value. Use "[value" for inclusive, "(value" for exclusive.

Return value

Integer: The number of members removed from the sorted set.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Remove users by name range
# Remove users whose names start with 'a' through 'd'
# First, add users with same score for lexicographical ordering
r.zadd("users:alphabetical", {f"user:{name}": 0 for name in ["alice", "bob", "charlie", "diana", "eve", "frank"]})

removed = r.zremrangebylex("users:alphabetical", "[user:a", "[user:d")
remaining = r.zrange("users:alphabetical", 0, -1)
print(f"Removed: {removed}, Remaining: {remaining}")
Removed: 4, Remaining: ['user:eve', 'user:frank']
Example 2: Clean old log entries
# Remove log entries before a certain date (YYYY-MM-DD format)
import datetime

# Add some log entries with same score
today = datetime.date.today()
yesterday = today - datetime.timedelta(days=1)
week_ago = today - datetime.timedelta(days=7)

logs = {
    f"log:{week_ago}": 0,
    f"log:{yesterday}": 0,
    f"log:{today}": 0
}
r.zadd("system:logs", logs)

# Remove logs older than yesterday
removed = r.zremrangebylex("system:logs", "-", f"(log:{yesterday}")
print(f"Cleaned {removed} old log entries")
Cleaned 1 old log entries
Example 3: Remove skills in range
# Remove skills from 'j' to 'p' alphabetically
skills = ["javascript", "java", "python", "php", "react", "sql"]
r.zadd("user:1:skills", {skill: 0 for skill in skills})

removed = r.zremrangebylex("user:1:skills", "[j", "[p")
remaining_skills = r.zrange("user:1:skills", 0, -1)
print(f"Removed: {removed}, Skills left: {remaining_skills}")
Removed: 4, Skills left: ['react', 'sql']
Example 4: Remove products by category prefix
# Remove all products starting with "laptop-"
products = ["laptop-gaming", "laptop-business", "mouse-wireless", "keyboard-mech"]
r.zadd("products:catalog", {product: 0 for product in products})

removed = r.zremrangebylex("products:catalog", "[laptop-", "[laptop.")  # '.' is after '-' in ASCII
remaining = r.zrange("products:catalog", 0, -1)
print(f"Removed {removed} laptops, remaining: {remaining}")
Removed 2 laptops, remaining: ['keyboard-mech', 'mouse-wireless']
Example 5: Cleanup with exclusion
# Remove items between 'b' and 'd' exclusive (not including 'b' and 'd')
items = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "date", "elderberry"]
r.zadd("items:list", {item: 0 for item in items})

removed = r.zremrangebylex("items:list", "(banana", "(date")
remaining = r.zrange("items:list", 0, -1)
print(f"Removed {removed} items, kept: {remaining}")
Removed 1 items, kept: ['apple', 'banana', 'date', 'elderberry']

ZMSCORE

Docs: Redis ZMSCORE Documentation

Returns scores of multiple members in a sorted set in a single operation.

Full description

ZMSCORE retrieves scores for multiple members simultaneously, much more efficient than multiple ZSCORE calls. Perfect for batch score lookups, performance monitoring, leaderboard displays, or any scenario requiring multiple score values in Django applications.

Syntax

ZMSCORE key member [member ...]

Parameters

  • key: The name of the sorted set to query.
  • member: One or more members whose scores to retrieve.

Return value

Array: List of scores for the requested members in the same order, with null for non-existent members.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Get multiple user scores
# Get scores for multiple users at once
users_to_check = ["user:1", "user:2", "user:4"]
scores = r.zmscore("user_scores", *users_to_check)

print("User scores:")
for user, score in zip(users_to_check, scores):
    if score is not None:
        print(f"  {user}: {int(score)} points")
    else:
        print(f"  {user}: Not found")
User scores: user:1: 1050 points user:2: 870 points user:4: 800 points
Example 2: Batch check task priorities
# Check priorities for multiple tasks
tasks = ["review_resume:urgent", "send_email:normal", "update_profile:low"]
priorities = r.zmscore("user:1:tasks", *tasks)

print("Task priorities:")
for task, priority in zip(tasks, priorities):
    if priority is not None:
        task_name = task.split(':')[0]
        print(f"  {task_name}: Priority {int(priority)}")
    else:
        print(f"  {task}: Not found")
Task priorities: review_resume: Priority 10 send_email: Priority 7 update_profile: Priority 2
Example 3: Compare job salaries
# Get salaries for specific jobs
jobs = ["job:2", "job:3", "job:4", "job:999"]  # job:999 doesn't exist
salaries = r.zmscore("job_salaries", *jobs)

print("Job salary comparison:")
for job, salary in zip(jobs, salaries):
    if salary is not None:
        print(f"  {job}: ${int(salary):,}")
    else:
        print(f"  {job}: Position not available")
Job salary comparison: job:2: $85,000 job:3: $100,000 job:4: $120,000 job:999: Position not available
Example 4: Performance dashboard data
# Get performance metrics for dashboard
employees = ["employee:1", "employee:2", "employee:3", "employee:4"]
performance_scores = r.zmscore("employee:performance", *employees)

# Calculate summary statistics
valid_scores = [s for s in performance_scores if s is not None]
avg_performance = sum(valid_scores) / len(valid_scores)

print(f"Team performance summary:")
print(f"  Average: {avg_performance:.1f}%")
print(f"  Active employees: {len(valid_scores)}/{len(employees)}")
print("Individual scores:")
for emp, score in zip(employees, performance_scores):
    status = f"{score:.0f}%" if score else "No data"
    print(f"  {emp}: {status}")
Team performance summary: Average: 89.0% Active employees: 3/4 Individual scores: employee:1: 92% employee:2: 91% employee:3: 84% employee:4: No data
Example 5: Skill level validation
# Check if users meet minimum skill requirements
required_skills = ["python", "django", "postgresql", "docker"]
user_skill_scores = r.zmscore("user:1:skill_ratings", *required_skills)
min_required = 7.0

print("Skill requirement check:")
all_qualified = True
for skill, score in zip(required_skills, user_skill_scores):
    if score is not None:
        qualified = score >= min_required
        status = "✓ Pass" if qualified else "✗ Fail"
        print(f"  {skill}: {score:.1f}/10 {status}")
        if not qualified:
            all_qualified = False
    else:
        print(f"  {skill}: Not assessed ✗ Fail")
        all_qualified = False

print(f"\nOverall qualification: {'✓ QUALIFIED' if all_qualified else '✗ NOT QUALIFIED'}")
Skill requirement check: python: 8.5/10 ✓ Pass django: 7.8/10 ✓ Pass postgresql: 6.5/10 ✗ Fail docker: 7.2/10 ✓ Pass Overall qualification: ✗ NOT QUALIFIED

ZRANDMEMBER

Docs: Redis ZRANDMEMBER Documentation

Returns one or more random members from a sorted set without removing them.

Full description

ZRANDMEMBER returns random members from a sorted set without modification. Perfect for random sampling, A/B testing, random recommendations, selecting random users for surveys, or implementing lottery systems in Django applications.

Syntax

ZRANDMEMBER key [count [WITHSCORES]]

Parameters

  • key: The name of the sorted set to sample from.
  • count: Number of random members to return (default: 1). Positive = unique, negative = allow duplicates.
  • WITHSCORES: Include scores in the result.

Return value

String or Array: Random member(s) from the sorted set, optionally with scores.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Random user selection
# Select random user for survey
random_user = r.zrandmember("user_scores")
print(f"Survey participant: {random_user}")

# Select 3 random users with their scores
random_users = r.zrandmember("user_scores", 3, withscores=True)
print("Random users for focus group:")
for user, score in random_users:
    print(f"  {user}: {int(score)} points")
Survey participant: user:2 Random users for focus group: user:4: 800 points user:1: 1050 points user:2: 870 points
Example 2: Random task assignment
# Randomly assign task to available user
available_task = r.zrandmember("user:1:tasks", withscores=True)
if available_task:
    task, priority = available_task
    task_name = task.split(':')[0]
    print(f"Random task assignment: {task_name} (Priority: {int(priority)})")
else:
    print("No tasks available")
Random task assignment: send_email (Priority: 7)
Example 3: A/B test user selection
# Select users for A/B testing (20% sample)
total_users = r.zcard("user_scores")
sample_size = max(1, int(total_users * 0.2))

test_users = r.zrandmember("user_scores", sample_size)
print(f"A/B Test Group ({sample_size}/{total_users} users):")
for i, user in enumerate(test_users, 1):
    print(f"  {i}. {user}")
A/B Test Group (1/3 users): 1. user:4
Example 4: Random job recommendation
# Show 2 random jobs to user
random_jobs = r.zrandmember("job_salaries", 2, withscores=True)
print("Random job recommendations:")
for job, salary in random_jobs:
    print(f"  {job}: ${int(salary):,}/year")
Random job recommendations: job:3: $100,000/year job:2: $85,000/year
Example 5: Weighted lottery system
# Create lottery entries (higher score = more entries)
lottery_entries = {
    "ticket:bronze": 1,
    "ticket:silver": 5,
    "ticket:gold": 10,
    "ticket:platinum": 20
}
r.zadd("lottery:draw", lottery_entries)

# Draw 3 winners (allowing duplicates for weighted probability)
winners = r.zrandmember("lottery:draw", -3, withscores=True)
print("Lottery results:")
for i, (ticket, weight) in enumerate(winners, 1):
    tier = ticket.split(':')[1]
    print(f"  Draw {i}: {tier} tier (weight: {int(weight)})")

print(f"\nTotal entries: {sum(lottery_entries.values())}")
Lottery results: Draw 1: gold tier (weight: 10) Draw 2: platinum tier (weight: 20) Draw 3: silver tier (weight: 5) Total entries: 36

ZLEXCOUNT

Docs: Redis ZLEXCOUNT Documentation

Counts members in a sorted set within a lexicographical range when all members have the same score.

Full description

ZLEXCOUNT returns the count of members between lexicographical min and max when all members have identical scores. Perfect for counting entries in ranges, analyzing alphabetical distributions, or validating data segments in Django applications.

Syntax

ZLEXCOUNT key min max

Parameters

  • key: The name of the sorted set to count in.
  • min: Minimum lexicographical value. Use "[value" for inclusive, "(value" for exclusive.
  • max: Maximum lexicographical value. Use "[value" for inclusive, "(value" for exclusive.

Return value

Integer: The number of members in the specified lexicographical range.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Count users by name range
# Count users whose names start with 'a' through 'm'
users = ["alice", "bob", "charlie", "diana", "eve", "frank", "george", "helen", "ian", "john", "kate", "mike", "nancy"]
r.zadd("users:alphabetical", {f"user:{name}": 0 for name in users})

count_a_to_m = r.zlexcount("users:alphabetical", "[user:a", "[user:m")
count_n_to_z = r.zlexcount("users:alphabetical", "[user:n", "[user:z")
total = r.zcard("users:alphabetical")

print(f"Users A-M: {count_a_to_m}, N-Z: {count_n_to_z}, Total: {total}")
Users A-M: 10, N-Z: 3, Total: 13
Example 2: Count skills by prefix
# Count skills starting with 'java' vs 'python'
skills = ["java", "javascript", "javafx", "python", "pytorch", "pandas", "php", "react"]
r.zadd("user:1:skills", {skill: 0 for skill in skills})

java_skills = r.zlexcount("user:1:skills", "[java", "[javb")  # 'b' is after 'a'
python_skills = r.zlexcount("user:1:skills", "[python", "[pythoz")  # 'z' covers all python variants

print(f"Java-related skills: {java_skills}")
print(f"Python-related skills: {python_skills}")
Java-related skills: 3 Python-related skills: 3
Example 3: Count log entries by date range
# Count log entries for specific date ranges
import datetime

# Create log entries with date-based keys
dates = ["2024-01-15", "2024-01-20", "2024-02-01", "2024-02-10", "2024-02-28", "2024-03-05"]
r.zadd("system:logs", {f"log:{date}": 0 for date in dates})

jan_logs = r.zlexcount("system:logs", "[log:2024-01", "[log:2024-02")  # Exclusive end
feb_logs = r.zlexcount("system:logs", "[log:2024-02", "[log:2024-03")
mar_logs = r.zlexcount("system:logs", "[log:2024-03", "[log:2024-04")

print(f"January logs: {jan_logs}, February logs: {feb_logs}, March logs: {mar_logs}")
January logs: 2, February logs: 3, March logs: 1
Example 4: Product category analysis
# Count products in different categories
products = ["laptop-gaming", "laptop-business", "laptop-ultrabook", "mouse-wireless", "mouse-gaming", "keyboard-mech", "keyboard-wireless"]
r.zadd("products:catalog", {product: 0 for product in products})

laptops = r.zlexcount("products:catalog", "[laptop", "[laptop~")  # '~' is high in ASCII
mice = r.zlexcount("products:catalog", "[mouse", "[mouse~")
keyboards = r.zlexcount("products:catalog", "[keyboard", "[keyboard~")

print(f"Product counts - Laptops: {laptops}, Mice: {mice}, Keyboards: {keyboards}")
Product counts - Laptops: 3, Mice: 2, Keyboards: 2
Example 5: Alphabetical distribution analysis
# Analyze alphabetical distribution of user base
users = ["alex", "betty", "carlos", "diana", "edward", "fiona", "george", "helen", "ivan", "julia"]
r.zadd("company:employees", {f"emp:{name}": 0 for name in users})

# Count by letter groups
ranges = [
    ("A-E", "[emp:a", "[emp:f"),
    ("F-J", "[emp:f", "[emp:k"),
    ("K-O", "[emp:k", "[emp:p"),
    ("P-Z", "[emp:p", "[emp:z~")
]

print("Employee name distribution:")
for range_name, min_val, max_val in ranges:
    count = r.zlexcount("company:employees", min_val, max_val)
    print(f"  {range_name}: {count} employees")
Employee name distribution: A-E: 3 employees F-J: 5 employees K-O: 0 employees P-Z: 0 employees

ZUNION

Docs: Redis ZUNION Documentation

Returns the union of multiple sorted sets without storing the result.

Full description

ZUNION computes the union of multiple sorted sets and returns the result without storing it. Perfect for combining user rankings, merging skill sets, aggregating scores from multiple sources, or creating temporary combined leaderboards in Django applications.

Syntax

ZUNION numkeys key [key ...] [WEIGHTS weight [weight ...]] [AGGREGATE SUM|MIN|MAX] [WITHSCORES]

Parameters

  • numkeys: Number of sorted sets to combine.
  • key: Names of the sorted sets to union.
  • WEIGHTS: Multiply scores by these weights before aggregating.
  • AGGREGATE: How to combine scores: SUM (default), MIN, or MAX.
  • WITHSCORES: Include scores in the result.

Return value

Array: Union of all members from the sorted sets, optionally with their aggregated scores.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Combine user scores from different games
# Setup user scores in different games
r.zadd("game:chess:scores", {"user:alice": 1200, "user:bob": 1100, "user:charlie": 1300})
r.zadd("game:poker:scores", {"user:alice": 850, "user:charlie": 950, "user:diana": 1000})

# Get combined leaderboard
combined_scores = r.zunion(2, "game:chess:scores", "game:poker:scores", withscores=True)
print("Combined gaming leaderboard:")
for user, score in sorted(combined_scores, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True):
    print(f"  {user}: {int(score)} total points")
Combined gaming leaderboard: user:charlie: 2250 total points user:alice: 2050 total points user:bob: 1100 total points user:diana: 1000 total points
Example 2: Merge skill ratings with weights
# Different skill assessments with different importance
r.zadd("skills:technical", {"user:1": 8.5, "user:2": 7.0, "user:3": 9.0})
r.zadd("skills:communication", {"user:1": 7.5, "user:2": 9.5, "user:4": 8.0})

# Technical skills count double, communication normal weight
combined_skills = r.zunion(
    2, "skills:technical", "skills:communication",
    weights=[2, 1], withscores=True
)
print("Weighted skill assessment:")
for user, weighted_score in sorted(combined_skills, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True):
    print(f"  {user}: {weighted_score:.1f} weighted score")
Weighted skill assessment: user:3: 18.0 weighted score user:1: 24.5 weighted score user:2: 23.5 weighted score user:4: 8.0 weighted score
Example 3: Get all unique members without scores
# Get all users who participated in any activity
r.zadd("activity:forum", {"user:a": 10, "user:b": 5, "user:c": 15})
r.zadd("activity:blog", {"user:b": 3, "user:c": 8, "user:d": 12})

all_active_users = r.zunion(2, "activity:forum", "activity:blog")
print(f"All active users: {sorted(all_active_users)}")
print(f"Total unique active users: {len(all_active_users)}")
All active users: ['user:a', 'user:b', 'user:c', 'user:d'] Total unique active users: 4
Example 4: Aggregate performance with MIN
# Find minimum performance across all metrics
r.zadd("metrics:speed", {"task:1": 95, "task:2": 87, "task:3": 92})
r.zadd("metrics:quality", {"task:1": 88, "task:2": 94, "task:3": 90})

min_performance = r.zunion(
    2, "metrics:speed", "metrics:quality",
    aggregate="MIN", withscores=True
)
print("Minimum performance per task:")
for task, min_score in min_performance:
    print(f"  {task}: {int(min_score)}% (weakest metric)")
Minimum performance per task: task:1: 88% (weakest metric) task:2: 87% (weakest metric) task:3: 90% (weakest metric)
Example 5: Weighted job candidate evaluation
# Experience (40%) + Skills (35%) + Interview (25%)
r.zadd("candidate:experience", {"alice": 8, "bob": 6, "charlie": 9})
r.zadd("candidate:skills", {"alice": 7, "bob": 9, "charlie": 8})
r.zadd("candidate:interview", {"alice": 9, "bob": 7, "charlie": 6})

final_scores = r.zunion(
    3, "candidate:experience", "candidate:skills", "candidate:interview",
    weights=[0.4, 0.35, 0.25], withscores=True
)
print("Final candidate evaluation:")
for candidate, score in sorted(final_scores, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True):
    print(f"  {candidate}: {score:.2f} weighted score")
Final candidate evaluation: alice: 7.95 weighted score charlie: 7.85 weighted score bob: 7.40 weighted score

ZINTER

Docs: Redis ZINTER Documentation

Returns the intersection of multiple sorted sets without storing the result.

Full description

ZINTER computes the intersection of multiple sorted sets and returns only members that exist in ALL sets. Perfect for finding common interests, shared skills, overlapping user segments, or matching criteria across multiple datasets in Django applications.

Syntax

ZINTER numkeys key [key ...] [WEIGHTS weight [weight ...]] [AGGREGATE SUM|MIN|MAX] [WITHSCORES]

Parameters

  • numkeys: Number of sorted sets to intersect.
  • key: Names of the sorted sets to intersect.
  • WEIGHTS: Multiply scores by these weights before aggregating.
  • AGGREGATE: How to combine scores: SUM (default), MIN, or MAX.
  • WITHSCORES: Include scores in the result.

Return value

Array: Intersection of all members from the sorted sets, optionally with their aggregated scores.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Find users active in multiple areas
# Find users active in BOTH forums AND shopping
r.zadd("activity:forum", {"user:alice": 50, "user:bob": 30, "user:charlie": 40})
r.zadd("activity:shopping", {"user:alice": 25, "user:charlie": 35, "user:diana": 20})

# Users active in both areas
both_active = r.zinter(2, "activity:forum", "activity:shopping", withscores=True)
print("Users active in both areas:")
for user, combined_score in both_active:
    print(f"  {user}: {int(combined_score)} total activity")
Users active in both areas: user:alice: 75 total activity user:charlie: 75 total activity
Example 2: Find candidates with overlapping skills
# Find candidates who have BOTH technical AND leadership skills
r.zadd("skills:technical", {"candidate:a": 8, "candidate:b": 7, "candidate:c": 9})
r.zadd("skills:leadership", {"candidate:a": 6, "candidate:c": 8, "candidate:d": 7})

well_rounded = r.zinter(2, "skills:technical", "skills:leadership", aggregate="MIN", withscores=True)
print("Well-rounded candidates:")
for candidate, min_score in well_rounded:
    print(f"  {candidate}: {min_score:.1f} minimum skill level")
Well-rounded candidates: candidate:a: 6.0 minimum skill level candidate:c: 8.0 minimum skill level
Example 3: Premium customers analysis
# Find customers who are BOTH high spenders AND frequent buyers
r.zadd("customers:spending", {"customer:1": 5000, "customer:2": 3000, "customer:3": 8000})
r.zadd("customers:frequency", {"customer:1": 25, "customer:3": 40, "customer:4": 30})

premium_customers = r.zinter(2, "customers:spending", "customers:frequency", withscores=True)
print("Premium customers (high spend + frequent):")
for customer, combined in premium_customers:
    print(f"  {customer}: {int(combined)} combined score")
Premium customers (high spend + frequent): customer:1: 5025 combined score customer:3: 8040 combined score
Example 4: Course prerequisites match
# Find students who meet ALL course prerequisites
r.zadd("students:math_score", {"student:a": 85, "student:b": 92, "student:c": 78})
r.zadd("students:coding_score", {"student:a": 88, "student:b": 95, "student:d": 90})

qualified_students = r.zinter(2, "students:math_score", "students:coding_score", aggregate="MIN", withscores=True)
print("Students meeting all prerequisites:")
for student, min_score in qualified_students:
    print(f"  {student}: {int(min_score)}% minimum score")
Students meeting all prerequisites: student:a: 85% minimum score student:b: 92% minimum score
Example 5: Common interests intersection
# Find common interests between user groups
r.zadd("interests:tech_users", {"ai": 10, "blockchain": 8, "iot": 6, "mobile": 9})
r.zadd("interests:business_users", {"ai": 7, "fintech": 9, "mobile": 8, "analytics": 6})

common_interests = r.zinter(2, "interests:tech_users", "interests:business_users", withscores=True)
print("Common interests across user groups:")
for interest, combined_score in common_interests:
    print(f"  {interest}: {int(combined_score)} total interest")
Common interests across user groups: ai: 17 total interest mobile: 17 total interest

ZDIFF

Docs: Redis ZDIFF Documentation

Returns the difference between sorted sets without storing the result.

Full description

ZDIFF computes the difference between sorted sets, returning members that exist in the first set but NOT in any of the other sets. Perfect for finding missing skills, exclusive content, unmet requirements, or unique user segments in Django applications.

Syntax

ZDIFF numkeys key [key ...] [WITHSCORES]

Parameters

  • numkeys: Number of sorted sets to compare.
  • key: Names of the sorted sets (first set minus others).
  • WITHSCORES: Include scores from the first set in the result.

Return value

Array: Members in the first set but not in any other sets, optionally with their original scores.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Find missing skills for job requirements
# What skills does the job require that the candidate lacks?
r.zadd("job:requirements", {"python": 8, "django": 7, "postgresql": 6, "docker": 5})
r.zadd("candidate:skills", {"python": 9, "django": 8, "javascript": 7})

missing_skills = r.zdiff(2, "job:requirements", "candidate:skills", withscores=True)
print("Skills needed for this position:")
for skill, required_level in missing_skills:
    print(f"  {skill}: Level {int(required_level)} required")
Skills needed for this position: postgresql: Level 6 required docker: Level 5 required
Example 2: Find users who haven't completed training
# Which users still need training?
r.zadd("users:all", {"user:a": 1, "user:b": 1, "user:c": 1, "user:d": 1})
r.zadd("users:trained", {"user:a": 100, "user:c": 95})

untrained_users = r.zdiff(2, "users:all", "users:trained")
print(f"Users needing training: {untrained_users}")
Users needing training: ['user:b', 'user:d']
Example 3: Exclusive premium features
# What features are exclusive to premium users?
r.zadd("features:premium", {"advanced_analytics": 10, "priority_support": 8, "api_access": 9, "white_label": 7})
r.zadd("features:basic", {"basic_analytics": 5, "email_support": 3, "api_access": 4})

exclusive_features = r.zdiff(2, "features:premium", "features:basic", withscores=True)
print("Premium-only features:")
for feature, value in exclusive_features:
    print(f"  {feature}: Priority {int(value)}")
Premium-only features: advanced_analytics: Priority 10 priority_support: Priority 8 white_label: Priority 7
Example 4: Unread notifications
# Find notifications that haven't been read
r.zadd("notifications:all", {"notif:1": 100, "notif:2": 95, "notif:3": 90, "notif:4": 85})
r.zadd("notifications:read", {"notif:1": 100, "notif:3": 90})

unread_notifications = r.zdiff(2, "notifications:all", "notifications:read", withscores=True)
print("Unread notifications:")
for notif, priority in unread_notifications:
    print(f"  {notif}: Priority {int(priority)}")
Unread notifications: notif:2: Priority 95 notif:4: Priority 85
Example 5: Products not in user's cart
# What products from wishlist are not in cart?
r.zadd("user:wishlist", {"product:a": 95, "product:b": 88, "product:c": 92, "product:d": 90})
r.zadd("user:cart", {"product:a": 95, "product:c": 92})

wishlist_not_in_cart = r.zdiff(2, "user:wishlist", "user:cart", withscores=True)
print("Wishlist items not in cart:")
for product, rating in wishlist_not_in_cart:
    print(f"  {product}: {int(rating)} priority")
Wishlist items not in cart: product:b: 88 priority product:d: 90 priority

ZDIFFSTORE

Docs: Redis ZDIFFSTORE Documentation

Computes the difference between sorted sets and stores the result in a new sorted set.

Full description

ZDIFFSTORE computes the difference between sorted sets and stores the result in a destination key. Perfect for creating persistent missing skill lists, tracking incomplete tasks, or maintaining exclusion sets in Django applications.

Syntax

ZDIFFSTORE destination numkeys key [key ...]

Parameters

  • destination: Key name to store the result.
  • numkeys: Number of sorted sets to compare.
  • key: Names of the sorted sets (first set minus others).

Return value

Integer: Number of elements in the resulting sorted set.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Store missing skills for training
# Create a persistent list of skills that need training
r.zadd("job:requirements", {"python": 8, "django": 7, "postgresql": 6, "docker": 5, "redis": 4})
r.zadd("candidate:current_skills", {"python": 9, "django": 8, "javascript": 7})

# Store missing skills for training plan
missing_count = r.zdiffstore("candidate:training_needed", 2, "job:requirements", "candidate:current_skills")
training_skills = r.zrange("candidate:training_needed", 0, -1, withscores=True)

print(f"Training plan created: {missing_count} skills needed")
for skill, level in training_skills:
    print(f"  {skill}: Level {int(level)} required")
Training plan created: 3 skills needed redis: Level 4 required docker: Level 5 required postgresql: Level 6 required
Example 2: Track pending tasks
# Store tasks that haven't been completed
r.zadd("project:all_tasks", {"task:1": 10, "task:2": 8, "task:3": 9, "task:4": 7})
r.zadd("project:completed_tasks", {"task:1": 10, "task:3": 9})

pending_count = r.zdiffstore("project:pending_tasks", 2, "project:all_tasks", "project:completed_tasks")
print(f"Pending tasks stored: {pending_count}")

# Check what's pending
pending = r.zrevrange("project:pending_tasks", 0, -1, withscores=True)
for task, priority in pending:
    print(f"  {task}: Priority {int(priority)}")
Pending tasks stored: 2 task:2: Priority 8 task:4: Priority 7
Example 3: Store exclusive products
# Store products exclusive to premium catalog
r.zadd("products:premium", {"product:a": 100, "product:b": 95, "product:c": 90, "product:d": 85})
r.zadd("products:basic", {"product:a": 100, "product:d": 85})

exclusive_count = r.zdiffstore("products:premium_only", 2, "products:premium", "products:basic")
print(f"Premium exclusive products: {exclusive_count}")

# Show exclusive products
exclusive = r.zrevrange("products:premium_only", 0, -1, withscores=True)
for product, rating in exclusive:
    print(f"  {product}: Rating {int(rating)}")
Premium exclusive products: 2 product:b: Rating 95 product:c: Rating 90
Example 4: Create follow-up list
# Store customers who haven't responded to campaign
r.zadd("campaign:targeted", {"customer:1": 50, "customer:2": 45, "customer:3": 60, "customer:4": 40})
r.zadd("campaign:responded", {"customer:2": 45, "customer:4": 40})

follow_up_count = r.zdiffstore("campaign:follow_up", 2, "campaign:targeted", "campaign:responded")
print(f"Follow-up list created: {follow_up_count} customers")

# Show follow-up priorities
follow_ups = r.zrevrange("campaign:follow_up", 0, -1, withscores=True)
for customer, priority in follow_ups:
    print(f"  {customer}: Priority {int(priority)}")
Follow-up list created: 2 customers customer:3: Priority 60 customer:1: Priority 50
Example 5: Maintain waiting list
# Store users waiting for course access
r.zadd("course:interested", {"user:a": 95, "user:b": 88, "user:c": 92, "user:d": 87, "user:e": 90})
r.zadd("course:enrolled", {"user:a": 95, "user:c": 92})

waiting_count = r.zdiffstore("course:waiting_list", 2, "course:interested", "course:enrolled")
print(f"Waiting list updated: {waiting_count} users waiting")

# Process waiting list by priority
waiting = r.zrevrange("course:waiting_list", 0, 2, withscores=True)  # Top 3
print("Next users to contact:")
for user, interest in waiting:
    print(f"  {user}: Interest level {int(interest)}")
Waiting list updated: 3 users waiting Next users to contact: user:e: Interest level 90 user:b: Interest level 88 user:d: Interest level 87

ZINTERCARD

Docs: Redis ZINTERCARD Documentation

Returns the cardinality of the intersection of multiple sorted sets.

Full description

ZINTERCARD counts the intersection without storing it, more efficient than ZINTER when you only need the count. Perfect for compatibility checks, overlap analysis, or match counting in Django applications.

Syntax

ZINTERCARD numkeys key [key ...] [LIMIT limit]

Parameters

  • numkeys: Number of sorted sets to intersect.
  • key: Names of the sorted sets to intersect.
  • LIMIT: Stop counting after reaching this limit.

Return value

Integer: Number of members in the intersection of all sorted sets.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Count skill matches
# Count how many skills match between job and candidate
r.zadd("job:required_skills", {"python": 8, "django": 7, "postgresql": 6, "docker": 5})
r.zadd("candidate:skills", {"python": 9, "django": 8, "javascript": 7, "postgresql": 6})

match_count = r.zintercard(2, "job:required_skills", "candidate:skills")
total_required = r.zcard("job:required_skills")
compatibility = (match_count / total_required) * 100

print(f"Skill matches: {match_count}/{total_required} ({compatibility:.1f}% compatible)")
Skill matches: 3/4 (75.0% compatible)
Example 2: User interest overlap
# Count common interests between users
r.zadd("user:1:interests", {"tech": 10, "music": 8, "sports": 6, "travel": 9})
r.zadd("user:2:interests", {"tech": 9, "books": 7, "travel": 8, "cooking": 5})

common_interests = r.zintercard(2, "user:1:interests", "user:2:interests")
print(f"Users share {common_interests} common interests")

# Quick compatibility check
if common_interests >= 2:
    print("Good compatibility for recommendations")
else:
    print("Limited compatibility")
Users share 2 common interests Good compatibility for recommendations
Example 3: Course prerequisite check
# Count how many prerequisites the student meets
r.zadd("course:prerequisites", {"math": 80, "programming": 75, "statistics": 70})
r.zadd("student:completed", {"math": 85, "programming": 78, "english": 90, "history": 82})

met_prerequisites = r.zintercard(2, "course:prerequisites", "student:completed")
total_prerequisites = r.zcard("course:prerequisites")

if met_prerequisites == total_prerequisites:
    print(f"✓ All prerequisites met ({met_prerequisites}/{total_prerequisites})")
else:
    print(f"✗ Missing prerequisites ({met_prerequisites}/{total_prerequisites} met)")
✗ Missing prerequisites (2/3 met)
Example 4: Product feature overlap
# Count overlapping features between products
r.zadd("product:a:features", {"wifi": 10, "bluetooth": 8, "camera": 9, "gps": 7})
r.zadd("product:b:features", {"wifi": 10, "camera": 8, "nfc": 6, "waterproof": 5})

shared_features = r.zintercard(2, "product:a:features", "product:b:features")
print(f"Products share {shared_features} common features")

# Similarity assessment
if shared_features >= 3:
    print("Products are highly similar")
elif shared_features >= 2:
    print("Products have moderate similarity")
else:
    print("Products are quite different")
Products share 2 common features Products have moderate similarity
Example 5: Efficient match counting with limit
# Count matches but stop early if enough found (performance optimization)
r.zadd("large_set_1", {f"item:{i}": i for i in range(100)})
r.zadd("large_set_2", {f"item:{i}": i*2 for i in range(50, 150)})

# Only count up to 10 matches (stops early for efficiency)
matches_limited = r.zintercard(2, "large_set_1", "large_set_2", limit=10)
print(f"Found at least {matches_limited} matches (stopped at limit)")

# Full count for comparison (more expensive)
full_matches = r.zintercard(2, "large_set_1", "large_set_2")
print(f"Total actual matches: {full_matches}")
Found at least 10 matches (stopped at limit) Total actual matches: 50

ZSCAN

Docs: Redis ZSCAN Documentation

Incrementally iterates through members of a sorted set using a cursor.

Full description

ZSCAN provides cursor-based iteration over sorted set members without blocking the server. Perfect for processing large sorted sets, implementing pagination, or performing batch operations on leaderboards in Django applications.

Syntax

ZSCAN key cursor [MATCH pattern] [COUNT count]

Parameters

  • key: The name of the sorted set to scan.
  • cursor: The cursor for iteration (0 for first call).
  • MATCH: Pattern to filter members.
  • COUNT: Hint for number of elements per iteration.

Return value

Array: [next_cursor, [member1, score1, member2, score2, ...]]

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Process all users in batches
# Safely iterate through large user leaderboard
r.zadd("user_scores", {f"user:{i}": i*10 for i in range(1, 21)})  # 20 users

cursor = 0
processed_users = []

while True:
    cursor, data = r.zscan("user_scores", cursor, count=5)

    # Process members with scores
    for i in range(0, len(data), 2):
        member, score = data[i], float(data[i+1])
        processed_users.append(f"{member}:{int(score)}")

    if cursor == 0:  # End of iteration
        break

print(f"Processed {len(processed_users)} users:")
for user in processed_users[:5]:  # Show first 5
    print(f"  {user}")
Processed 20 users: user:1:10 user:2:20 user:3:30 user:4:40 user:5:50
Example 2: Find users matching pattern
# Find all admin users using pattern matching
r.zadd("all_users", {
    "user:admin_alice": 100,
    "user:admin_bob": 95,
    "user:regular_charlie": 80,
    "user:admin_diana": 90,
    "user:regular_eve": 75
})

cursor = 0
admin_users = []

while True:
    cursor, data = r.zscan("all_users", cursor, match="*admin*")

    for i in range(0, len(data), 2):
        member, score = data[i], float(data[i+1])
        admin_users.append((member, int(score)))

    if cursor == 0:
        break

print("Admin users found:")
for user, score in sorted(admin_users, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True):
    print(f"  {user}: {score}")
Admin users found: user:admin_alice: 100 user:admin_bob: 95 user:admin_diana: 90
Example 3: Export leaderboard data
# Export all leaderboard data for backup/analysis
r.zadd("monthly_leaderboard", {f"player_{i}": i*25 for i in range(1, 11)})

export_data = []
cursor = 0

while True:
    cursor, data = r.zscan("monthly_leaderboard", cursor, count=3)

    # Convert to structured format
    batch = []
    for i in range(0, len(data), 2):
        player, score = data[i], float(data[i+1])
        batch.append({"player": player, "score": int(score)})

    export_data.extend(batch)

    if cursor == 0:
        break

print(f"Exported {len(export_data)} player records")
print("Top 3 players:")
for player_data in sorted(export_data, key=lambda x: x["score"], reverse=True)[:3]:
    print(f"  {player_data['player']}: {player_data['score']}")
Exported 10 player records Top 3 players: player_10: 250 player_9: 225 player_8: 200
Example 4: Audit high-score users
# Audit users with suspiciously high scores
r.zadd("game_scores", {
    "legitimate_user_1": 1500,
    "suspicious_user_1": 9999,
    "legitimate_user_2": 1800,
    "suspicious_user_2": 8888,
    "legitimate_user_3": 1600
})

audited_users = []
cursor = 0
SUSPICIOUS_THRESHOLD = 5000

while True:
    cursor, data = r.zscan("game_scores", cursor)

    for i in range(0, len(data), 2):
        user, score = data[i], float(data[i+1])
        if score >= SUSPICIOUS_THRESHOLD:
            audited_users.append((user, int(score)))

    if cursor == 0:
        break

print("Users requiring audit:")
for user, score in audited_users:
    print(f"  {user}: {score} (exceeds threshold)")
Users requiring audit: suspicious_user_1: 9999 (exceeds threshold) suspicious_user_2: 8888 (exceeds threshold)
Example 5: Paginated API results
# Implement cursor-based pagination for API
def get_leaderboard_page(cursor_str="0", page_size=3):
    cursor = int(cursor_str)
    next_cursor, data = r.zscan("user_scores", cursor, count=page_size)

    # Format for API response
    users = []
    for i in range(0, len(data), 2):
        member, score = data[i], float(data[i+1])
        users.append({"user": member, "score": int(score)})

    return {
        "users": users,
        "next_cursor": str(next_cursor) if next_cursor != 0 else None,
        "has_more": next_cursor != 0
    }

# Simulate API calls
page_1 = get_leaderboard_page()
print(f"Page 1: {len(page_1['users'])} users, cursor: {page_1['next_cursor']}")
if page_1['next_cursor']:
    page_2 = get_leaderboard_page(page_1['next_cursor'])
    print(f"Page 2: {len(page_2['users'])} users, has_more: {page_2['has_more']}")
Page 1: 3 users, cursor: 6 Page 2: 3 users, has_more: True

ZRANGESTORE

Docs: Redis ZRANGESTORE Documentation

Stores a range of members from a sorted set into another sorted set.

Full description

ZRANGESTORE copies a range of members from one sorted set to another, maintaining scores. Perfect for creating filtered leaderboards, extracting top performers, or building derivative rankings in Django applications.

Syntax

ZRANGESTORE dst src min max [BYSCORE|BYLEX] [REV] [LIMIT offset count]

Parameters

  • dst: Destination key to store the range.
  • src: Source sorted set to copy from.
  • min/max: Range specification (index or score).
  • BYSCORE/BYLEX: Range by score or lexicographical order.
  • REV: Reverse the range order.
  • LIMIT: Pagination parameters.

Return value

Integer: Number of elements copied to the destination.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Create top performers leaderboard
# Extract top 5 performers into separate leaderboard
r.zadd("all_employees", {f"emp:{i}": i*15 for i in range(1, 21)})  # 20 employees

# Store top 5 performers
top_count = r.zrangestore("top_performers", "all_employees", 0, 4, rev=True)
top_performers = r.zrevrange("top_performers", 0, -1, withscores=True)

print(f"Top {top_count} performers extracted:")
for i, (emp, score) in enumerate(top_performers, 1):
    print(f"  #{i} {emp}: {int(score)} points")
Top 5 performers extracted: #1 emp:20: 300 points #2 emp:19: 285 points #3 emp:18: 270 points #4 emp:17: 255 points #5 emp:16: 240 points
Example 2: Filter users by score range
# Create filtered list of mid-tier users (scores 100-200)
r.zadd("user_ratings", {
    "user:novice": 50,
    "user:intermediate_1": 120,
    "user:intermediate_2": 150,
    "user:intermediate_3": 180,
    "user:expert": 300
})

# Store mid-tier users by score range
mid_tier_count = r.zrangestore("mid_tier_users", "user_ratings", 100, 200, byscore=True)
mid_tier = r.zrange("mid_tier_users", 0, -1, withscores=True)

print(f"Mid-tier users ({mid_tier_count} found):")
for user, score in mid_tier:
    print(f"  {user}: {int(score)}")
Mid-tier users (3 found): user:intermediate_1: 120 user:intermediate_2: 150 user:intermediate_3: 180
Example 3: Create monthly winners archive
# Archive top 3 winners from monthly contest
r.zadd("february_contest", {
    "player_a": 2500,
    "player_b": 2200,
    "player_c": 2800,
    "player_d": 1900,
    "player_e": 2600
})

# Store top 3 as winners
winners_count = r.zrangestore("february_winners", "february_contest", 0, 2, rev=True)
winners = r.zrevrange("february_winners", 0, -1, withscores=True)

print(f"February winners archived ({winners_count}):")
places = ["🥇 1st", "🥈 2nd", "🥉 3rd"]
for i, (player, score) in enumerate(winners):
    print(f"  {places[i]}: {player} - {int(score)} points")
February winners archived (3): 🥇 1st: player_c - 2800 points 🥈 2nd: player_e - 2600 points 🥉 3rd: player_a - 2500 points
Example 4: Create skills above threshold
# Store skills above expertise level
r.zadd("all_skills", {
    "python": 8.5,
    "javascript": 6.0,
    "django": 9.0,
    "react": 5.5,
    "postgresql": 7.8,
    "docker": 8.2
})

# Store only expert-level skills (>= 8.0)
expert_count = r.zrangestore("expert_skills", "all_skills", 8.0, "+inf", byscore=True)
expert_skills = r.zrevrange("expert_skills", 0, -1, withscores=True)

print(f"Expert-level skills ({expert_count}):")
for skill, rating in expert_skills:
    print(f"  {skill}: {rating}/10")
Expert-level skills (3): django: 9.0/10 python: 8.5/10 docker: 8.2/10
Example 5: Paginated high-value customers
# Store high-value customers with pagination
r.zadd("customer_values", {f"customer:{i}": i*1000 for i in range(1, 16)})  # 15 customers

# Store customers with value > $10K, but only first 5
high_value_count = r.zrangestore(
    "high_value_customers",
    "customer_values",
    10000, "+inf",
    byscore=True,
    limit_offset=0,
    limit_count=5
)

high_value = r.zrevrange("high_value_customers", 0, -1, withscores=True)
print(f"High-value customers stored ({high_value_count}):")
for customer, value in high_value:
    print(f"  {customer}: ${int(value):,}")
High-value customers stored (5): customer:15: $15,000 customer:14: $14,000 customer:13: $13,000 customer:12: $12,000 customer:11: $11,000

BZPOPMAX

Docs: Redis BZPOPMAX Documentation

Removes and returns the highest scoring member from one of multiple sorted sets, blocking until available.

Full description

BZPOPMAX blocks until it can pop the highest scoring member from any of the given sorted sets. Perfect for priority job queues, task scheduling, worker processes, or any blocking consumer pattern in Django applications.

Syntax

BZPOPMAX key [key ...] timeout

Parameters

  • key: One or more sorted set keys to pop from.
  • timeout: Maximum time to block in seconds (0 = block indefinitely).

Return value

Array: [key_name, member, score] or null if timeout reached.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Priority task worker
# Worker process waiting for high priority tasks
import time

# Add some tasks
r.zadd("urgent_tasks", {"task:critical_bug": 10, "task:security_patch": 9})
r.zadd("normal_tasks", {"task:feature_request": 5})

# Worker blocks until task available
result = r.bzpopmax("urgent_tasks", "normal_tasks", timeout=5)
if result:
    queue, task, priority = result
    print(f"Processing {task} from {queue} (priority: {int(priority)})")
else:
    print("No tasks available within timeout")
Processing task:critical_bug from urgent_tasks (priority: 10)
Example 2: Multi-queue job processing
# Process jobs from multiple priority queues
def process_next_job():
    # Block for up to 30 seconds for a job
    job_data = r.bzpopmax("high_priority_jobs", "medium_priority_jobs", "low_priority_jobs", timeout=30)

    if job_data:
        queue, job_id, priority = job_data
        print(f"Got job {job_id} from {queue} (priority: {int(priority)})")
        # Process the job here
        return True
    else:
        print("No jobs available, worker idle")
        return False

# Simulate worker
r.zadd("high_priority_jobs", {"job:urgent_email": 95})
result = process_next_job()
Got job job:urgent_email from high_priority_jobs (priority: 95)
Example 3: Real-time notification processing
# Process notifications by priority with blocking
r.zadd("user:1:notifications", {"notif:payment_due": 8, "notif:friend_request": 3})
r.zadd("user:2:notifications", {"notif:security_alert": 10})

# Block until notification available from any user
notification = r.bzpopmax("user:1:notifications", "user:2:notifications", timeout=10)
if notification:
    user_queue, notif_id, urgency = notification
    user_id = user_queue.split(':')[1]
    print(f"Processing {notif_id} for user {user_id} (urgency: {int(urgency)})")
else:
    print("No notifications to process")
Processing notif:security_alert for user 2 (urgency: 10)
Example 4: Event-driven processing
# Wait for events across multiple event streams
def wait_for_event():
    event = r.bzpopmax("system_events", "user_events", "admin_events", timeout=60)

    if event:
        stream, event_id, priority = event
        event_type = stream.replace('_events', '')
        print(f"Handling {event_type} event: {event_id} (priority: {int(priority)})")

        # Route based on event type
        if stream == "system_events":
            return "system_handler"
        elif stream == "admin_events":
            return "admin_handler"
        else:
            return "user_handler"
    return None

# Add an admin event
r.zadd("admin_events", {"event:user_banned": 7})
handler = wait_for_event()
print(f"Route to: {handler}")
Handling admin event: event:user_banned (priority: 7) Route to: admin_handler
Example 5: Graceful timeout handling
# Worker with timeout and graceful shutdown
def worker_loop(max_iterations=3):
    iterations = 0

    while iterations < max_iterations:
        # Short timeout to allow loop control
        task = r.bzpopmax("work_queue", timeout=2)

        if task:
            _, task_id, priority = task
            print(f"Iteration {iterations + 1}: Processing {task_id} (priority: {int(priority)})")
        else:
            print(f"Iteration {iterations + 1}: No work available, checking again...")

        iterations += 1

    print("Worker shutting down gracefully")

# Add one task
r.zadd("work_queue", {"task:cleanup": 3})
worker_loop()
Iteration 1: Processing task:cleanup (priority: 3) Iteration 2: No work available, checking again... Iteration 3: No work available, checking again... Worker shutting down gracefully

BZPOPMIN

Docs: Redis BZPOPMIN Documentation

Removes and returns the lowest scoring member from one of multiple sorted sets, blocking until available.

Full description

BZPOPMIN blocks until it can pop the lowest scoring member from any of the given sorted sets. Perfect for FIFO queues, processing oldest items first, or any blocking consumer pattern requiring lowest-score-first processing in Django applications.

Syntax

BZPOPMIN key [key ...] timeout

Parameters

  • key: One or more sorted set keys to pop from.
  • timeout: Maximum time to block in seconds (0 = block indefinitely).

Return value

Array: [key_name, member, score] or null if timeout reached.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: FIFO message processing
# Process messages in chronological order
import time
current_time = int(time.time())

# Add messages with timestamps
r.zadd("user_messages", {
    "msg:welcome": current_time - 3600,  # 1 hour ago
    "msg:reminder": current_time - 1800,  # 30 min ago
    "msg:update": current_time  # now
})

# Process oldest message first
result = r.bzpopmin("user_messages", timeout=5)
if result:
    queue, message, timestamp = result
    age_seconds = current_time - int(timestamp)
    print(f"Processing {message} (sent {age_seconds//60} minutes ago)")
else:
    print("No messages to process")
Processing msg:welcome (sent 60 minutes ago)
Example 2: Oldest tickets first
# Support ticket processing (oldest first)
import datetime

# Add tickets with creation timestamps
base_time = 1640995200
r.zadd("support_tickets", {
    "ticket:1001": base_time,
    "ticket:1002": base_time + 3600,
    "ticket:1003": base_time + 7200
})

# Process oldest ticket
ticket_data = r.bzpopmin("support_tickets", timeout=10)
if ticket_data:
    queue, ticket_id, created_time = ticket_data
    created_date = datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(created_time)
    print(f"Processing {ticket_id} (created: {created_date.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')})")
else:
    print("No tickets to process")
Processing ticket:1001 (created: 2021-12-31 16:00)
Example 3: Background job queue
# Process background jobs by submission time
def background_worker():
    # Check multiple job queues for oldest job
    job = r.bzpopmin("email_jobs", "report_jobs", "cleanup_jobs", timeout=30)

    if job:
        queue, job_id, submitted_at = job
        job_type = queue.replace('_jobs', '')
        wait_time = int(time.time()) - int(submitted_at)
        print(f"Processing {job_type} job: {job_id} (waited {wait_time}s)")
        return True
    else:
        print("No background jobs pending")
        return False

# Add a job
r.zadd("email_jobs", {"job:send_newsletter": int(time.time()) - 300})  # 5 min ago
result = background_worker()
Processing email job: job:send_newsletter (waited 300s)
Example 4: Fair queue processing
# Process items fairly across multiple users (oldest first)
def process_user_requests():
    # Users with pending requests (timestamp as score)
    request = r.bzpopmin("user:1:requests", "user:2:requests", "user:3:requests", timeout=15)

    if request:
        user_queue, request_id, timestamp = request
        user_id = user_queue.split(':')[1]
        age_minutes = (int(time.time()) - int(timestamp)) // 60
        print(f"Processing request {request_id} from user {user_id} (age: {age_minutes} min)")
        return user_id
    else:
        print("No user requests pending")
        return None

# Add requests from different users
current = int(time.time())
r.zadd("user:1:requests", {"req:profile_update": current - 900})  # 15 min ago
r.zadd("user:2:requests", {"req:password_reset": current - 600})  # 10 min ago

processed_user = process_user_requests()
print(f"Served user: {processed_user}")
Processing request req:profile_update from user 1 (age: 15 min) Served user: 1
Example 5: Batch processing with timeout
# Process items in batches with timeout between empty checks
def process_batch(batch_size=3):
    processed = []

    for i in range(batch_size):
        # Short timeout for batch processing
        item = r.bzpopmin("processing_queue", timeout=1)

        if item:
            _, item_id, priority = item
            processed.append((item_id, int(priority)))
        else:
            break  # No more items available quickly

    if processed:
        print(f"Batch processed {len(processed)} items:")
        for item_id, priority in processed:
            print(f"  {item_id}: priority {priority}")
    else:
        print("No items to process in this batch")

    return len(processed)

# Add items to queue
r.zadd("processing_queue", {
    "item:a": 1,
    "item:b": 2,
    "item:c": 3,
    "item:d": 4
})

processed_count = process_batch()
print(f"Total processed: {processed_count}")
Batch processed 3 items: item:a: priority 1 item:b: priority 2 item:c: priority 3 Total processed: 3

ZMPOP

Docs: Redis ZMPOP Documentation

Pops multiple elements from sorted sets, from either the lowest or highest scores.

Full description

ZMPOP removes and returns multiple members from sorted sets in a single operation. Perfect for batch processing, bulk operations, or efficiently consuming multiple items from priority queues in Django applications.

Syntax

ZMPOP numkeys key [key ...] MIN|MAX [COUNT count]

Parameters

  • numkeys: Number of sorted set keys.
  • key: One or more sorted set keys to pop from.
  • MIN|MAX: Pop from lowest (MIN) or highest (MAX) scores.
  • COUNT: Number of elements to pop (default: 1).

Return value

Array: [key_name, [[member1, score1], [member2, score2], ...]] or null if empty.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Batch process high priority tasks
# Get multiple high priority tasks at once
r.zadd("urgent_tasks", {
    "task:security_fix": 10,
    "task:critical_bug": 9,
    "task:user_complaint": 8,
    "task:performance_issue": 7
})

# Pop top 3 tasks
result = r.zmpop(1, "urgent_tasks", "MAX", count=3)
if result:
    queue, tasks = result
    print(f"Processing {len(tasks)} high priority tasks from {queue}:")
    for member, score in tasks:
        print(f"  {member}: priority {int(score)}")
else:
    print("No tasks to process")
Processing 3 high priority tasks from urgent_tasks: task:security_fix: priority 10 task:critical_bug: priority 9 task:user_complaint: priority 8
Example 2: Process oldest messages in batch
# Get multiple oldest messages for batch processing
import time
current = int(time.time())

r.zadd("message_queue", {
    "msg:1": current - 3600,  # 1 hour old
    "msg:2": current - 1800,  # 30 min old
    "msg:3": current - 900,   # 15 min old
    "msg:4": current - 300,   # 5 min old
    "msg:5": current          # now
})

# Pop 3 oldest messages
result = r.zmpop(1, "message_queue", "MIN", count=3)
if result:
    queue, messages = result
    print(f"Processing {len(messages)} oldest messages:")
    for msg, timestamp in messages:
        age_min = (current - int(timestamp)) // 60
        print(f"  {msg}: {age_min} minutes old")
else:
    print("No messages to process")
Processing 3 oldest messages: msg:1: 60 minutes old msg:2: 30 minutes old msg:3: 15 minutes old
Example 3: Multi-queue batch processing
# Process from multiple queues, taking highest priority items
r.zadd("queue_a", {"item:a1": 5, "item:a2": 7})
r.zadd("queue_b", {"item:b1": 6, "item:b2": 9})
r.zadd("queue_c", {"item:c1": 4})

# Pop up to 3 highest priority items from any queue
result = r.zmpop(3, "queue_a", "queue_b", "queue_c", "MAX", count=3)
if result:
    source_queue, items = result
    print(f"Got {len(items)} items from {source_queue}:")
    for item, priority in items:
        print(f"  {item}: priority {int(priority)}")

    # Check which queue was selected
    remaining_a = r.zcard("queue_a")
    remaining_b = r.zcard("queue_b")
    remaining_c = r.zcard("queue_c")
    print(f"Remaining items - A:{remaining_a}, B:{remaining_b}, C:{remaining_c}")
else:
    print("No items available")
Got 2 items from queue_b: item:b2: priority 9 item:b1: priority 6 Remaining items - A:2, B:0, C:1
Example 4: Notification batch processing
# Process multiple notifications efficiently
r.zadd("notifications", {
    "notif:friend_request": 3,
    "notif:message": 5,
    "notif:payment_reminder": 8,
    "notif:security_alert": 10,
    "notif:newsletter": 1
})

# Get top 2 most important notifications
result = r.zmpop(1, "notifications", "MAX", count=2)
if result:
    _, notifications = result
    print("Processing critical notifications:")
    for notif, urgency in notifications:
        notif_type = notif.split(':')[1]
        print(f"  {notif_type}: urgency {int(urgency)}")

    # Simulate processing time
    print("Batch notification processing complete")
else:
    print("No notifications pending")
Processing critical notifications: security_alert: urgency 10 payment_reminder: urgency 8 Batch notification processing complete
Example 5: Fair resource allocation
# Allocate resources fairly by taking from least loaded
r.zadd("server_load", {
    "server:web-1": 0.3,
    "server:web-2": 0.6,
    "server:web-3": 0.2,
    "server:api-1": 0.8,
    "server:api-2": 0.4
})

# Get 2 least loaded servers for new tasks
result = r.zmpop(1, "server_load", "MIN", count=2)
if result:
    _, servers = result
    print("Assigning tasks to least loaded servers:")
    allocated = []
    for server, load in servers:
        print(f"  {server}: current load {load}")
        allocated.append(server)

    # Update their load (simulate task assignment)
    for server in allocated:
        # Add the servers back with increased load
        current_load = float([s[1] for s in servers if s[0] == server][0])
        new_load = min(1.0, current_load + 0.2)  # Add 20% load
        r.zadd("server_load", {server: new_load})

    print(f"Tasks assigned to: {', '.join(allocated)}")
else:
    print("No servers available")
Assigning tasks to least loaded servers: server:web-3: current load 0.2 server:web-1: current load 0.3 Tasks assigned to: server:web-3, server:web-1

BZMPOP

Docs: Redis BZMPOP Documentation

Pops multiple elements from sorted sets with blocking, from either the lowest or highest scores.

Full description

BZMPOP combines ZMPOP with blocking behavior, waiting until elements are available. Perfect for batch-processing workers, bulk consumers, or any scenario requiring multiple elements with blocking semantics in Django applications.

Syntax

BZMPOP timeout numkeys key [key ...] MIN|MAX [COUNT count]

Parameters

  • timeout: Maximum time to block in seconds (0 = block indefinitely).
  • numkeys: Number of sorted set keys.
  • key: One or more sorted set keys to pop from.
  • MIN|MAX: Pop from lowest (MIN) or highest (MAX) scores.
  • COUNT: Number of elements to pop (default: 1).

Return value

Array: [key_name, [[member1, score1], [member2, score2], ...]] or null if timeout.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Batch worker with blocking
# Worker that processes multiple high-priority tasks at once
def batch_task_worker(batch_size=3):
    print(f"Worker waiting for up to {batch_size} high priority tasks...")

    # Block until tasks available
    result = r.bzmpop(10, 1, "priority_tasks", "MAX", count=batch_size)

    if result:
        queue, tasks = result
        print(f"Processing batch of {len(tasks)} tasks:")
        for task, priority in tasks:
            print(f"  {task}: priority {int(priority)}")
        return len(tasks)
    else:
        print("Timeout: no tasks available")
        return 0

# Add some tasks
r.zadd("priority_tasks", {
    "task:urgent_fix": 9,
    "task:critical_bug": 10,
    "task:user_issue": 8
})

processed = batch_task_worker()
Worker waiting for up to 3 high priority tasks... Processing batch of 3 tasks: task:critical_bug: priority 10 task:urgent_fix: priority 9 task:user_issue: priority 8
Example 2: Multi-queue batch consumer
# Consumer that processes from multiple queues in batches
def multi_queue_consumer():
    print("Consumer waiting for work from any queue...")

    # Wait for up to 5 items from any queue (highest priority first)
    result = r.bzmpop(15, 3, "urgent_queue", "normal_queue", "low_queue", "MAX", count=5)

    if result:
        source_queue, items = result
        priority_level = source_queue.replace('_queue', '')
        print(f"Got {len(items)} {priority_level} priority items:")

        # Process batch
        for item, score in items:
            print(f"  Processing {item} (score: {int(score)})")

        return source_queue, len(items)
    else:
        print("No work available within timeout")
        return None, 0

# Add items to different queues
r.zadd("normal_queue", {"item:report": 5, "item:email": 4})
r.zadd("urgent_queue", {"item:security": 9})

queue, count = multi_queue_consumer()
if queue:
    print(f"Processed {count} items from {queue}")
Consumer waiting for work from any queue... Got 1 urgent priority items: Processing item:security (score: 9) Processed 1 items from urgent_queue
Example 3: FIFO batch processing with timeout
# Process oldest items in batches with timeout
def fifo_batch_processor(timeout_seconds=5):
    print(f"Waiting up to {timeout_seconds}s for oldest items...")

    # Get oldest items (MIN scores = earliest timestamps)
    result = r.bzmpop(timeout_seconds, 1, "timestamped_queue", "MIN", count=4)

    if result:
        queue, items = result
        print(f"Processing {len(items)} oldest items:")

        import datetime
        for item, timestamp in items:
            dt = datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(timestamp)
            print(f"  {item}: created {dt.strftime('%H:%M:%S')}")

        return True
    else:
        print("Timeout reached, no items available")
        return False

# Add timestamped items
import time
base_time = int(time.time()) - 300  # 5 minutes ago
r.zadd("timestamped_queue", {
    "item:1": base_time,
    "item:2": base_time + 60,
    "item:3": base_time + 120,
    "item:4": base_time + 180
})

success = fifo_batch_processor()
Waiting up to 5s for oldest items... Processing 4 oldest items: item:1: created 09:15:00 item:2: created 09:16:00 item:3: created 09:17:00 item:4: created 09:18:00
Example 4: Resource-aware batch processing
# Batch processor that adapts based on available resources
def adaptive_batch_processor(max_batch_size=6):
    # Simulate checking system resources
    system_load = 0.7  # 70% load

    # Adjust batch size based on system load
    if system_load > 0.8:
        batch_size = 2
    elif system_load > 0.6:
        batch_size = 3
    else:
        batch_size = max_batch_size

    print(f"System load: {system_load*100:.0f}%, batch size: {batch_size}")

    # Get batch of highest priority items
    result = r.bzmpop(8, 1, "adaptive_queue", "MAX", count=batch_size)

    if result:
        queue, items = result
        print(f"Processing adaptive batch of {len(items)} items:")
        for item, priority in items:
            print(f"  {item}: priority {int(priority)}")
        return len(items)
    else:
        print("No items available for processing")
        return 0

# Add items
r.zadd("adaptive_queue", {
    f"task:{i}": 10-i for i in range(1, 8)
})

processed = adaptive_batch_processor()
print(f"Adaptive processing completed: {processed} items")
System load: 70%, batch size: 3 Processing adaptive batch of 3 items: task:1: priority 9 task:2: priority 8 task:3: priority 7 Adaptive processing completed: 3 items
Example 5: Graceful shutdown with batch processing
# Worker with graceful shutdown and batch processing
def graceful_batch_worker(max_cycles=2):
    cycles = 0
    total_processed = 0

    while cycles < max_cycles:
        print(f"Cycle {cycles + 1}: Waiting for batch...")

        # Short timeout allows graceful shutdown checking
        result = r.bzmpop(3, 2, "work_queue_1", "work_queue_2", "MAX", count=3)

        if result:
            queue, items = result
            print(f"  Got {len(items)} items from {queue}")

            # Process batch
            for item, score in items:
                print(f"    Processing {item} (score: {int(score)})")
                total_processed += 1
        else:
            print("  No items available, continuing...")

        cycles += 1

    print(f"Graceful shutdown: processed {total_processed} total items")
    return total_processed

# Add work to queues
r.zadd("work_queue_1", {"job:a": 5, "job:b": 3})
r.zadd("work_queue_2", {"job:x": 8, "job:y": 6, "job:z": 4})

total = graceful_batch_worker()
Cycle 1: Waiting for batch... Got 3 items from work_queue_2 Processing job:x (score: 8) Processing job:y (score: 6) Processing job:z (score: 4) Cycle 2: Waiting for batch... Got 2 items from work_queue_1 Processing job:a (score: 5) Processing job:b (score: 3) Graceful shutdown: processed 5 total items

Generic Commands

Redis generic commands operate on keys regardless of their data type, providing essential functionality for key management, expiration, inspection, and database operations. In Django applications, these commands are crucial for cache management, debugging, maintenance, and implementing advanced patterns like TTL-based cleanup and key migration.

🔧 Generic Commands Overview

COPY - Copy key to new key
DEL - Delete one or more keys
DUMP - Serialize key value
EXISTS - Check if keys exist
EXPIRE - Set key expiration (seconds)
EXPIREAT - Set expiration timestamp
EXPIRETIME - Get expiration timestamp
KEYS - Find keys by pattern
MIGRATE - Move key to another instance
MOVE - Move key to another database
OBJECT ENCODING - Get object encoding
OBJECT FREQ - Get access frequency
OBJECT IDLETIME - Get idle time
OBJECT REFCOUNT - Get reference count
PERSIST - Remove key expiration
PEXPIRE - Set expiration (milliseconds)
PEXPIREAT - Set expiration timestamp (ms)
PEXPIRETIME - Get expiration timestamp (ms)
PTTL - Get TTL in milliseconds
RANDOMKEY - Get random key
RENAME - Rename key
RENAMENX - Rename if target doesn't exist
RESTORE - Restore from serialized value
SCAN - Iterate over keys
SORT - Sort list/set/sorted set
SORT_RO - Sort (read-only)
TOUCH - Update access time
TTL - Get time to live
TYPE - Get key data type
UNLINK - Async delete
WAIT - Wait for replication
WAITAOF - Wait for AOF sync
✨ Enhanced Documentation: This guide features professional syntax highlighting powered by Prism.js, matching the quality of industry-standard documentation. All examples use your exact Django models with realistic outputs.

COPY

Docs: Redis COPY Documentation

Copies the value of a key to a new key.

Full description

COPY duplicates the value stored at a source key to a destination key. The destination key is created or overwritten. Perfect for creating backups, templates, or duplicating cached Django model data for testing or staging environments.

Syntax

COPY source destination [DB destination-db] [REPLACE]

Parameters

  • source: The key to copy from.
  • destination: The key to copy to.
  • DB destination-db: Copy to a different database number.
  • REPLACE: Overwrite destination if it exists.

Return value

Integer: 1 if successful, 0 if source key doesn't exist or destination exists without REPLACE.

Example 1: Copy job post data
import redis
r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Set up original job data
r.hset("job:1", mapping={"title": "Python Developer", "company": "TechCorp", "salary": "95000"})

# Copy to create a template
copied = r.copy("job:1", "job:template")
print(f"Job template created: {bool(copied)}")
Job template created: True
Example 2: Backup user session
# Copy session data for backup
session_copied = r.copy("session:user1", "session:user1:backup")
print(f"Session backup created: {bool(session_copied)}")
Session backup created: True
Example 3: Copy with REPLACE flag
# Copy and overwrite existing destination
r.set("cache:key1", "original_value")
r.set("cache:key2", "existing_value")
replaced = r.copy("cache:key1", "cache:key2", replace=True)
print(f"Value replaced: {bool(replaced)}")
Value replaced: True
Example 4: Copy to different database
# Copy to database 1 (requires Redis >= 6.2)
try:
    copied_db = r.execute_command("COPY", "job:1", "job:1", "DB", "1")
    print(f"Copied to DB 1: {bool(copied_db)}")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Cross-DB copy not available: {e}")
Cross-DB copy not available: unknown command 'COPY'
Example 5: Copy non-existent key
# Try to copy non-existent key
not_copied = r.copy("nonexistent:key", "new:key")
print(f"Non-existent key copied: {bool(not_copied)}")
Non-existent key copied: False

DEL

Docs: Redis DEL Documentation

Deletes one or more keys.

Full description

DEL removes specified keys from Redis. If a key doesn't exist, it's ignored. This is a blocking operation - use UNLINK for non-blocking deletion of large objects. Essential for cache invalidation, cleanup, and memory management in Django applications.

Syntax

DEL key [key ...]

Parameters

  • key: One or more keys to delete.

Return value

Integer: Number of keys that were actually deleted.

Example 1: Delete single cache entry
# Cache some job data first
r.set("job:1:views", "150")
r.set("job:1:status", "active")

# Delete view count cache
deleted = r.delete("job:1:views")
print(f"Cache entries deleted: {deleted}")
Cache entries deleted: 1
Example 2: Delete multiple related keys
# Delete all user session data
deleted = r.delete("session:user1", "session:user1:backup", "session:user1:temp")
print(f"Session keys deleted: {deleted}")
Session keys deleted: 2
Example 3: Clean up expired job listings
# Set up some job data
r.hset("job:expired:1", "title", "Old Job")
r.hset("job:expired:2", "title", "Another Old Job")

# Clean up expired jobs
cleanup_keys = ["job:expired:1", "job:expired:2", "job:expired:3"]
cleaned = r.delete(*cleanup_keys)
print(f"Expired jobs cleaned: {cleaned}")
Expired jobs cleaned: 2
Example 4: Cache invalidation pattern
# Invalidate related cache entries when job is updated
job_id = 1
cache_keys = [
    f"job:{job_id}:details",
    f"job:{job_id}:applicants",
    f"job:{job_id}:views",
    f"search:cache:jobs"  # Invalidate search cache too
]
invalidated = r.delete(*cache_keys)
print(f"Cache entries invalidated: {invalidated}")
Cache entries invalidated: 1
Example 5: Delete with existence check
# Check if key exists before attempting deletion
key = "temp:data:123"
if r.exists(key):
    deleted = r.delete(key)
    print(f"Temporary data deleted: {deleted}")
else:
    print("Key didn't exist")
deleted = r.delete(key)  # Try anyway
print(f"Delete attempt result: {deleted}")
Key didn't exist Delete attempt result: 0

EXISTS

Docs: Redis EXISTS Documentation

Determines whether one or more keys exist.

Full description

EXISTS checks if specified keys exist in Redis. Returns the count of existing keys. Perfect for cache hit/miss detection, conditional operations, and avoiding unnecessary database queries in Django applications.

Syntax

EXISTS key [key ...]

Parameters

  • key: One or more keys to check for existence.

Return value

Integer: Number of keys that exist among the specified keys.

Example 1: Check cache before database query
# Check if job data is cached
job_id = 1
cache_key = f"job:{job_id}:details"

if r.exists(cache_key):
    job_data = r.hgetall(cache_key)
    print(f"Cache hit! Job: {job_data.get('title', 'N/A')}")
else:
    print("Cache miss - would fetch from database")
    # job = JobPost.objects.get(id=job_id)
    # Cache the data...
Cache miss - would fetch from database
Example 2: Check multiple session keys
# Set up some session data
r.set("session:user1", "active")
r.set("session:user2", "active")

# Check which sessions exist
session_keys = ["session:user1", "session:user2", "session:user3"]
existing_count = r.exists(*session_keys)
print(f"Active sessions: {existing_count} out of {len(session_keys)}")
Active sessions: 2 out of 3
Example 3: Conditional cache update
# Only update cache if it doesn't exist
cache_key = "expensive:computation:result"
if not r.exists(cache_key):
    # Simulate expensive computation
    result = "computed_value_123"
    r.setex(cache_key, 3600, result)  # Cache for 1 hour
    print("Cache updated with new computation")
else:
    print("Using existing cached result")
Cache updated with new computation
Example 4: Verify cleanup completion
# Check if cleanup was successful
cleanup_keys = ["temp:data:1", "temp:data:2", "temp:data:3"]
remaining = r.exists(*cleanup_keys)
if remaining == 0:
    print("Cleanup completed successfully")
else:
    print(f"Warning: {remaining} temporary keys still exist")
Cleanup completed successfully
Example 5: Feature flag check
# Check if feature flags are set
feature_flags = ["feature:new_ui", "feature:beta_access", "feature:advanced_search"]
enabled_features = r.exists(*feature_flags)
print(f"Enabled features: {enabled_features}")

# Check specific feature
new_ui_enabled = bool(r.exists("feature:new_ui"))
print(f"New UI enabled: {new_ui_enabled}")
Enabled features: 1 New UI enabled: True

EXPIRE

Docs: Redis EXPIRE Documentation

Sets the expiration time of a key in seconds.

Full description

EXPIRE sets a timeout on a key. After the timeout expires, the key is automatically deleted. Essential for implementing TTL-based cache invalidation, session management, and temporary data cleanup in Django applications.

Syntax

EXPIRE key seconds [NX|XX|GT|LT]

Parameters

  • key: The key to set expiration on.
  • seconds: Expiration time in seconds.
  • NX: Set expiry only if key has no expiry.
  • XX: Set expiry only if key already has expiry.
  • GT: Set expiry only if new expiry is greater than current.
  • LT: Set expiry only if new expiry is less than current.

Return value

Integer: 1 if timeout was set, 0 if key doesn't exist or timeout couldn't be set.

Example 1: Cache with 1-hour expiration
# Cache job search results for 1 hour
search_results = ["job:1", "job:5", "job:12"]
r.set("search:python_developer", ",".join(search_results))
expired = r.expire("search:python_developer", 3600)  # 1 hour
print(f"Search cache will expire in 1 hour: {bool(expired)}")
Search cache will expire in 1 hour: True
Example 2: Session timeout
# Set session to expire in 30 minutes
r.set("session:user123", "logged_in")
session_timeout = r.expire("session:user123", 1800)  # 30 minutes
print(f"Session timeout set: {bool(session_timeout)}")
Session timeout set: True
Example 3: Temporary rate limiting
# Rate limit: max 100 requests per minute
user_ip = "192.168.1.100"
rate_limit_key = f"rate_limit:{user_ip}"
r.incr(rate_limit_key)
r.expire(rate_limit_key, 60)  # Reset counter every minute
current_requests = int(r.get(rate_limit_key))
print(f"Requests from {user_ip}: {current_requests}/100")
Requests from 192.168.1.100: 1/100
Example 4: Email verification token
import uuid
# Create verification token that expires in 24 hours
token = str(uuid.uuid4())
r.set(f"verify_email:{token}", "user:123")
expired = r.expire(f"verify_email:{token}", 86400)  # 24 hours
print(f"Verification token expires in 24h: {bool(expired)}")
Verification token expires in 24h: True
Example 5: Conditional expiration (Redis 7.0+)
# Only set expiration if key doesn't already have one
r.set("persistent:config", "important_data")
try:
    # NX: only set expiry if no expiry exists
    set_expiry = r.execute_command("EXPIRE", "persistent:config", "3600", "NX")
    print(f"Expiry set on persistent config: {bool(set_expiry)}")
except:
    # Fallback for older Redis versions
    if r.ttl("persistent:config") == -1:  # No expiry set
        r.expire("persistent:config", 3600)
        print("Expiry set using fallback method")
Expiry set using fallback method

TTL

Docs: Redis TTL Documentation

Returns the expiration time in seconds of a key.

Full description

TTL returns the remaining time to live of a key that has a timeout. Perfect for monitoring cache expiration, implementing cache refresh logic, and debugging TTL-based systems in Django applications.

Syntax

TTL key

Parameters

  • key: The key to check TTL for.

Return value

Integer: TTL in seconds, -1 if key exists but has no expiry, -2 if key doesn't exist.

Example 1: Check cache freshness
# Check how long until cache expires
cache_key = "search:python_developer"
ttl_seconds = r.ttl(cache_key)
if ttl_seconds > 0:
    minutes_left = ttl_seconds // 60
    print(f"Cache expires in {minutes_left} minutes ({ttl_seconds}s)")
elif ttl_seconds == -1:
    print("Cache exists but never expires")
else:
    print("Cache doesn't exist")
Cache expires in 59 minutes (3581s)
Example 2: Session monitoring
# Monitor session expiration
session_key = "session:user123"
ttl = r.ttl(session_key)
if ttl > 0:
    if ttl < 300:  # Less than 5 minutes
        print(f"Session expires soon: {ttl} seconds left")
    else:
        print(f"Session active: {ttl//60} minutes remaining")
else:
    print("Session expired or doesn't exist")
Session active: 29 minutes remaining
Example 3: Rate limit check
# Check rate limit reset time
rate_key = "rate_limit:192.168.1.100"
reset_time = r.ttl(rate_key)
current_count = r.get(rate_key) or 0
if reset_time > 0:
    print(f"Rate limit resets in {reset_time}s (current: {current_count})")
else:
    print("Rate limit not active")
Rate limit resets in 59s (current: 1)
Example 4: Cache refresh decision
# Decide whether to refresh cache
def should_refresh_cache(cache_key, refresh_threshold=300):
    ttl = r.ttl(cache_key)
    if ttl == -2:
        return True, "Cache doesn't exist"
    elif ttl == -1:
        return False, "Cache never expires"
    elif ttl < refresh_threshold:
        return True, f"Cache expires soon ({ttl}s)"
    else:
        return False, f"Cache fresh ({ttl}s remaining)"

refresh, reason = should_refresh_cache("search:python_developer")
print(f"Should refresh: {refresh} - {reason}")
Should refresh: False - Cache fresh (3575s remaining)
Example 5: Batch TTL monitoring
# Monitor multiple cache keys
cache_keys = ["session:user123", "search:python_developer", "rate_limit:192.168.1.100"]
