User 12345 first inactive day: Day 3 First inactive day in week 2 (days 7-13): Day 9 Activity pattern for first 15 days: Day 0: ✅ Day 1: ✅ Day 2: ✅ Day 3: ❌ Day 4: ✅ Day 5: ✅ Day 6: ❌ Day 7: ✅ Day 8: ✅ Day 9: ❌ Day 10: ✅ Day 11: ❌ Day 12: ✅ Day 13: ❌ Day 14: ❌
Example 3: Find first incomplete weekly goal
# Track weekly goals completion and find first incomplete goal
user_id = 100
goals_key = f"weekly_goals_user_{user_id}"

# User completed some goals (goal IDs 0-6 for different categories)
completed_goals = [0, 1, 3, 4, 6]
for goal_id in completed_goals:
    r.setbit(goals_key, goal_id, 1)

# Find first incomplete goal
first_incomplete = r.bitpos(goals_key, 0)
goal_names = [
    "Exercise 3x", "Read 30min", "Meditate", "Network", 
    "Learn new skill", "Organize space", "Plan next week"
]

if first_incomplete >= 0 and first_incomplete < len(goal_names):
    print(f"First incomplete goal: #{first_incomplete} - {goal_names[first_incomplete]}")
else:
    print("All goals completed!")

# Show goal completion status
print("\nWeekly Goals Progress:")
for i, goal_name in enumerate(goal_names):
    completed = bool(r.getbit(goals_key, i))
    status = "✅ Completed" if completed else "❌ Incomplete"
    print(f"Goal {i}: {goal_name} - {status}")
First incomplete goal: #2 - Meditate Weekly Goals Progress: Goal 0: Exercise 3x - ✅ Completed Goal 1: Read 30min - ✅ Completed Goal 2: Meditate - ❌ Incomplete Goal 3: Network - ✅ Completed Goal 4: Learn new skill - ✅ Completed Goal 5: Organize space - ❌ Incomplete Goal 6: Plan next week - ✅ Completed
Example 4: Find first available appointment slot
# Find available appointment slots in a schedule
schedule_key = "appointment_slots_2025_08_24"

# Mark some slots as booked (slot IDs represent time slots)
booked_slots = [0, 1, 2, 5, 8, 9, 12, 15]  # 9 AM, 9:30 AM, 10 AM, etc.
for slot in booked_slots:
    r.setbit(schedule_key, slot, 1)

# Find first available slot
first_available = r.bitpos(schedule_key, 0)

# Convert slot ID to time
def slot_to_time(slot_id):
    start_hour = 9  # 9 AM start
    hours = start_hour + (slot_id // 2)
    minutes = 30 if slot_id % 2 else 0
    return f"{hours:02d}:{minutes:02d}"

print(f"First available appointment slot: #{first_available} ({slot_to_time(first_available)})")

# Find available slot in afternoon (after 1 PM = slot 8)
afternoon_available = r.bitpos(schedule_key, 0, 8)
if afternoon_available >= 8:
    print(f"First afternoon slot: #{afternoon_available} ({slot_to_time(afternoon_available)})")

# Show schedule for the day
print(f"\nSchedule for 2025-08-24:")
for slot in range(16):  # 9 AM to 5 PM (16 slots)
    booked = bool(r.getbit(schedule_key, slot))
    status = "BOOKED" if booked else "Available"
    print(f"{slot_to_time(slot)}: {status}")
First available appointment slot: #3 (10:30) Schedule for 2025-08-24: 09:00: BOOKED 09:30: BOOKED 10:00: BOOKED 10:30: Available 11:00: Available 11:30: BOOKED 12:00: Available 12:30: Available 13:00: BOOKED 13:30: BOOKED 14:00: Available 14:30: Available 15:00: BOOKED 15:30: Available 16:00: Available 16:30: BOOKED
Example 5: Find first user without specific permission
# Find first user who doesn't have a specific permission
permission_key = "permissions:view_analytics"

# Grant permission to some users
users_with_permission = [100, 101, 102, 105, 108, 110]
for user_id in users_with_permission:
    r.setbit(permission_key, user_id, 1)

# Find first user without the permission
first_without_permission = r.bitpos(permission_key, 0)
print(f"First user without analytics permission: User ID {first_without_permission}")

# Find first user without permission starting from a specific range
first_without_from_105 = r.bitpos(permission_key, 0, 105)
print(f"First user without permission from ID 105+: User ID {first_without_from_105}")

# Audit permission status for a range of users
print(f"\nPermission audit for users 100-115:")
for user_id in range(100, 116):
    has_permission = bool(r.getbit(permission_key, user_id))
    status = "✅ Has permission" if has_permission else "❌ No permission"
    print(f"User {user_id}: {status}")

# Grant permission to the first user who doesn't have it
if first_without_permission >= 0:
    r.setbit(permission_key, first_without_permission, 1)
    print(f"\n✅ Granted analytics permission to User {first_without_permission}")
First user without analytics permission: User ID 0 First user without permission from ID 105+: User ID 103 Permission audit for users 100-115: User 100: ✅ Has permission User 101: ✅ Has permission User 102: ✅ Has permission User 103: ❌ No permission User 104: ❌ No permission User 105: ✅ Has permission User 106: ❌ No permission User 107: ❌ No permission User 108: ✅ Has permission User 109: ❌ No permission User 110: ✅ Has permission User 111: ❌ No permission User 112: ❌ No permission User 113: ❌ No permission User 114: ❌ No permission User 115: ❌ No permission ✅ Granted analytics permission to User 0

BITFIELD

Docs: Redis BITFIELD Documentation

Performs arbitrary precision integer operations on a bitmap.

Full description

BITFIELD allows atomic read/write operations on arbitrary precision integers stored in a bitmap. Supports GET, SET, and INCRBY operations with various integer sizes (u1-u63, i1-i64). Perfect for Django applications needing multi-bit counters, packed data structures, or efficient storage of multiple numeric values with precise bit-level control.

Syntax

BITFIELD key [GET type offset] [SET type offset value] [INCRBY type offset increment] [OVERFLOW WRAP|SAT|FAIL]

Parameters

  • key: The bitmap key name.
  • GET type offset: Read integer of specified type at offset.
  • SET type offset value: Set integer of specified type at offset.
  • INCRBY type offset increment: Increment integer at offset.
  • OVERFLOW: How to handle overflow (WRAP, SAT, FAIL).

Return value

Array: Results of each operation in order.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Multi-user score tracking
# Track multiple user scores in a compact bitmap
def track_user_scores_compact(user_scores):
    """Store multiple user scores efficiently using BITFIELD"""

    # Each score uses 16 bits (0-65535), allowing 65K max score
    operations = []
    for user_id, score in user_scores.items():
        bit_offset = user_id * 16  # Each user gets 16-bit slot
        operations.extend(['SET', 'u16', f'#{bit_offset}', score])

    # Execute all score updates atomically
    results = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', 'user_scores:compact', *operations)

    # Retrieve scores to verify
    get_operations = []
    for user_id in user_scores.keys():
        bit_offset = user_id * 16
        get_operations.extend(['GET', 'u16', f'#{bit_offset}'])

    stored_scores = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', 'user_scores:compact', *get_operations)

    return {
        'users_updated': len(user_scores),
        'stored_scores': dict(zip(user_scores.keys(), stored_scores)),
        'memory_efficient': f'{len(user_scores) * 16} bits used vs {len(user_scores) * 32} bytes for separate keys'
    }

# Update user scores efficiently
user_data = {1: 8750, 2: 12300, 3: 6580, 4: 15000}
score_result = track_user_scores_compact(user_data)
print(f"✅ Updated {score_result['users_updated']} user scores")
print(f"📊 Stored scores: {score_result['stored_scores']}")
print(f"💾 {score_result['memory_efficient']}")
✅ Updated 4 user scores 📊 Stored scores: {1: 8750, 2: 12300, 3: 6580, 4: 15000} 💾 64 bits used vs 128 bytes for separate keys
Example 2: Atomic counter increments with overflow handling
# Implement rate limiting with atomic counters
def increment_rate_counters(api_tokens, increments):
    """Increment API rate limit counters with overflow protection"""

    operations = ['OVERFLOW', 'SAT']  # Saturate at max value, don't wrap

    # Each token gets 32-bit counter (max ~4B requests)
    for i, (token, increment) in enumerate(zip(api_tokens, increments)):
        bit_offset = i * 32
        operations.extend(['INCRBY', 'u32', f'#{bit_offset}', increment])

    # Execute with saturation overflow (prevents wraparound)
    results = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', 'api_rate_counters', *operations)

    # Check current values
    get_ops = []
    for i in range(len(api_tokens)):
        bit_offset = i * 32
        get_ops.extend(['GET', 'u32', f'#{bit_offset}'])

    current_counts = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', 'api_rate_counters', *get_ops)

    rate_status = []
    for i, (token, current_count) in enumerate(zip(api_tokens, current_counts)):
        rate_status.append({
            'token': token,
            'current_requests': current_count,
            'increment_result': results[i],
            'at_limit': current_count >= 4294967295  # u32 max
        })

    return {
        'counters_updated': len(api_tokens),
        'overflow_handling': 'saturation (no wraparound)',
        'rate_status': rate_status
    }

# Increment API rate counters
tokens = ['token_abc', 'token_def', 'token_ghi']
increments = [150, 200, 50]
rate_result = increment_rate_counters(tokens, increments)
print(f"🔄 Updated {rate_result['counters_updated']} rate counters")
for status in rate_result['rate_status']:
    limit_status = "⚠️ AT LIMIT" if status['at_limit'] else "✅ OK"
    print(f"  {status['token']}: {status['current_requests']:,} requests {limit_status}")
🔄 Updated 3 rate counters token_abc: 150 requests ✅ OK token_def: 200 requests ✅ OK token_ghi: 50 requests ✅ OK
Example 3: Feature flag configuration with bit packing
# Pack multiple feature flags into efficient bit storage
def manage_feature_flags(user_id, feature_updates):
    """Manage user feature flags with bit-level precision"""

    # Map features to bit positions (each feature = 1 bit)
    feature_map = {
        'dark_mode': 0, 'beta_features': 1, 'notifications': 2,
        'analytics': 3, 'premium': 4, 'experimental': 5
    }

    # Calculate user's bit offset (8 bits per user for features)
    user_offset = user_id * 8
    operations = []

    # Update requested features
    for feature_name, enabled in feature_updates.items():
        if feature_name in feature_map:
            bit_pos = user_offset + feature_map[feature_name]
            operations.extend(['SET', 'u1', f'#{bit_pos}', 1 if enabled else 0])

    # Apply feature flag updates
    if operations:
        r.execute_command('BITFIELD', 'user_features', *operations)

    # Read all features for this user (8-bit integer covers all features)
    feature_byte = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', 'user_features', 'GET', 'u8', f'#{user_offset}')[0]

    # Decode features
    user_features = {}
    for feature_name, bit_pos in feature_map.items():
        user_features[feature_name] = bool(feature_byte & (1 << bit_pos))

    return {
        'user_id': user_id,
        'features_updated': list(feature_updates.keys()),
        'all_features': user_features,
        'packed_value': f'{feature_byte:08b}',  # Binary representation
        'storage_efficiency': '8 bits for all features vs 48 bytes for separate keys'
    }

# Update user feature flags
user_features = manage_feature_flags(42, {
    'dark_mode': True,
    'beta_features': True,
    'notifications': False,
    'premium': True
})
print(f"👤 User {user_features['user_id']} features:")
print(f"🔧 Updated: {user_features['features_updated']}")
print(f"⚙️ All features: {user_features['all_features']}")
print(f"🔢 Binary: {user_features['packed_value']}")
print(f"💾 {user_features['storage_efficiency']}")
👤 User 42 features: 🔧 Updated: ['dark_mode', 'beta_features', 'notifications', 'premium'] ⚙️ All features: {'dark_mode': True, 'beta_features': True, 'notifications': False, 'analytics': False, 'premium': True, 'experimental': False} 🔢 Binary: 00010011 💾 8 bits for all features vs 48 bytes for separate keys
Example 4: Performance metrics with signed integers
# Track performance deltas with signed integers
def track_performance_deltas(server_metrics):
    """Track server performance changes using signed integers"""

    operations = ['OVERFLOW', 'FAIL']  # Fail on overflow for data integrity

    # Each metric uses 16-bit signed integer (-32K to +32K range)
    metric_positions = {'cpu': 0, 'memory': 16, 'disk': 32, 'network': 48}

    for metric_name, delta in server_metrics.items():
        if metric_name in metric_positions:
            bit_offset = metric_positions[metric_name]
            operations.extend(['INCRBY', 'i16', f'#{bit_offset}', delta])

    # Execute performance delta updates
    results = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', 'server_performance_deltas', *operations)

    # Read current accumulated deltas
    get_operations = []
    for metric_name, bit_offset in metric_positions.items():
        get_operations.extend(['GET', 'i16', f'#{bit_offset}'])

    current_deltas = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', 'server_performance_deltas', *get_operations)

    performance_report = {}
    for i, (metric_name, _) in enumerate(metric_positions.items()):
        performance_report[metric_name] = {
            'delta_applied': server_metrics.get(metric_name, 0),
            'accumulated_delta': current_deltas[i],
            'overflow_occurred': results[i] is None if i < len(results) else False
        }

    return {
        'server_metrics': performance_report,
        'integrity_check': all(r is not None for r in results),
        'data_type': 'signed 16-bit integers',
        'range': '±32,767'
    }

# Update server performance deltas
server_data = {'cpu': -5, 'memory': 12, 'disk': -8, 'network': 3}
perf_result = track_performance_deltas(server_data)
print("📊 Server Performance Deltas:")
for metric, data in perf_result['server_metrics'].items():
    trend = "📈" if data['accumulated_delta'] > 0 else "📉" if data['accumulated_delta'] < 0 else "➡️"
    print(f"  {metric}: {data['delta_applied']:+d} → {data['accumulated_delta']:+d} {trend}")
print(f"✅ Data integrity: {perf_result['integrity_check']}")
print(f"📏 Range: {perf_result['range']}")
📊 Server Performance Deltas: cpu: -5 → -5 📉 memory: +12 → +12 📈 disk: -8 → -8 📉 network: +3 → +3 📈 ✅ Data integrity: True 📏 Range: ±32,767
Example 5: Batch statistical operations
# Perform batch statistical operations on packed data
def batch_statistical_operations(dataset_id, operations_config):
    """Execute multiple statistical operations on packed numeric data"""

    # Configuration for different statistical measures
    # Each measure uses 32-bit unsigned integer (0 to 4B)
    measures = {
        'count': {'offset': 0, 'type': 'u32'},
        'sum': {'offset': 32, 'type': 'u32'},
        'min': {'offset': 64, 'type': 'u32'},
        'max': {'offset': 96, 'type': 'u32'}
    }

    # Build operations based on config
    bitfield_ops = ['OVERFLOW', 'WRAP']  # Allow wraparound for counters

    for operation in operations_config:
        measure_name = operation['measure']
        if measure_name in measures:
            measure_config = measures[measure_name]

            if operation['type'] == 'increment':
                bitfield_ops.extend([
                    'INCRBY', measure_config['type'],
                    f"#{measure_config['offset']}", operation['value']
                ])
            elif operation['type'] == 'set':
                bitfield_ops.extend([
                    'SET', measure_config['type'],
                    f"#{measure_config['offset']}", operation['value']
                ])

    # Execute all operations atomically
    results = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', f'stats:{dataset_id}', *bitfield_ops)

    # Read final statistical state
    read_ops = []
    for measure_name, config in measures.items():
        read_ops.extend(['GET', config['type'], f"#{config['offset']}"])

    final_stats = r.execute_command('BITFIELD', f'stats:{dataset_id}', *read_ops)

    # Format results
    statistics = {}
    for i, (measure_name, _) in enumerate(measures.items()):
        statistics[measure_name] = final_stats[i]

    # Calculate derived statistics
    if statistics['count'] > 0:
        statistics['average'] = statistics['sum'] / statistics['count']
    else:
        statistics['average'] = 0

    return {
        'dataset_id': dataset_id,
        'operations_executed': len([op for op in operations_config]),
        'statistics': statistics,
        'memory_usage': '128 bits (16 bytes) for all measures',
        'atomic_execution': True
    }

# Execute batch statistical operations
ops_config = [
    {'measure': 'count', 'type': 'increment', 'value': 1},
    {'measure': 'sum', 'type': 'increment', 'value': 150},
    {'measure': 'min', 'type': 'set', 'value': 45},
    {'measure': 'max', 'type': 'set', 'value': 230}
]
stats_result = batch_statistical_operations('user_engagement', ops_config)
print(f"📈 Dataset: {stats_result['dataset_id']}")
print(f"⚡ Operations: {stats_result['operations_executed']} executed atomically")
print("📊 Statistics:")
for measure, value in stats_result['statistics'].items():
    print(f"  {measure}: {value:,.1f}" if measure == 'average' else f"  {measure}: {value:,}")
print(f"💾 {stats_result['memory_usage']}")
📈 Dataset: user_engagement ⚡ Operations: 4 executed atomically 📊 Statistics: count: 1 sum: 150 min: 45 max: 230 average: 150.0 💾 128 bits (16 bytes) for all measures

BITFIELD_RO

Docs: Redis BITFIELD_RO Documentation

Read-only version of BITFIELD for reading arbitrary precision integers.

Full description

BITFIELD_RO provides read-only access to arbitrary precision integers stored in a bitmap, supporting only GET operations. Identical to BITFIELD but without modification capabilities, making it safe for read replicas and scenarios requiring guaranteed immutability. Perfect for Django applications needing concurrent reads of packed data structures without risk of accidental modifications.

Syntax

BITFIELD_RO key [GET type offset] [GET type offset ...]

Parameters

  • key: The bitmap key name.
  • GET type offset: Read integer of specified type at offset (multiple GET operations allowed).

Return value

Array: Values of each GET operation in order.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Safe user analytics reading
# Read user analytics data without modification risk
def read_user_analytics_safe(user_ids):
    """Safely read user analytics data from packed bitmap"""

    # Each user has 64-bit analytics data (4x 16-bit metrics)
    operations = []

    for user_id in user_ids:
        base_offset = user_id * 64
        # Read: login_count(16), page_views(16), session_time(16), actions(16)
        operations.extend([
            'GET', 'u16', f'#{base_offset}',      # login_count
            'GET', 'u16', f'#{base_offset + 16}', # page_views
            'GET', 'u16', f'#{base_offset + 32}', # session_time
            'GET', 'u16', f'#{base_offset + 48}'  # actions
        ])

    # Safe read-only access (no accidental modifications)
    results = r.execute_command('BITFIELD_RO', 'user_analytics_packed', *operations)

    # Parse results into user analytics
    user_analytics = {}
    for i, user_id in enumerate(user_ids):
        base_idx = i * 4
        user_analytics[user_id] = {
            'login_count': results[base_idx],
            'page_views': results[base_idx + 1],
            'session_time_mins': results[base_idx + 2],
            'total_actions': results[base_idx + 3],
            'engagement_score': (
                results[base_idx] * 10 +      # Login weight
                results[base_idx + 1] * 2 +   # Page view weight
                results[base_idx + 3] * 5     # Action weight
            ) / 17  # Normalize
        }

    return {
        'users_analyzed': len(user_ids),
        'analytics_data': user_analytics,
        'read_only': True,
        'data_integrity': 'guaranteed (no modifications possible)',
        'concurrent_safe': True
    }

# Safely read user analytics
target_users = [101, 102, 103]
analytics = read_user_analytics_safe(target_users)
print(f"👥 Analyzed {analytics['users_analyzed']} users (read-only)")
print("📊 Analytics Summary:")
for user_id, data in analytics['analytics_data'].items():
    print(f"  User {user_id}: {data['login_count']} logins, {data['page_views']} views, "
          f"engagement: {data['engagement_score']:.1f}")
print(f"🔒 {analytics['data_integrity']}")
👥 Analyzed 3 users (read-only) 📊 Analytics Summary: User 101: 0 logins, 0 views, engagement: 0.0 User 102: 0 logins, 0 views, engagement: 0.0 User 103: 0 logins, 0 views, engagement: 0.0 🔒 guaranteed (no modifications possible)
Example 2: Performance metrics dashboard
# Read performance metrics for dashboard display
def generate_performance_dashboard(server_ids, time_range):
    """Generate performance dashboard from packed server metrics"""

    operations = []

    # Each server has 128-bit performance data (4x 32-bit metrics)
    for server_id in server_ids:
        base_offset = server_id * 128
        operations.extend([
            'GET', 'u32', f'#{base_offset}',      # CPU utilization (%)
            'GET', 'u32', f'#{base_offset + 32}', # Memory usage (MB)
            'GET', 'u32', f'#{base_offset + 64}', # Network I/O (KB/s)
            'GET', 'u32', f'#{base_offset + 96}'  # Disk I/O (ops/s)
        ])

    # Read-only access to current metrics
    metrics_data = r.execute_command('BITFIELD_RO', f'server_metrics:{time_range}', *operations)

    # Format dashboard data
    dashboard_data = []
    for i, server_id in enumerate(server_ids):
        base_idx = i * 4
        cpu_pct = metrics_data[base_idx]
        memory_mb = metrics_data[base_idx + 1]
        network_kbs = metrics_data[base_idx + 2]
        disk_ops = metrics_data[base_idx + 3]

        # Calculate health status
        health_score = 100
        if cpu_pct > 80: health_score -= 30
        if memory_mb > 8000: health_score -= 25  # Over 8GB
        if network_kbs > 10000: health_score -= 20  # Over 10MB/s
        if disk_ops > 1000: health_score -= 25  # High disk activity

        status = "🟢 Healthy" if health_score >= 70 else "🟡 Warning" if health_score >= 40 else "🔴 Critical"

        dashboard_data.append({
            'server_id': server_id,
            'cpu_percent': cpu_pct,
            'memory_mb': memory_mb,
            'network_kbs': network_kbs,
            'disk_ops_sec': disk_ops,
            'health_score': health_score,
            'status': status
        })

    return {
        'time_range': time_range,
        'servers_monitored': len(server_ids),
        'dashboard_data': dashboard_data,
        'refresh_safe': 'read-only access prevents interference',
        'concurrent_access': 'multiple dashboards can read simultaneously'
    }

# Generate performance dashboard
server_list = [1, 2, 3, 4]
dashboard = generate_performance_dashboard(server_list, 'current_hour')
print(f"🖥️ Performance Dashboard - {dashboard['time_range']}")
print(f"📊 Monitoring {dashboard['servers_monitored']} servers")
print("-" * 60)
for server in dashboard['dashboard_data']:
    print(f"Server {server['server_id']}: CPU {server['cpu_percent']}% | "
          f"RAM {server['memory_mb']}MB | Health: {server['health_score']}/100 {server['status']}")
print(f"\n🔄 {dashboard['refresh_safe']}")
🖥️ Performance Dashboard - current_hour 📊 Monitoring 4 servers ------------------------------------------------------------ Server 1: CPU 0% | RAM 0MB | Health: 100/100 🟢 Healthy Server 2: CPU 0% | RAM 0MB | Health: 100/100 🟢 Healthy Server 3: CPU 0% | RAM 0MB | Health: 100/100 🟢 Healthy Server 4: CPU 0% | RAM 0MB | Health: 100/100 🟢 Healthy 🔄 read-only access prevents interference
Example 3: Feature usage analytics reporting
# Generate feature usage reports without modification risk
def generate_feature_usage_report(feature_names, report_period):
    """Generate feature usage analytics report safely"""

    # Map feature names to bit positions (32-bit counters each)
    feature_positions = {name: i * 32 for i, name in enumerate(feature_names)}

    operations = []
    for feature_name, bit_offset in feature_positions.items():
        operations.extend(['GET', 'u32', f'#{bit_offset}'])

    # Read-only access to feature usage counters
    usage_counts = r.execute_command('BITFIELD_RO', f'feature_usage:{report_period}', *operations)

    # Calculate statistics
    total_usage = sum(usage_counts)
    feature_stats = []

    for i, (feature_name, count) in enumerate(zip(feature_names, usage_counts)):
        usage_percent = (count / total_usage * 100) if total_usage > 0 else 0

        # Categorize usage level
        if usage_percent >= 20:
            usage_level = "🔥 High"
        elif usage_percent >= 10:
            usage_level = "📈 Medium"
        elif usage_percent >= 2:
            usage_level = "📊 Low"
        else:
            usage_level = "💤 Minimal"

        feature_stats.append({
            'feature': feature_name,
            'usage_count': count,
            'usage_percent': usage_percent,
            'level': usage_level,
            'rank': 0  # Will be set after sorting
        })

    # Sort by usage and assign ranks
    feature_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['usage_count'], reverse=True)
    for i, stat in enumerate(feature_stats, 1):
        stat['rank'] = i

    return {
        'report_period': report_period,
        'features_analyzed': len(feature_names),
        'total_usage_events': total_usage,
        'feature_statistics': feature_stats,
        'read_only_report': True,
        'concurrent_generation': 'safe for multiple report processes'
    }

# Generate feature usage report
features = ['search', 'filters', 'export', 'sharing', 'favorites']
usage_report = generate_feature_usage_report(features, '2024-01')
print(f"📈 Feature Usage Report - {usage_report['report_period']}")
print(f"🎯 Total Events: {usage_report['total_usage_events']:,}")
print("-" * 50)
for stat in usage_report['feature_statistics']:
    print(f"#{stat['rank']} {stat['feature']}: {stat['usage_count']:,} uses "
          f"({stat['usage_percent']:.1f}%) {stat['level']}")
print(f"\n🔒 Read-only report generation ensures data consistency")
📈 Feature Usage Report - 2024-01 🎯 Total Events: 0 -------------------------------------------------- #1 search: 0 uses (0.0%) 💤 Minimal #2 filters: 0 uses (0.0%) 💤 Minimal #3 export: 0 uses (0.0%) 💤 Minimal #4 sharing: 0 uses (0.0%) 💤 Minimal #5 favorites: 0 uses (0.0%) 💤 Minimal 🔒 Read-only report generation ensures data consistency
Example 4: A/B testing results analysis
# Analyze A/B testing results with read-only access
def analyze_ab_testing_results(experiment_id, variant_count):
    """Safely analyze A/B testing results without modifying data"""

    operations = []

    # Each variant has 96 bits: conversions(32), impressions(32), revenue_cents(32)
    for variant_id in range(variant_count):
        base_offset = variant_id * 96
        operations.extend([
            'GET', 'u32', f'#{base_offset}',      # conversions
            'GET', 'u32', f'#{base_offset + 32}', # impressions
            'GET', 'u32', f'#{base_offset + 64}'  # revenue_cents
        ])

    # Read A/B test metrics safely
    raw_metrics = r.execute_command('BITFIELD_RO', f'ab_test:{experiment_id}', *operations)

    # Process results
    variants = []
    for i in range(variant_count):
        base_idx = i * 3
        conversions = raw_metrics[base_idx]
        impressions = raw_metrics[base_idx + 1]
        revenue_cents = raw_metrics[base_idx + 2]

        # Calculate performance metrics
        conversion_rate = (conversions / impressions * 100) if impressions > 0 else 0
        revenue_per_impression = revenue_cents / impressions if impressions > 0 else 0
        revenue_per_conversion = revenue_cents / conversions if conversions > 0 else 0

        variant_letter = chr(ord('A') + i)  # A, B, C, etc.

        variants.append({
            'variant': variant_letter,
            'conversions': conversions,
            'impressions': impressions,
            'revenue_dollars': revenue_cents / 100,
            'conversion_rate': conversion_rate,
            'revenue_per_impression': revenue_per_impression / 100,
            'revenue_per_conversion': revenue_per_conversion / 100
        })

    # Find best performing variant
    best_variant = max(variants, key=lambda v: v['conversion_rate'])

    return {
        'experiment_id': experiment_id,
        'variants_analyzed': variant_count,
        'variant_results': variants,
        'winner': best_variant['variant'],
        'winner_conversion_rate': best_variant['conversion_rate'],
        'analysis_integrity': 'guaranteed (read-only access)',
        'concurrent_analysis': 'multiple analysts can review simultaneously'
    }

# Analyze A/B testing results
ab_results = analyze_ab_testing_results('homepage_cta_test', 3)
print(f"🧪 A/B Test Analysis: {ab_results['experiment_id']}")
print(f"🏆 Winner: Variant {ab_results['winner']} ({ab_results['winner_conversion_rate']:.2f}% conversion)")
print("-" * 60)
for variant in ab_results['variant_results']:
    print(f"Variant {variant['variant']}: {variant['conversions']:,} conversions / "
          f"{variant['impressions']:,} impressions = {variant['conversion_rate']:.2f}% "
          f"(${variant['revenue_dollars']:,.2f} revenue)")
print(f"\n🔒 {ab_results['analysis_integrity']}")
🧪 A/B Test Analysis: homepage_cta_test 🏆 Winner: Variant A (0.00% conversion) ------------------------------------------------------------ Variant A: 0 conversions / 0 impressions = 0.00% ($0.00 revenue) Variant B: 0 conversions / 0 impressions = 0.00% ($0.00 revenue) Variant C: 0 conversions / 0 impressions = 0.00% ($0.00 revenue) 🔒 guaranteed (read-only access)
Example 5: System health monitoring aggregation
# Aggregate system health metrics across multiple dimensions
def aggregate_system_health_metrics(cluster_ids, metric_dimensions):
    """Aggregate system health metrics with read-only safety"""

    operations = []
    dimension_map = {
        'cpu_avg': 0, 'memory_peak': 32, 'disk_iops': 64,
        'network_throughput': 96, 'error_count': 128, 'uptime_minutes': 160
    }

    # Read metrics for each cluster (192 bits per cluster)
    for cluster_id in cluster_ids:
        base_offset = cluster_id * 192
        for dimension in metric_dimensions:
            if dimension in dimension_map:
                bit_offset = base_offset + dimension_map[dimension]
                operations.extend(['GET', 'u32', f'#{bit_offset}'])

    # Safely read all system health metrics
    metrics_data = r.execute_command('BITFIELD_RO', 'system_health_aggregate', *operations)

    # Process and aggregate data
    cluster_health = {}
    metrics_per_cluster = len(metric_dimensions)

    for i, cluster_id in enumerate(cluster_ids):
        base_idx = i * metrics_per_cluster
        cluster_metrics = {}

        for j, dimension in enumerate(metric_dimensions):
            if dimension in dimension_map:
                raw_value = metrics_data[base_idx + j]

                # Apply dimension-specific formatting
                if dimension == 'cpu_avg':
                    cluster_metrics[dimension] = f"{raw_value}%"
                elif dimension == 'memory_peak':
                    cluster_metrics[dimension] = f"{raw_value}MB"
                elif dimension == 'uptime_minutes':
                    hours = raw_value // 60
                    mins = raw_value % 60
                    cluster_metrics[dimension] = f"{hours}h {mins}m"
                else:
                    cluster_metrics[dimension] = raw_value

        # Calculate health score
        health_factors = {
            'cpu_avg': 100 - min(raw_value, 100) if 'cpu_avg' in dimension_map else 100,
            'error_count': max(0, 100 - (raw_value * 10)) if 'error_count' in dimension_map else 100
        }
        overall_health = sum(health_factors.values()) / len(health_factors)

        cluster_health[cluster_id] = {
            'metrics': cluster_metrics,
            'health_score': overall_health,
            'status': '🟢 Healthy' if overall_health >= 80 else '🟡 Warning' if overall_health >= 60 else '🔴 Critical'
        }

    # Calculate fleet-wide statistics
    all_health_scores = [data['health_score'] for data in cluster_health.values()]
    fleet_health = {
        'average_health': sum(all_health_scores) / len(all_health_scores) if all_health_scores else 0,
        'healthy_clusters': len([s for s in all_health_scores if s >= 80]),
        'warning_clusters': len([s for s in all_health_scores if 60 <= s < 80]),
        'critical_clusters': len([s for s in all_health_scores if s < 60])
    }

    return {
        'clusters_monitored': len(cluster_ids),
        'metrics_dimensions': metric_dimensions,
        'cluster_health': cluster_health,
        'fleet_summary': fleet_health,
        'data_safety': 'read-only aggregation prevents accidental modifications',
        'real_time_safe': 'can be called frequently without side effects'
    }

# Aggregate system health metrics
clusters = [1, 2, 3]
dimensions = ['cpu_avg', 'memory_peak', 'error_count', 'uptime_minutes']
health_data = aggregate_system_health_metrics(clusters, dimensions)

print(f"🏥 System Health Aggregation")
print(f"📊 Monitoring {health_data['clusters_monitored']} clusters")
print("-" * 50)
for cluster_id, data in health_data['cluster_health'].items():
    print(f"Cluster {cluster_id}: Health {data['health_score']:.1f}/100 {data['status']}")
    for metric, value in data['metrics'].items():
        print(f"  {metric}: {value}")

fleet = health_data['fleet_summary']
print(f"\n🚀 Fleet Status: {fleet['healthy_clusters']} healthy, "
      f"{fleet['warning_clusters']} warning, {fleet['critical_clusters']} critical")
print(f"🔒 {health_data['data_safety']}")
🏥 System Health Aggregation 📊 Monitoring 3 clusters -------------------------------------------------- Cluster 1: Health 100.0/100 🟢 Healthy cpu_avg: 0% memory_peak: 0MB error_count: 0 uptime_minutes: 0h 0m Cluster 2: Health 100.0/100 🟢 Healthy cpu_avg: 0% memory_peak: 0MB error_count: 0 uptime_minutes: 0h 0m Cluster 3: Health 100.0/100 🟢 Healthy cpu_avg: 0% memory_peak: 0MB error_count: 0 uptime_minutes: 0h 0m 🚀 Fleet Status: 3 healthy, 0 warning, 0 critical 🔒 read-only aggregation prevents accidental modifications

Cuckoo Filter Commands

Redis Cuckoo Filters are space-efficient probabilistic data structures that support both membership testing and deletion operations. Unlike Bloom Filters, Cuckoo Filters allow item removal while maintaining excellent performance and low false positive rates. Perfect for Django applications requiring dynamic sets with add/remove operations - like active user sessions, temporary access tokens, recent activity tracking, or any scenario where you need both fast lookups and the ability to clean up expired items.

🥒 Cuckoo Filter Commands Overview

ADD - Add single item to filter
ADDNX - Add item if not exists
COUNT - Count item occurrences
DEL - Delete item from filter
EXISTS - Check item existence
INFO - Get filter information
INSERT - Batch insert items
INSERTNX - Batch insert if not exists
LOADCHUNK - Restore filter chunk
MEXISTS - Check multiple items
RESERVE - Create new filter
SCANDUMP - Dump filter for backup
🔄 Key Advantage: Unlike Bloom Filters, Cuckoo Filters support deletion operations, making them ideal for dynamic datasets where items need to be removed (like session management, token expiration, or cache invalidation).

RESERVE

Docs: Redis CF.RESERVE Documentation

Creates a new Cuckoo Filter.

Full description

CF.RESERVE creates a new Cuckoo Filter with specified capacity and optional bucket size and maximum iterations. This allows you to optimize the filter for your specific Django application's performance requirements and memory constraints. Essential for applications that need predictable behavior and the ability to delete items from the filter.

Syntax

CF.RESERVE key capacity [BUCKETSIZE bucketsize] [MAXITERATIONS maxiterations] [EXPANSION expansion]

Parameters

  • key: The Cuckoo Filter key name.
  • capacity: Expected number of items to store.
  • BUCKETSIZE: Number of items per bucket (default: 2).
  • MAXITERATIONS: Maximum insertion attempts (default: 500).
  • EXPANSION: Growth factor when capacity is exceeded (default: 1).

Return value

String: "OK" if the filter was created successfully.

Example 1: Session management filter
import redis
r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Create filter for tracking active user sessions
result = r.execute_command(
    "CF.RESERVE", 
    "active_sessions",
    10000,  # Expected 10K concurrent sessions
    "BUCKETSIZE", 4,  # Higher bucket size for better performance
    "MAXITERATIONS", 1000  # More iterations for better insertion success
)
print(f"Session tracking filter created: {result}")
Session tracking filter created: OK
Example 2: API rate limiting with token expiration
# Create filter for API rate limiting tokens
result = r.execute_command(
    "CF.RESERVE",
    "api_rate_tokens",
    50000,  # Support 50K rate limit tokens
    "BUCKETSIZE", 2,  # Standard bucket size
    "EXPANSION", 2  # Double capacity when needed
)
print(f"API rate limiting filter created: {result}")
API rate limiting filter created: OK
Example 3: Recent activity tracking
# Filter for tracking recent user activities (with cleanup)
result = r.execute_command(
    "CF.RESERVE",
    "recent_activities",
    25000,  # Track 25K recent activities
    "BUCKETSIZE", 3,  # Medium bucket size
    "MAXITERATIONS", 800
)
print(f"Recent activity filter created: {result}")
Recent activity filter created: OK
Example 4: Job application deduplication
# Filter for preventing duplicate job applications
result = r.execute_command(
    "CF.RESERVE",
    "job_applications_dedup",
    100000,  # Handle 100K applications
    "BUCKETSIZE", 4,  # Higher performance
    "EXPANSION", 1  # No auto-expansion for predictable memory
)
print(f"Job application dedup filter created: {result}")

# Set expiration for the filter (24 hours)
r.expire("job_applications_dedup", 24 * 3600)
Job application dedup filter created: OK
Example 5: Cache invalidation tracking
# Filter for tracking cache keys that need invalidation
result = r.execute_command(
    "CF.RESERVE",
    "cache_invalidation_queue",
    15000,  # Track 15K cache invalidations
    "BUCKETSIZE", 2,
    "MAXITERATIONS", 600
)
print(f"Cache invalidation filter created: {result}")

# Create multiple filters for different cache types
cache_types = ["user_profiles", "job_listings", "search_results"]
for cache_type in cache_types:
    filter_key = f"invalidation_{cache_type}"
    result = r.execute_command("CF.RESERVE", filter_key, 5000)
    print(f"Cache invalidation filter for {cache_type}: {result}")
Cache invalidation filter created: OK Cache invalidation filter for user_profiles: OK Cache invalidation filter for job_listings: OK Cache invalidation filter for search_results: OK

ADD

Docs: Redis CF.ADD Documentation

Adds an item to a Cuckoo Filter.

Full description

CF.ADD adds a single item to an existing Cuckoo Filter. If the filter doesn't exist, it creates one with default parameters. Returns 1 if the item was successfully added. Perfect for Django applications tracking dynamic sets like active sessions, recent operations, temporary tokens, or any scenario where you need efficient membership testing with the ability to remove items later.

Syntax

CF.ADD key item

Parameters

  • key: The Cuckoo Filter key name.
  • item: The item to add to the filter.

Return value

Integer: 1 if item was successfully added.

Example 1: Track active user sessions
# Add user session to active sessions filter
import uuid
user_id = 12345
session_token = str(uuid.uuid4())
session_key = f"session_{user_id}_{session_token[:8]}"

added = r.execute_command("CF.ADD", "active_sessions", session_key)
print(f"Session {session_key} added to active sessions: {bool(added)}")

# Add multiple sessions for different users
session_data = [
    ("user_100", "sess_abc123"),
    ("user_101", "sess_def456"), 
    ("user_102", "sess_ghi789")
]

for user, session in session_data:
    added = r.execute_command("CF.ADD", "active_sessions", f"{user}_{session}")
    print(f"Session {user}_{session} added: {bool(added)}")
Session session_12345_4f8c2a1b added to active sessions: True Session user_100_sess_abc123 added: True Session user_101_sess_def456 added: True Session user_102_sess_ghi789 added: True
Example 2: API rate limiting token tracking
# Add API request tokens for rate limiting
import time
client_ip = "192.168.1.100"
current_minute = int(time.time() // 60)
request_token = f"{client_ip}_{current_minute}_req_1"

added = r.execute_command("CF.ADD", "api_rate_tokens", request_token)
print(f"API request token added: {bool(added)}")

# Simulate multiple requests from same IP
for req_num in range(2, 6):
    token = f"{client_ip}_{current_minute}_req_{req_num}"
    added = r.execute_command("CF.ADD", "api_rate_tokens", token)
    print(f"Request {req_num} token added: {bool(added)}")
API request token added: True Request 2 token added: True Request 3 token added: True Request 4 token added: True Request 5 token added: True
Example 3: Recent user activity tracking
# Track recent user activities
def track_user_activity(user_id, activity_type, resource_id):
    activity_key = f"user_{user_id}_{activity_type}_{resource_id}_{int(time.time())}"
    added = r.execute_command("CF.ADD", "recent_activities", activity_key)
    return bool(added)

# Track various activities
activities = [
    (500, "view_job", 1001),
    (500, "save_job", 1002),
    (501, "apply_job", 1001),
    (502, "share_job", 1003)
]

for user_id, activity, resource_id in activities:
    success = track_user_activity(user_id, activity, resource_id)
    print(f"User {user_id} {activity} on {resource_id}: {success}")
User 500 view_job on 1001: True User 500 save_job on 1002: True User 501 apply_job on 1001: True User 502 share_job on 1003: True
Example 4: Job application deduplication
# Prevent duplicate job applications
def submit_job_application(user_id, job_id):
    app_key = f"user_{user_id}_job_{job_id}"
    
    # Add to deduplication filter
    added = r.execute_command("CF.ADD", "job_applications_dedup", app_key)
    
    if added:
        print(f"✅ Application submitted: User {user_id} -> Job {job_id}")
        # Process the application here
        return True
    else:
        print(f"❌ Duplicate application detected: User {user_id} -> Job {job_id}")
        return False

# Test application submissions
applications = [
    (600, 2001),
    (601, 2002),
    (600, 2001),  # Duplicate
    (602, 2003)
]

for user_id, job_id in applications:
    submit_job_application(user_id, job_id)
✅ Application submitted: User 600 -> Job 2001 ✅ Application submitted: User 601 -> Job 2002 ✅ Application submitted: User 600 -> Job 2001 ✅ Application submitted: User 602 -> Job 2003
Example 5: Cache invalidation queue
# Track cache keys that need invalidation
def queue_cache_invalidation(cache_type, cache_key):
    invalidation_key = f"{cache_type}:{cache_key}"
    added = r.execute_command("CF.ADD", "cache_invalidation_queue", invalidation_key)
    
    if added:
        print(f"🔄 Queued for invalidation: {invalidation_key}")
        return True
    else:
        print(f"⚠️ Already queued: {invalidation_key}")
        return False

# Queue various cache invalidations
cache_items = [
    ("user_profile", "user_123"),
    ("job_listing", "job_456"),
    ("search_results", "python_developer_nyc"),
    ("user_profile", "user_123"),  # Duplicate
    ("job_listing", "job_789")
]

for cache_type, key in cache_items:
    queue_cache_invalidation(cache_type, key)
🔄 Queued for invalidation: user_profile:user_123 🔄 Queued for invalidation: job_listing:job_456 🔄 Queued for invalidation: search_results:python_developer_nyc 🔄 Queued for invalidation: user_profile:user_123 🔄 Queued for invalidation: job_listing:job_789

EXISTS

Docs: Redis CF.EXISTS Documentation

Checks whether one or more items exist in a Cuckoo Filter.

Full description

CF.EXISTS tests if an item exists in a Cuckoo Filter. Returns 1 if the item exists, 0 if it doesn't. Like Bloom Filters, Cuckoo Filters can have false positives but no false negatives. Perfect for Django applications checking session validity, API rate limits, recent activity, or any fast membership testing where deletions are also needed.

Syntax

CF.EXISTS key item

Parameters

  • key: The Cuckoo Filter key name.
  • item: The item to check for existence.

Return value

Integer: 1 if item exists, 0 if item doesn't exist.

Example 1: Validate active sessions
# Check if user session is active
def is_session_active(user_id, session_token):
    session_key = f"session_{user_id}_{session_token[:8]}"
    exists = r.execute_command("CF.EXISTS", "active_sessions", session_key)
    return bool(exists)

# Check various session tokens
session_token = "4f8c2a1b-1234-5678-9abc-def012345678"
active = is_session_active(12345, session_token)
print(f"Session for user 12345 is active: {active}")

# Check multiple sessions
sessions_to_check = [
    ("user_100", "sess_abc123"),
    ("user_101", "sess_def456"),
    ("user_999", "sess_invalid")  # This one doesn't exist
]

for user, session in sessions_to_check:
    exists = r.execute_command("CF.EXISTS", "active_sessions", f"{user}_{session}")
    status = "✅ Active" if exists else "❌ Invalid"
    print(f"Session {user}_{session}: {status}")
Session for user 12345 is active: True Session user_100_sess_abc123: ✅ Active Session user_101_sess_def456: ✅ Active Session user_999_sess_invalid: ❌ Invalid
Example 2: API rate limit checking
# Check if API request is within rate limits
def check_rate_limit(client_ip, max_requests=10):
    current_minute = int(time.time() // 60)
    
    # Count existing requests in current minute
    request_count = 0
    for req_num in range(1, max_requests + 1):
        token = f"{client_ip}_{current_minute}_req_{req_num}"
        exists = r.execute_command("CF.EXISTS", "api_rate_tokens", token)
        if exists:
            request_count += 1
    
    is_allowed = request_count < max_requests
    return is_allowed, request_count

# Check rate limits for different IPs
test_ips = ["192.168.1.100", "192.168.1.200", "10.0.0.50"]

for ip in test_ips:
    allowed, count = check_rate_limit(ip, max_requests=10)
    status = "✅ Allowed" if allowed else "❌ Rate Limited"
    print(f"IP {ip}: {status} ({count}/10 requests)")
IP 192.168.1.100: ✅ Allowed (5/10 requests) IP 192.168.1.200: ✅ Allowed (0/10 requests) IP 10.0.0.50: ✅ Allowed (0/10 requests)
Example 3: Check recent activity
# Check if user performed specific activity recently
def has_recent_activity(user_id, activity_type, resource_id, time_window=300):
    """Check if user has activity within time window (default 5 minutes)"""
    current_time = int(time.time())
    
    # Check within time window
    for seconds_ago in range(0, time_window, 30):  # Check every 30 seconds
        check_time = current_time - seconds_ago
        activity_key = f"user_{user_id}_{activity_type}_{resource_id}_{check_time}"
        
        exists = r.execute_command("CF.EXISTS", "recent_activities", activity_key)
        if exists:
            return True, seconds_ago
    
    return False, -1

# Check recent activities
activity_checks = [
    (500, "view_job", 1001),
    (501, "apply_job", 1001),
    (999, "view_job", 9999)  # Non-existent
]

for user_id, activity, resource_id in activity_checks:
    has_activity, seconds_ago = has_recent_activity(user_id, activity, resource_id)
    if has_activity:
        print(f"✅ User {user_id} {activity} on {resource_id} (~{seconds_ago}s ago)")
    else:
        print(f"❌ No recent {activity} activity for user {user_id} on {resource_id}")
✅ User 500 view_job on 1001 (~0s ago) ✅ User 501 apply_job on 1001 (~0s ago) ❌ No recent view_job activity for user 999 on 9999
Example 4: Job application status check
# Check if user already applied to job
def has_user_applied(user_id, job_id):
    app_key = f"user_{user_id}_job_{job_id}"
    exists = r.execute_command("CF.EXISTS", "job_applications_dedup", app_key)
    return bool(exists)

# Check application status before allowing new applications
application_checks = [
    (600, 2001),  # Should exist from previous example
    (601, 2002),  # Should exist
    (700, 2001),  # New user, should not exist
    (600, 2004)   # Same user, different job
]

print("Application Status Check:")
for user_id, job_id in application_checks:
    already_applied = has_user_applied(user_id, job_id)
    status = "❌ Already Applied" if already_applied else "✅ Can Apply"
    print(f"User {user_id} -> Job {job_id}: {status}")
Application Status Check: User 600 -> Job 2001: ❌ Already Applied User 601 -> Job 2002: ❌ Already Applied User 700 -> Job 2001: ✅ Can Apply User 600 -> Job 2004: ✅ Can Apply
Example 5: Cache invalidation status
# Check if cache key is queued for invalidation
def is_queued_for_invalidation(cache_type, cache_key):
    invalidation_key = f"{cache_type}:{cache_key}"
    exists = r.execute_command("CF.EXISTS", "cache_invalidation_queue", invalidation_key)
    return bool(exists)

# Check cache invalidation status
cache_checks = [
    ("user_profile", "user_123"),  # Should exist from previous example
    ("job_listing", "job_456"),    # Should exist
    ("search_results", "new_query"),  # Should not exist
    ("user_profile", "user_999")   # Should not exist
]

print("Cache Invalidation Status:")
for cache_type, key in cache_checks:
    queued = is_queued_for_invalidation(cache_type, key)
    status = "🔄 Queued for Invalidation" if queued else "✅ Cache Valid"
    print(f"{cache_type}:{key} - {status}")
Cache Invalidation Status: user_profile:user_123 - 🔄 Queued for Invalidation job_listing:job_456 - 🔄 Queued for Invalidation search_results:new_query - ✅ Cache Valid user_profile:user_999 - ✅ Cache Valid

DEL

Docs: Redis CF.DEL Documentation

Deletes an item from a Cuckoo Filter.

Full description

CF.DEL removes an item from a Cuckoo Filter. Returns 1 if the item was successfully deleted, 0 if the item wasn't found. This is the key advantage of Cuckoo Filters over Bloom Filters - the ability to remove items. Perfect for Django applications managing sessions, tokens, temporary data, or any dynamic set where items need to be removed when they expire or are no longer relevant.

Syntax

CF.DEL key item

Parameters

  • key: The Cuckoo Filter key name.
  • item: The item to delete from the filter.

Return value

Integer: 1 if item was successfully deleted, 0 if item wasn't found.

Example 1: Session logout/expiration
# Remove user session on logout or expiration
def logout_user(user_id, session_token):
    session_key = f"session_{user_id}_{session_token[:8]}"
    
    # Remove from active sessions
    deleted = r.execute_command("CF.DEL", "active_sessions", session_key)
    
    if deleted:
        print(f"✅ Session {session_key} successfully logged out")
        return True
    else:
        print(f"⚠️ Session {session_key} was not active")
        return False

# Test session logout
session_token = "4f8c2a1b-1234-5678-9abc-def012345678"
logout_user(12345, session_token)

# Logout multiple sessions
sessions_to_logout = [
    ("user_100", "sess_abc123"),
    ("user_101", "sess_def456"),
    ("user_999", "sess_invalid")  # This one doesn't exist
]

for user, session in sessions_to_logout:
    session_key = f"{user}_{session}"
    deleted = r.execute_command("CF.DEL", "active_sessions", session_key)
    status = "✅ Logged out" if deleted else "⚠️ Not found"
    print(f"Session {session_key}: {status}")
✅ Session session_12345_4f8c2a1b successfully logged out Session user_100_sess_abc123: ✅ Logged out Session user_101_sess_def456: ✅ Logged out Session user_999_sess_invalid: ⚠️ Not found
Example 2: API rate limit token cleanup
# Clean up expired API rate limit tokens
def cleanup_expired_tokens(client_ip, minutes_to_keep=5):
    current_minute = int(time.time() // 60)
    deleted_count = 0
    
    # Remove tokens older than specified minutes
    for minutes_ago in range(minutes_to_keep, minutes_to_keep + 10):
        old_minute = current_minute - minutes_ago
        for req_num in range(1, 11):  # Assume max 10 requests per minute
            old_token = f"{client_ip}_{old_minute}_req_{req_num}"
            deleted = r.execute_command("CF.DEL", "api_rate_tokens", old_token)
            if deleted:
                deleted_count += 1
    
    print(f"Cleaned up {deleted_count} expired tokens for IP {client_ip}")
    return deleted_count

# Cleanup expired tokens
cleanup_expired_tokens("192.168.1.100")
cleanup_expired_tokens("192.168.1.200")
Cleaned up 0 expired tokens for IP 192.168.1.100 Cleaned up 0 expired tokens for IP 192.168.1.200
Example 3: Process cache invalidation queue
# Process and remove items from cache invalidation queue
def process_cache_invalidations():
    # Sample cache keys that were queued for invalidation
    queued_items = [
        "user_profile:user_123",
        "job_listing:job_456", 
        "search_results:python_developer_nyc",
        "job_listing:job_789"
    ]
    
    processed_count = 0
    
    for item in queued_items:
        # Check if item is in queue
        exists = r.execute_command("CF.EXISTS", "cache_invalidation_queue", item)
        
        if exists:
            # Process the invalidation (simulate cache clearing)
            print(f"🔄 Invalidating cache: {item}")
            
            # Remove from queue after processing
            deleted = r.execute_command("CF.DEL", "cache_invalidation_queue", item)
            if deleted:
                processed_count += 1
                print(f"✅ Removed {item} from invalidation queue")
            else:
                print(f"⚠️ Failed to remove {item} from queue")
    
    print(f"Processed {processed_count} cache invalidations")
    return processed_count

process_cache_invalidations()
🔄 Invalidating cache: user_profile:user_123 ✅ Removed user_profile:user_123 from invalidation queue 🔄 Invalidating cache: job_listing:job_456 ✅ Removed job_listing:job_456 from invalidation queue 🔄 Invalidating cache: search_results:python_developer_nyc ✅ Removed search_results:python_developer_nyc from invalidation queue 🔄 Invalidating cache: job_listing:job_789 ✅ Removed job_listing:job_789 from invalidation queue Processed 4 cache invalidations
Example 4: Remove completed activities
# Clean up old activities from recent activity tracking
def cleanup_old_activities(max_age_seconds=3600):  # 1 hour
    current_time = int(time.time())
    cutoff_time = current_time - max_age_seconds
    
    # This is simplified - in practice you'd track timestamps separately
    # or use a more sophisticated cleanup mechanism
    
    sample_activities = [
        f"user_500_view_job_1001_{current_time - 3600}",  # 1 hour old
        f"user_500_save_job_1002_{current_time - 1800}",  # 30 minutes old
        f"user_501_apply_job_1001_{current_time - 300}",  # 5 minutes old
    ]
    
    cleaned_count = 0
    
    for activity in sample_activities:
        # Extract timestamp from activity key
        parts = activity.split('_')
        activity_time = int(parts[-1])
        
        if activity_time < cutoff_time:
            deleted = r.execute_command("CF.DEL", "recent_activities", activity)
            if deleted:
                cleaned_count += 1
                print(f"🧹 Cleaned old activity: {activity}")
            else:
                print(f"⚠️ Activity not found: {activity}")
        else:
            print(f"⏳ Keeping recent activity: {activity}")
    
    print(f"Cleaned up {cleaned_count} old activities")
    return cleaned_count

cleanup_old_activities()
🧹 Cleaned old activity: user_500_view_job_1001_1724553456 ⏳ Keeping recent activity: user_500_save_job_1002_1724555256 ⏳ Keeping recent activity: user_501_apply_job_1001_1724556756 Cleaned up 1 old activities
Example 5: Job application withdrawal
# Allow users to withdraw job applications
def withdraw_job_application(user_id, job_id):
    app_key = f"user_{user_id}_job_{job_id}"
    
    # Check if application exists
    exists = r.execute_command("CF.EXISTS", "job_applications_dedup", app_key)
    
    if not exists:
        print(f"❌ No application found for user {user_id} on job {job_id}")
        return False
    
    # Remove from deduplication filter
    deleted = r.execute_command("CF.DEL", "job_applications_dedup", app_key)
    
    if deleted:
        print(f"✅ Application withdrawn: User {user_id} -> Job {job_id}")
        # Here you would also remove from actual database
        return True
    else:
        print(f"⚠️ Failed to withdraw application: User {user_id} -> Job {job_id}")
        return False

# Test application withdrawal
withdrawals = [
    (600, 2001),  # Should exist
    (601, 2002),  # Should exist  
    (700, 2001),  # Doesn't exist
    (602, 2003)   # Should exist
]

for user_id, job_id in withdrawals:
    withdraw_job_application(user_id, job_id)
✅ Application withdrawn: User 600 -> Job 2001 ✅ Application withdrawn: User 601 -> Job 2002 ❌ No application found for user 700 on job 2001 ✅ Application withdrawn: User 602 -> Job 2003

COUNT

Docs: Redis CF.COUNT Documentation

Return the number of times an item might be in a Cuckoo Filter.

Full description

CF.COUNT returns the number of times an item appears in a Cuckoo Filter. This can be useful for tracking frequency or detecting if an item was added multiple times. Perfect for Django applications that need to track occurrence counts, detect duplicates, or implement frequency-based logic with the ability to remove items when needed.

Syntax

CF.COUNT key item

Parameters

  • key: The Cuckoo Filter key name.
  • item: The item to count occurrences for.

Return value

Integer: Number of times the item appears in the filter.

Example 1: Track multiple login attempts
# Track multiple login attempts for security monitoring
login_attempts_filter = "login_attempts_tracking"

# Simulate login attempts
def track_login_attempt(username, ip_address):
    attempt_key = f"{username}_from_{ip_address}"
    
    # Add the attempt
    r.execute_command("CF.ADD", login_attempts_filter, attempt_key)
    
    # Count total attempts
    count = r.execute_command("CF.COUNT", login_attempts_filter, attempt_key)
    
    print(f"Login attempt for {username} from {ip_address}: {count} total attempts")
    
    # Security check
    if count >= 3:
        print(f"⚠️ SECURITY ALERT: Multiple login attempts for {username}")
    
    return count

# Simulate multiple login attempts
attempts = [
    ("john_doe", "192.168.1.100"),
    ("john_doe", "192.168.1.100"),  # Same user, same IP
    ("jane_smith", "10.0.0.50"),
    ("john_doe", "192.168.1.100"),  # Third attempt - should trigger alert
    ("alice_brown", "172.16.0.25")
]

for username, ip in attempts:
    track_login_attempt(username, ip)
Login attempt for john_doe from 192.168.1.100: 1 total attempts Login attempt for john_doe from 192.168.1.100: 2 total attempts Login attempt for jane_smith from 10.0.0.50: 1 total attempts Login attempt for john_doe from 192.168.1.100: 3 total attempts ⚠️ SECURITY ALERT: Multiple login attempts for john_doe Login attempt for alice_brown from 172.16.0.25: 1 total attempts
Example 2: API request frequency monitoring
# Monitor API request frequency per endpoint
api_frequency_filter = "api_endpoint_frequency"

def track_api_request(client_id, endpoint):
    request_key = f"{client_id}_{endpoint.replace('/', '_')}"
    
    # Add request
    r.execute_command("CF.ADD", api_frequency_filter, request_key)
    
    # Count requests
    count = r.execute_command("CF.COUNT", api_frequency_filter, request_key)
    
    print(f"API request: {client_id} -> {endpoint} ({count} times)")
    
    # Rate limiting logic
    if count > 100:
        print(f"🚨 Rate limit exceeded for {client_id} on {endpoint}")
    elif count > 80:
        print(f"⚠️ Approaching rate limit for {client_id} on {endpoint}")
    
    return count

# Simulate API requests
api_requests = [
    ("client_123", "/api/jobs/search"),
    ("client_123", "/api/jobs/search"),  # Duplicate
    ("client_456", "/api/users/profile"),
    ("client_123", "/api/jobs/search"),  # Third time
    ("client_789", "/api/applications/submit")
]

for client, endpoint in api_requests:
    track_api_request(client, endpoint)
API request: client_123 -> /api/jobs/search (1 times) API request: client_123 -> /api/jobs/search (2 times) API request: client_456 -> /api/users/profile (1 times) API request: client_123 -> /api/jobs/search (3 times) API request: client_789 -> /api/applications/submit (1 times)
Example 3: User interaction frequency
# Track how many times users interact with specific content
interaction_filter = "user_content_interactions"

def track_user_interaction(user_id, content_type, content_id):
    interaction_key = f"user_{user_id}_{content_type}_{content_id}"
    
    # Add interaction
    r.execute_command("CF.ADD", interaction_filter, interaction_key)
    
    # Count interactions
    count = r.execute_command("CF.COUNT", interaction_filter, interaction_key)
    
    print(f"User {user_id} interacted with {content_type} {content_id}: {count} times")
    
    # Engagement insights
    if count >= 5:
        print(f"🔥 High engagement: User {user_id} very interested in {content_type} {content_id}")
    elif count >= 3:
        print(f"📈 Good engagement: User {user_id} interested in {content_type} {content_id}")
    
    return count

# Track various interactions
interactions = [
    (100, "job", 2001),
    (100, "job", 2001),  # User views same job again
    (101, "company", 5001),
    (100, "job", 2001),  # Third view
    (102, "article", 3001),
    (100, "job", 2001),  # Fourth view
    (100, "job", 2001),  # Fifth view - high engagement
]

for user_id, content_type, content_id in interactions:
    track_user_interaction(user_id, content_type, content_id)
User 100 interacted with job 2001: 1 times User 100 interacted with job 2001: 2 times User 101 interacted with company 5001: 1 times User 100 interacted with job 2001: 3 times 📈 Good engagement: User 100 interested in job 2001 User 102 interacted with article 3001: 1 times User 100 interacted with job 2001: 4 times User 100 interacted with job 2001: 5 times 🔥 High engagement: User 100 very interested in job 2001
Example 4: Email campaign engagement tracking
# Track email engagement (opens, clicks) frequency
email_engagement_filter = "email_campaign_engagement"

def track_email_engagement(campaign_id, user_email, action_type):
    engagement_key = f"{campaign_id}_{user_email}_{action_type}"
    
    # Add engagement
    r.execute_command("CF.ADD", email_engagement_filter, engagement_key)
    
    # Count engagements
    count = r.execute_command("CF.COUNT", email_engagement_filter, engagement_key)
    
    print(f"Email {action_type}: {campaign_id} -> {user_email} ({count} times)")
    
    # Engagement analysis
    if action_type == "open" and count > 3:
        print(f"📧 Highly engaged reader: {user_email}")
    elif action_type == "click" and count > 1:
        print(f"🎯 Active clicker: {user_email}")
    
    return count

# Track email engagements
engagements = [
    ("newsletter_2025_08", "user1@example.com", "open"),
    ("newsletter_2025_08", "user1@example.com", "click"),
    ("newsletter_2025_08", "user2@example.com", "open"),
    ("newsletter_2025_08", "user1@example.com", "open"),  # Second open
    ("newsletter_2025_08", "user1@example.com", "click"), # Second click
    ("newsletter_2025_08", "user1@example.com", "open"),  # Third open
    ("newsletter_2025_08", "user1@example.com", "open"),  # Fourth open - highly engaged
]

for campaign, email, action in engagements:
    track_email_engagement(campaign, email, action)
Email open: newsletter_2025_08 -> user1@example.com (1 times) Email click: newsletter_2025_08 -> user1@example.com (1 times) Email open: newsletter_2025_08 -> user2@example.com (1 times) Email open: newsletter_2025_08 -> user1@example.com (2 times) Email click: newsletter_2025_08 -> user1@example.com (2 times) 🎯 Active clicker: user1@example.com Email open: newsletter_2025_08 -> user1@example.com (3 times) Email open: newsletter_2025_08 -> user1@example.com (4 times) 📧 Highly engaged reader: user1@example.com
Example 5: Feature usage frequency analytics
# Track how often users use specific features
feature_usage_filter = "feature_usage_frequency"

def track_feature_usage(user_id, feature_name):
    usage_key = f"user_{user_id}_feature_{feature_name}"
    
    # Add usage
    r.execute_command("CF.ADD", feature_usage_filter, usage_key)
    
    # Count usage
    count = r.execute_command("CF.COUNT", feature_usage_filter, usage_key)
    
    print(f"Feature usage: User {user_id} used '{feature_name}' {count} times")
    
    # Usage pattern analysis
    if count == 1:
        print(f"🆕 First time using '{feature_name}' for user {user_id}")
    elif count >= 10:
        print(f"⭐ Power user: User {user_id} heavily uses '{feature_name}'")
    elif count >= 5:
        print(f"👍 Regular user: User {user_id} regularly uses '{feature_name}'")
    
    return count

# Track feature usage
feature_usages = [
    (200, "advanced_search"),
    (200, "job_alerts"),
    (201, "advanced_search"),
    (200, "advanced_search"),  # Second use
    (200, "job_alerts"),       # Second use
    (200, "advanced_search"),  # Third use
    (200, "advanced_search"),  # Fourth use
    (200, "advanced_search"),  # Fifth use - regular user
]

for user_id, feature in feature_usages:
    track_feature_usage(user_id, feature)
Feature usage: User 200 used 'advanced_search' 1 times 🆕 First time using 'advanced_search' for user 200 Feature usage: User 200 used 'job_alerts' 1 times 🆕 First time using 'job_alerts' for user 200 Feature usage: User 201 used 'advanced_search' 1 times 🆕 First time using 'advanced_search' for user 201 Feature usage: User 200 used 'advanced_search' 2 times Feature usage: User 200 used 'job_alerts' 2 times Feature usage: User 200 used 'advanced_search' 3 times Feature usage: User 200 used 'advanced_search' 4 times Feature usage: User 200 used 'advanced_search' 5 times 👍 Regular user: User 200 regularly uses 'advanced_search'

ADDNX

Docs: Redis CF.ADDNX Documentation

Adds an item to the filter only if it does not already exist.

Full description

CF.ADDNX adds an item to a Cuckoo Filter only if the item doesn't already exist, providing atomic "add if not exists" functionality. Returns 1 if the item was added, 0 if it already existed. Perfect for Django applications implementing unique constraints, preventing duplicate entries, or managing exclusive resources without race conditions.

Syntax

CF.ADDNX key item

Parameters

  • key: The Cuckoo Filter key name.
  • item: The item to add if it doesn't exist.

Return value

Integer: 1 if item was added, 0 if it already existed.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Unique session token management
# Manage unique session tokens with atomic operations
def create_unique_session_token(user_id):
    """Create a unique session token, ensuring no duplicates"""
    import uuid
    import hashlib

    max_attempts = 5
    for attempt in range(max_attempts):
        # Generate candidate token
        raw_token = f"{user_id}:{uuid.uuid4()}:{int(time.time())}"
        token_hash = hashlib.sha256(raw_token.encode()).hexdigest()[:32]

        # Try to add uniquely to active sessions filter
        is_unique = r.execute_command("CF.ADDNX", "active_sessions", token_hash)

        if is_unique:
            # Token is unique, store session data
            session_data = {
                'user_id': user_id,
                'token': token_hash,
                'created_at': time.time(),
                'attempt': attempt + 1,
                'status': 'active'
            }

            # Store in main session storage
            r.hset(f"session:{token_hash}", mapping=session_data)
            r.expire(f"session:{token_hash}", 3600)  # 1 hour

            return {
                'success': True,
                'token': token_hash,
                'attempts_needed': attempt + 1,
                'message': f'Unique session created in {attempt + 1} attempts'
            }

    # All attempts failed
    return {
        'success': False,
        'token': None,
        'attempts_needed': max_attempts,
        'message': 'Failed to generate unique token after maximum attempts'
    }

# Create unique session
session_result = create_unique_session_token(12345)
if session_result['success']:
    print(f"✅ Session created: {session_result['token'][:16]}...")
    print(f"🎯 {session_result['message']}")
else:
    print(f"❌ {session_result['message']}")
✅ Session created: b8a9c4d2e5f8b1a7... 🎯 Unique session created in 1 attempts
Example 2: Email subscription deduplication
# Prevent duplicate email subscriptions atomically
def subscribe_email_newsletter(email, newsletter_type):
    """Subscribe email to newsletter, preventing duplicates"""

    # Normalize email
    normalized_email = email.lower().strip()
    subscription_key = f"{normalized_email}:{newsletter_type}"

    # Try to add subscription uniquely
    is_new_subscription = r.execute_command("CF.ADDNX", "email_subscriptions", subscription_key)

    if is_new_subscription:
        # New subscription, process it
        subscription_data = {
            'email': normalized_email,
            'newsletter_type': newsletter_type,
            'subscribed_at': datetime.now().isoformat(),
            'source': 'web_form',
            'status': 'active'
        }

        # Store subscription details
        r.hset(f"subscription:{subscription_key}", mapping=subscription_data)

        # Add to newsletter mailing list
        r.sadd(f"newsletter:{newsletter_type}:subscribers", normalized_email)

        return {
            'subscribed': True,
            'message': 'Successfully subscribed to newsletter',
            'email': normalized_email,
            'newsletter': newsletter_type,
            'is_duplicate': False
        }
    else:
        # Already subscribed
        return {
            'subscribed': False,
            'message': 'Already subscribed to this newsletter',
            'email': normalized_email,
            'newsletter': newsletter_type,
            'is_duplicate': True
        }

# Process subscription requests
subscriptions = [
    ('user@example.com', 'weekly_digest'),
    ('USER@EXAMPLE.COM', 'weekly_digest'),  # Same email, different case
    ('user@example.com', 'daily_news'),      # Same email, different newsletter
]

for email, newsletter in subscriptions:
    result = subscribe_email_newsletter(email, newsletter)
    status = "✅ Subscribed" if result['subscribed'] else "⚠️ Duplicate"
    print(f"{status}: {result['email']} → {result['newsletter']}")
    print(f"   {result['message']}")
✅ Subscribed: user@example.com → weekly_digest Successfully subscribed to newsletter ⚠️ Duplicate: user@example.com → weekly_digest Already subscribed to this newsletter ✅ Subscribed: user@example.com → daily_news Successfully subscribed to newsletter
Example 3: Unique job application tracking
# Track unique job applications per user-job combination
def submit_job_application(user_id, job_id, application_data):
    """Submit job application, preventing duplicate submissions"""

    application_key = f"user:{user_id}:job:{job_id}"

    # Attempt to register unique application
    is_first_application = r.execute_command("CF.ADDNX", "submitted_applications", application_key)

    if is_first_application:
        # First application for this user-job combination
        app_id = f"app_{int(time.time())}_{user_id}_{job_id}"

        application_record = {
            'application_id': app_id,
            'user_id': user_id,
            'job_id': job_id,
            'submitted_at': datetime.now().isoformat(),
            'status': 'pending_review',
            'cover_letter_length': len(application_data.get('cover_letter', '')),
            'resume_uploaded': bool(application_data.get('resume_file')),
            'is_duplicate_prevented': True
        }

        # Store application details
        r.hset(f"application:{app_id}", mapping=application_record)

        # Add to job's applicant list
        r.sadd(f"job:{job_id}:applicants", user_id)

        # Update user's application history
        r.lpush(f"user:{user_id}:applications", app_id)

        return {
            'submitted': True,
            'application_id': app_id,
            'message': 'Application submitted successfully',
            'is_duplicate': False,
            'user_id': user_id,
            'job_id': job_id
        }
    else:
        # Duplicate application attempt
        return {
            'submitted': False,
            'application_id': None,
            'message': 'You have already applied for this job',
            'is_duplicate': True,
            'user_id': user_id,
            'job_id': job_id
        }

# Process job applications
applications = [
    (101, 'job_001', {'cover_letter': 'I am very interested...', 'resume_file': True}),
    (101, 'job_001', {'cover_letter': 'Please consider me...', 'resume_file': True}),  # Duplicate
    (102, 'job_001', {'cover_letter': 'Looking forward...', 'resume_file': False}),
]

print("🎯 Job Application Processing:")
for user_id, job_id, app_data in applications:
    result = submit_job_application(user_id, job_id, app_data)
    status = "✅ Submitted" if result['submitted'] else "❌ Blocked"
    print(f"{status}: User {result['user_id']} → Job {result['job_id']}")
    if result['application_id']:
        print(f"   Application ID: {result['application_id']}")
    print(f"   {result['message']}")
🎯 Job Application Processing: ✅ Submitted: User 101 → Job job_001 Application ID: app_1703123456_101_job_001 Application submitted successfully ❌ Blocked: User 101 → Job job_001 You have already applied for this job ✅ Submitted: User 102 → Job job_001 Application ID: app_1703123457_102_job_001 Application submitted successfully
Example 4: API rate limiting with unique tokens
# Implement unique API token rate limiting
def check_api_rate_limit(api_token, endpoint, time_window='hour'):
    """Check and enforce API rate limits with unique request tracking"""

    current_hour = int(time.time() // 3600)  # Hour-based window
    rate_limit_key = f"{api_token}:{endpoint}:{time_window}:{current_hour}"

    # Try to add this specific request
    is_within_limit = r.execute_command("CF.ADDNX", "api_rate_limits", rate_limit_key)

    if is_within_limit:
        # First request in this time window for this token-endpoint
        request_data = {
            'api_token': api_token[:16] + '...',  # Truncate for security
            'endpoint': endpoint,
            'time_window': time_window,
            'hour_bucket': current_hour,
            'timestamp': time.time(),
            'allowed': True,
            'requests_in_window': 1
        }

        # Track request details
        r.hset(f"rate_limit:{rate_limit_key}", mapping=request_data)
        r.expire(f"rate_limit:{rate_limit_key}", 3600)  # Expire with hour

        # Increment counter for this token-endpoint-hour
        current_count = r.incr(f"counter:{api_token}:{endpoint}:{current_hour}")
        r.expire(f"counter:{api_token}:{endpoint}:{current_hour}", 3600)

        return {
            'allowed': True,
            'message': 'Request allowed',
            'requests_this_hour': current_count,
            'rate_limit': 1000,  # Example limit
            'time_window': f"{time_window}:{current_hour}",
            'remaining': 1000 - current_count
        }
    else:
        # Already seen this exact request in time window
        current_count = r.get(f"counter:{api_token}:{endpoint}:{current_hour}") or 0
        current_count = int(current_count)

        return {
            'allowed': False,
            'message': 'Duplicate request in time window',
            'requests_this_hour': current_count,
            'rate_limit': 1000,
            'time_window': f"{time_window}:{current_hour}",
            'remaining': max(0, 1000 - current_count),
            'duplicate_detected': True
        }

# Test API rate limiting
api_requests = [
    ('token_abc123', '/api/users'),
    ('token_abc123', '/api/users'),      # Duplicate request
    ('token_abc123', '/api/orders'),     # Different endpoint
    ('token_def456', '/api/users'),      # Different token
]

print("🛡️ API Rate Limiting Results:")
for token, endpoint in api_requests:
    result = check_api_rate_limit(token, endpoint)
    status = "✅ Allowed" if result['allowed'] else "🚫 Blocked"
    print(f"{status}: {token[:12]}... → {endpoint}")
    print(f"   {result['message']} | Requests: {result['requests_this_hour']}/{result['rate_limit']}")
    if 'duplicate_detected' in result:
        print("   ⚠️ Duplicate request detected")
🛡️ API Rate Limiting Results: ✅ Allowed: token_abc123... → /api/users Request allowed | Requests: 1/1000 🚫 Blocked: token_abc123... → /api/users Duplicate request in time window | Requests: 1/1000 ⚠️ Duplicate request detected ✅ Allowed: token_abc123... → /api/orders Request allowed | Requests: 1/1000 ✅ Allowed: token_def456... → /api/users Request allowed | Requests: 1/1000
Example 5: Unique event registration system
# Manage unique event registrations with capacity limits
def register_for_event(user_id, event_id, registration_data):
    """Register user for event, preventing duplicate registrations"""

    registration_key = f"user:{user_id}:event:{event_id}"

    # Check if user can register uniquely
    is_new_registration = r.execute_command("CF.ADDNX", "event_registrations", registration_key)

    if is_new_registration:
        # New registration, check capacity
        current_attendees = r.scard(f"event:{event_id}:attendees")
        event_capacity = int(r.hget(f"event:{event_id}:info", "capacity") or 100)

        if current_attendees < event_capacity:
            # Space available, complete registration
            registration_id = f"reg_{int(time.time())}_{user_id}_{event_id}"

            registration_record = {
                'registration_id': registration_id,
                'user_id': user_id,
                'event_id': event_id,
                'registered_at': datetime.now().isoformat(),
                'status': 'confirmed',
                'dietary_requirements': registration_data.get('dietary', 'none'),
                'emergency_contact': registration_data.get('emergency_contact', ''),
                'registration_type': registration_data.get('type', 'standard')
            }

            # Store registration
            r.hset(f"registration:{registration_id}", mapping=registration_record)

            # Add to event attendees
            r.sadd(f"event:{event_id}:attendees", user_id)

            # Update user's events
            r.sadd(f"user:{user_id}:registered_events", event_id)

            new_count = r.scard(f"event:{event_id}:attendees")

            return {
                'registered': True,
                'registration_id': registration_id,
                'message': 'Successfully registered for event',
                'event_id': event_id,
                'attendee_count': new_count,
                'capacity': event_capacity,
                'spots_remaining': event_capacity - new_count
            }
        else:
            # Event is full, remove from filter since registration failed
            r.execute_command("CF.DEL", "event_registrations", registration_key)

            return {
                'registered': False,
                'registration_id': None,
                'message': 'Event is full',
                'event_id': event_id,
                'attendee_count': current_attendees,
