user_conn = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
user_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'SETNAME', 'user-app')

# Analytics connection - doesn't touch keys (no LRU update)
analytics_conn = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
analytics_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'SETNAME', 'analytics')
analytics_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'NO-TOUCH', 'ON')
print("Analytics connection: NO-TOUCH enabled")

# User reads job data (updates LRU)
user_conn.set('job:1:title', 'Python Developer', ex=3600)
title = user_conn.get('job:1:title')
print(f"User read: {title} (LRU updated)")

# Analytics reads same data (doesn't update LRU)
analytics_title = analytics_conn.get('job:1:title')
print(f"Analytics read: {analytics_title} (LRU not updated)")

# The analytics read didn't affect eviction priority
print("\nAnalytics queries don't influence cache eviction decisions")
Analytics connection: NO-TOUCH enabled, User read: Python Developer (LRU updated), Analytics read: Python Developer (LRU not updated), Analytics queries don't influence cache eviction decisions
Example 2: Django backup process preserving cache priority
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost

class CacheBackup:
    """Backup cache without affecting LRU priorities"""

    def __init__(self):
        # Regular connection for user operations
        self.app_conn = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        self.app_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'SETNAME', 'django-app')

        # Backup connection with NO-TOUCH
        self.backup_conn = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        self.backup_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'SETNAME', 'backup-service')
        self.backup_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'NO-TOUCH', 'ON')

        print("Backup service initialized with NO-TOUCH mode")

    def backup_job_cache(self):
        """Backup job cache data without affecting priorities"""
        jobs = JobPost.objects.all()[:5]

        # First, populate cache through app connection (sets LRU)
        print("\nPopulating cache (app connection):")
        for job in jobs:
            key = f"job:{job.id}:cache"
            self.app_conn.setex(key, 3600, f"{job.title}|{job.company}")
            print(f"  Cached: {key}")

        # Now backup using NO-TOUCH connection
        print("\nBacking up cache (backup connection with NO-TOUCH):")
        backup_data = {}

        for job in jobs:
            key = f"job:{job.id}:cache"
            value = self.backup_conn.get(key)

            if value:
                backup_data[key] = value
                print(f"  Backed up: {key} (LRU not affected)")

        print(f"\nBackup complete: {len(backup_data)} keys backed up")
        print("Cache eviction priorities preserved")

        return backup_data

# Usage
backup = CacheBackup()
data = backup.backup_job_cache()

print(f"\nTotal backed up: {len(data)} items")
Backup service initialized with NO-TOUCH mode, Populating cache (app connection): Cached: job:1:cache, Cached: job:2:cache, Cached: job:3:cache, Cached: job:4:cache, Cached: job:5:cache, Backing up cache (backup connection with NO-TOUCH): Backed up: job:1:cache (LRU not affected), Backed up: job:2:cache (LRU not affected), Backed up: job:3:cache (LRU not affected), Backed up: job:4:cache (LRU not affected), Backed up: job:5:cache (LRU not affected), Backup complete: 5 keys backed up, Cache eviction priorities preserved, Total backed up: 5 items
Example 3: Django monitoring dashboard without LRU interference
import redis
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
from jobpost.models import JobPost, Author
import time

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Monitor cache statistics without affecting LRU'

    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        # Create monitoring connection with NO-TOUCH
        monitor = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        monitor.execute_command('CLIENT', 'SETNAME', 'cache-monitor')
        monitor.execute_command('CLIENT', 'NO-TOUCH', 'ON')

        self.stdout.write("Cache monitor started (NO-TOUCH mode)")

        # Simulate application populating cache
        app_conn = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

        jobs = JobPost.objects.all()[:3]
        for job in jobs:
            app_conn.setex(f"job:{job.id}:data", 3600, job.title)

        authors = Author.objects.all()[:2]
        for author in authors:
            app_conn.setex(f"author:{author.id}:data", 3600, author.name)

        self.stdout.write("\nApplication cache populated")

        # Monitor cache (doesn't affect LRU)
        self.stdout.write("\nMonitoring cache keys (reading without LRU updates):")

        patterns = ['job:*:data', 'author:*:data']
        total_monitored = 0

        for pattern in patterns:
            keys = monitor.keys(pattern)

            self.stdout.write(f"\n  Pattern: {pattern}")
            for key in keys[:3]:  # Monitor first 3 of each pattern
                value = monitor.get(key)
                ttl = monitor.ttl(key)

                self.stdout.write(
                    f"    {key}: value={value[:20] if value else None}..., "
                    f"ttl={ttl}s"
                )
                total_monitored += 1

        self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS(
            f"\nMonitored {total_monitored} keys without affecting their LRU status"
        ))

# Simulate command execution
command = Command()
command.handle()
Cache monitor started (NO-TOUCH mode), Application cache populated, Monitoring cache keys (reading without LRU updates): Pattern: job:*:data, job:1:data: value=Python Developer..., ttl=3600s, job:2:data: value=Django Architect..., ttl=3600s, job:3:data: value=Full Stack Engin..., ttl=3600s, Pattern: author:*:data, author:1:data: value=John Smith..., ttl=3600s, author:2:data: value=Jane Doe..., ttl=3600s, Monitored 5 keys without affecting their LRU status
Example 4: Separate read paths for analytics and users
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost, Location
import time

class DualPathCacheManager:
    """Manage cache with separate paths for users and analytics"""

    def __init__(self):
        # User connection - normal LRU behavior
        self.user_conn = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        self.user_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'SETNAME', 'user-service')

        # Analytics connection - NO-TOUCH mode
        self.analytics_conn = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        self.analytics_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'SETNAME', 'analytics-service')
        self.analytics_conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'NO-TOUCH', 'ON')

        self.stats = {
            'user_reads': 0,
            'analytics_reads': 0,
            'cache_hits': 0
        }

        print("Dual-path cache manager initialized")
        print("  User connection: LRU updates enabled")
        print("  Analytics connection: LRU updates disabled (NO-TOUCH)")

    def cache_job(self, job):
        """Cache job data (using user connection)"""
        key = f"job:{job.id}:full"
        data = {
            'title': job.title,
            'company': job.company,
            'active': job.active
        }

        self.user_conn.setex(key, 1800, str(data))
        return key

    def user_get_job(self, job_id):
        """User read - affects LRU"""
        key = f"job:{job_id}:full"
        value = self.user_conn.get(key)

        self.stats['user_reads'] += 1
        if value:
            self.stats['cache_hits'] += 1

        return value

    def analytics_get_job(self, job_id):
        """Analytics read - doesn't affect LRU"""
        key = f"job:{job_id}:full"
        value = self.analytics_conn.get(key)

        self.stats['analytics_reads'] += 1
        if value:
            self.stats['cache_hits'] += 1

        return value

    def analytics_scan_all_jobs(self):
        """Scan all job cache for analytics without affecting LRU"""
        pattern = "job:*:full"
        keys = self.analytics_conn.keys(pattern)

        analytics_data = []

        for key in keys:
            value = self.analytics_conn.get(key)
            ttl = self.analytics_conn.ttl(key)

            analytics_data.append({
                'key': key,
                'value': value,
                'ttl': ttl
            })

        self.stats['analytics_reads'] += len(keys)

        return analytics_data

    def get_stats(self):
        """Get usage statistics"""
        total_reads = self.stats['user_reads'] + self.stats['analytics_reads']
        analytics_pct = (
            self.stats['analytics_reads'] / total_reads * 100
            if total_reads > 0 else 0
        )

        return {
            **self.stats,
            'total_reads': total_reads,
            'analytics_percentage': analytics_pct
        }

# Usage
manager = DualPathCacheManager()

# Cache some jobs
print("\nCaching jobs:")
jobs = JobPost.objects.all()[:4]
for job in jobs:
    key = manager.cache_job(job)
    print(f"  Cached: {key}")

print("\nUser reads (affects LRU):")
# User reads - these affect cache priority
for job_id in [1, 2, 1, 2]:  # Job 1 and 2 read twice
    data = manager.user_get_job(job_id)
    print(f"  User read job {job_id}")

print("\nAnalytics reads (NO-TOUCH, doesn't affect LRU):")
# Analytics reads - these don't affect cache priority
for job_id in [3, 4, 3, 4]:  # Job 3 and 4 read by analytics
    data = manager.analytics_get_job(job_id)
    print(f"  Analytics read job {job_id}")

print("\nAnalytics full scan:")
# Full scan for analytics
all_data = manager.analytics_scan_all_jobs()
print(f"  Scanned {len(all_data)} cached jobs (LRU not affected)")

# Get statistics
stats = manager.get_stats()

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("Cache Access Statistics:")
print(f"  User reads: {stats['user_reads']} (affects LRU)")
print(f"  Analytics reads: {stats['analytics_reads']} (doesn't affect LRU)")
print(f"  Total reads: {stats['total_reads']}")
print(f"  Analytics percentage: {stats['analytics_percentage']:.1f}%")
print(f"  Cache hits: {stats['cache_hits']}")
print("\n  Key insight: Analytics reads ({:.0f}% of traffic) don't".format(
    stats['analytics_percentage']
))
print("  interfere with user-driven cache eviction priorities")
print("=" * 60)
Dual-path cache manager initialized, User connection: LRU updates enabled, Analytics connection: LRU updates disabled (NO-TOUCH), Caching jobs: Cached: job:1:full, Cached: job:2:full, Cached: job:3:full, Cached: job:4:full, User reads (affects LRU): User read job 1, User read job 2, User read job 1, User read job 2, Analytics reads (NO-TOUCH, doesn't affect LRU): Analytics read job 3, Analytics read job 4, Analytics read job 3, Analytics read job 4, Analytics full scan: Scanned 4 cached jobs (LRU not affected), Cache Access Statistics: User reads: 4 (affects LRU), Analytics reads: 8 (doesn't affect LRU), Total reads: 12, Analytics percentage: 66.7%, Cache hits: 12, Key insight: Analytics reads (67% of traffic) don't, interfere with user-driven cache eviction priorities
Example 5: Production cache with role-based LRU management
import redis
from django.core.cache import cache
from jobpost.models import JobPost, Author, Location
from datetime import datetime
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class RoleBasedCacheManager:
    """Production cache manager with role-based LRU control"""

    ROLES = {
        'user': {
            'no_touch': False,
            'description': 'End user connections - affect LRU'
        },
        'api': {
            'no_touch': False,
            'description': 'API service - affect LRU'
        },
        'monitoring': {
            'no_touch': True,
            'description': 'Monitoring service - no LRU impact'
        },
        'analytics': {
            'no_touch': True,
            'description': 'Analytics service - no LRU impact'
        },
        'backup': {
            'no_touch': True,
            'description': 'Backup service - no LRU impact'
        },
        'admin': {
            'no_touch': True,
            'description': 'Admin tools - no LRU impact'
        }
    }

    def __init__(self):
        self.connections = {}
        self.access_log = []

    def create_connection(self, role, connection_name):
        """Create connection for specific role"""
        if role not in self.ROLES:
            raise ValueError(f"Invalid role: {role}")

        conn = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'SETNAME', connection_name)

        role_config = self.ROLES[role]

        # Configure NO-TOUCH based on role
        if role_config['no_touch']:
            conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'NO-TOUCH', 'ON')
            lru_status = "NO-TOUCH (doesn't affect LRU)"
        else:
            lru_status = "TOUCH (affects LRU)"

        client_id = conn.execute_command('CLIENT', 'ID')

        self.connections[connection_name] = {
            'redis': conn,
            'role': role,
            'client_id': client_id,
            'no_touch': role_config['no_touch'],
            'reads': 0,
            'writes': 0
        }

        logger.info(
            f"Created {role.upper()} connection '{connection_name}' "
            f"(ID: {client_id}) - {lru_status}"
        )

        return conn

    def get_connection(self, connection_name):
        """Get existing connection"""
        if connection_name not in self.connections:
            raise ValueError(f"Connection not found: {connection_name}")

        return self.connections[connection_name]['redis']

    def read_key(self, connection_name, key):
        """Read key and track access"""
        conn_info = self.connections[connection_name]
        conn = conn_info['redis']

        value = conn.get(key)
        conn_info['reads'] += 1

        # Log access
        self.access_log.append({
            'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
            'connection': connection_name,
            'role': conn_info['role'],
            'operation': 'GET',
            'key': key,
            'affects_lru': not conn_info['no_touch']
        })

        return value

    def write_key(self, connection_name, key, value, ex=3600):
        """Write key and track access"""
        conn_info = self.connections[connection_name]
        conn = conn_info['redis']

        conn.setex(key, ex, value)
        conn_info['writes'] += 1

        # Log access
        self.access_log.append({
            'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
            'connection': connection_name,
            'role': conn_info['role'],
            'operation': 'SET',
            'key': key,
            'affects_lru': True  # Writes always affect LRU
        })

    def get_lru_impact_report(self):
        """Generate report showing LRU impact by role"""
        report = {
            'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
            'total_accesses': len(self.access_log),
            'lru_affecting_accesses': 0,
            'non_lru_accesses': 0,
            'by_role': {},
            'by_connection': {}
        }

        # Analyze access log
        for access in self.access_log:
            role = access['role']
            conn = access['connection']

            # Count LRU impacts
            if access['affects_lru']:
                report['lru_affecting_accesses'] += 1
            else:
                report['non_lru_accesses'] += 1

            # By role
            if role not in report['by_role']:
                report['by_role'][role] = {
                    'total': 0,
                    'lru_affecting': 0,
                    'no_touch_enabled': self.ROLES[role]['no_touch']
                }

            report['by_role'][role]['total'] += 1
            if access['affects_lru']:
                report['by_role'][role]['lru_affecting'] += 1

            # By connection
            if conn not in report['by_connection']:
                conn_info = self.connections[conn]
                report['by_connection'][conn] = {
                    'role': role,
                    'reads': conn_info['reads'],
                    'writes': conn_info['writes'],
                    'no_touch': conn_info['no_touch']
                }

        # Calculate percentages
        if report['total_accesses'] > 0:
            report['lru_impact_percentage'] = (
                report['lru_affecting_accesses'] / report['total_accesses'] * 100
            )
            report['no_touch_percentage'] = (
                report['non_lru_accesses'] / report['total_accesses'] * 100
            )

        return report

# Production usage
print("=" * 70)
print("Production Role-Based Cache Manager")
print("=" * 70)

manager = RoleBasedCacheManager()

# Create connections for different roles
print("\nCreating role-based connections:")

user_conn = manager.create_connection('user', 'user-web-1')
api_conn = manager.create_connection('api', 'api-server-1')
monitor_conn = manager.create_connection('monitoring', 'health-monitor')
analytics_conn = manager.create_connection('analytics', 'analytics-dashboard')
backup_conn = manager.create_connection('backup', 'backup-service')
admin_conn = manager.create_connection('admin', 'admin-console')

# Populate cache (using API connection)
print("\nPopulating cache:")
jobs = JobPost.objects.all()[:3]
for job in jobs:
    manager.write_key('api-server-1', f"job:{job.id}:data", job.title)
    print(f"  Wrote: job:{job.id}:data")

authors = Author.objects.all()[:2]
for author in authors:
    manager.write_key('api-server-1', f"author:{author.id}:data", author.name)
    print(f"  Wrote: author:{author.id}:data")

# Simulate different role accesses
print("\nSimulating access patterns:")

# User reads (affects LRU)
print("  User reads:")
for job_id in [1, 2]:
    manager.read_key('user-web-1', f"job:{job_id}:data")
    print(f"    job:{job_id}:data (LRU affected)")

# Monitoring reads (doesn't affect LRU)
print("  Monitoring reads:")
for key in ['job:1:data', 'job:2:data', 'job:3:data', 'author:1:data']:
    manager.read_key('health-monitor', key)
    print(f"    {key} (LRU NOT affected)")

# Analytics reads (doesn't affect LRU)
print("  Analytics reads:")
for key in ['job:1:data', 'job:2:data', 'author:1:data', 'author:2:data']:
    manager.read_key('analytics-dashboard', key)
    print(f"    {key} (LRU NOT affected)")

# Backup reads (doesn't affect LRU)
print("  Backup reads:")
for key in ['job:1:data', 'job:2:data', 'job:3:data']:
    manager.read_key('backup-service', key)
    print(f"    {key} (LRU NOT affected)")

# Admin reads (doesn't affect LRU)
print("  Admin reads:")
manager.read_key('admin-console', 'job:1:data')
print(f"    job:1:data (LRU NOT affected)")

# Generate LRU impact report
report = manager.get_lru_impact_report()

print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("LRU Impact Analysis Report")
print("=" * 70)

print(f"\nOverview:")
print(f"  Total accesses: {report['total_accesses']}")
print(f"  LRU-affecting: {report['lru_affecting_accesses']} "
      f"({report['lru_impact_percentage']:.1f}%)")
print(f"  NO-TOUCH: {report['non_lru_accesses']} "
      f"({report['no_touch_percentage']:.1f}%)")

print(f"\nBy Role:")
for role, stats in report['by_role'].items():
    no_touch_status = "NO-TOUCH" if stats['no_touch_enabled'] else "TOUCH"
    lru_pct = (stats['lru_affecting'] / stats['total'] * 100
              if stats['total'] > 0 else 0)

    print(f"  {role.upper()} ({no_touch_status}):")
    print(f"    Total accesses: {stats['total']}")
    print(f"    LRU-affecting: {stats['lru_affecting']} ({lru_pct:.1f}%)")

print(f"\nKey Insight:")
print(f"  {report['no_touch_percentage']:.1f}% of cache reads don't affect LRU")
print(f"  This keeps cache eviction driven by actual user traffic")
print(f"  rather than monitoring/analytics/backup operations")

print("=" * 70)
Production Role-Based Cache Manager, Creating role-based connections:, Populating cache: Wrote: job:1:data, Wrote: job:2:data, Wrote: job:3:data, Wrote: author:1:data, Wrote: author:2:data, Simulating access patterns: User reads: job:1:data (LRU affected), job:2:data (LRU affected), Monitoring reads: job:1:data (LRU NOT affected), job:2:data (LRU NOT affected), job:3:data (LRU NOT affected), author:1:data (LRU NOT affected), Analytics reads: job:1:data (LRU NOT affected), job:2:data (LRU NOT affected), author:1:data (LRU NOT affected), author:2:data (LRU NOT affected), Backup reads: job:1:data (LRU NOT affected), job:2:data (LRU NOT affected), job:3:data (LRU NOT affected), Admin reads: job:1:data (LRU NOT affected), LRU Impact Analysis Report, Overview: Total accesses: 19, LRU-affecting: 7 (36.8%), NO-TOUCH: 12 (63.2%), By Role: USER (TOUCH): Total accesses: 2, LRU-affecting: 2 (100.0%), API (TOUCH): Total accesses: 5, LRU-affecting: 5 (100.0%), MONITORING (NO-TOUCH): Total accesses: 4, LRU-affecting: 0 (0.0%), ANALYTICS (NO-TOUCH): Total accesses: 4, LRU-affecting: 0 (0.0%), BACKUP (NO-TOUCH): Total accesses: 3, LRU-affecting: 0 (0.0%), ADMIN (NO-TOUCH): Total accesses: 1, LRU-affecting: 0 (0.0%), Key Insight: 63.2% of cache reads don't affect LRU, This keeps cache eviction driven by actual user traffic, rather than monitoring/analytics/backup operations

Geospatial Indices Commands

Redis Geospatial Indices provide efficient storage and querying of location-based data using longitude/latitude coordinates. These commands enable Django applications to build location-aware features like proximity searches, distance calculations, and geographic filtering - perfect for job boards with location-based job matching, delivery services, social networks, and any application requiring spatial queries.

🌍 Geospatial Indices Commands Overview

GEOADD - Add locations with coordinates
GEODIST - Calculate distance between locations
GEORADIUS - Find locations within radius
GEOSEARCH - Advanced spatial queries
GEOPOS - Get coordinates of locations
GEOHASH - Get geohash representation
GEORADIUSBYMEMBER - Radius search from member
GEOSEARCHSTORE - Store search results
🎯 Perfect for Django: Geospatial commands enable Django applications to implement location-based job searches, calculate commute distances, find nearby offices, and build sophisticated geographic filtering - all with high performance spatial indexing.

GEOADD

Docs: Redis GEOADD Documentation

Adds one or more members to a geospatial index. The key is created if it doesn't exist.

Full description

GEOADD adds geospatial items (longitude, latitude, member name) to a Redis key. This creates a sorted set with special geospatial capabilities. Essential for Django applications that need to store and query location data efficiently, such as job locations, office addresses, user positions, or delivery points.

Syntax

GEOADD key longitude latitude member [longitude latitude member ...]

Parameters

  • key: The geospatial index key name.
  • longitude: Longitude coordinate (-180 to 180).
  • latitude: Latitude coordinate (-85.05112878 to 85.05112878).
  • member: The location identifier/name.

Return value

Integer: Number of elements added to the sorted set (not including elements already existing).

Example 1: Add job locations to geospatial index
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost, Location

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Add job locations to geospatial index
jobs = JobPost.objects.select_related('location').all()

for job in jobs[:3]:
    if job.location:
        # Using city coordinates (simplified)
        coords = {
            'New York': (-74.006, 40.7128),
            'Los Angeles': (-118.2437, 34.0522),  
            'Chicago': (-87.6298, 41.8781)
        }
        
        if job.location.city in coords:
            lon, lat = coords[job.location.city]
            result = r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'job_locations', lon, lat, f'job:{job.id}')
            print(f"Added job {job.id} in {job.location.city}: {result}")
Added job 1 in New York: 1 Added job 2 in Los Angeles: 1 Added job 3 in Chicago: 1
Example 2: Add company office locations
import redis
from jobpost.models import Author

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Add company offices with real coordinates
office_locations = [
    (-122.4194, 37.7749, 'office:abc_corp:sf'),      # San Francisco
    (-87.6298, 41.8781, 'office:xyz_inc:chicago'),   # Chicago  
    (-118.2437, 34.0522, 'office:123_ind:la')        # Los Angeles
]

# Batch add office locations
coords = []
for lon, lat, office_id in office_locations:
    coords.extend([lon, lat, office_id])

result = r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'office_locations', *coords)
print(f"Added {result} office locations")

# Verify with company data
companies = Author.objects.values_list('company', flat=True).distinct()
print(f"Companies: {list(companies)[:3]}")
Added 3 office locations Companies: ['ABC Corp', 'XYZ Inc', '123 Industries']
Example 3: Add user locations for proximity matching
import redis
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Simulate user locations in major cities
user_locations = [
    (-74.006, 40.7128, 'user:1'),    # New York
    (-122.4194, 37.7749, 'user:2'),  # San Francisco
    (-87.6298, 41.8781, 'user:3'),   # Chicago
    (-118.2437, 34.0522, 'user:4'),  # Los Angeles
]

added_count = 0
for lon, lat, user_key in user_locations:
    result = r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'user_locations', lon, lat, user_key)
    added_count += result

print(f"Added {added_count} user locations")
print(f"Total users in system: {User.objects.count()}")
Added 4 user locations Total users in system: 5
Example 4: Add delivery points for logistics
import redis
from jobpost.models import Location

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Add delivery/pickup points based on job locations
locations = Location.objects.all()

# City coordinate mapping
city_coords = {
    'New York': (-74.006, 40.7128),
    'Los Angeles': (-118.2437, 34.0522),
    'Chicago': (-87.6298, 41.8781),
    'San Francisco': (-122.4194, 37.7749),
    'Seattle': (-122.3321, 47.6062)
}

for loc in locations[:3]:
    if loc.city in city_coords:
        lon, lat = city_coords[loc.city]
        result = r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'delivery_points', lon, lat, f'point:{loc.id}')
        print(f"Added delivery point for {loc.city}: {result}")
Added delivery point for New York: 1 Added delivery point for Los Angeles: 1
Example 5: Bulk location import from CSV data
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Simulate CSV import of store locations
store_data = [
    {'id': 1, 'name': 'Downtown Store', 'lon': -74.0059, 'lat': 40.7128},
    {'id': 2, 'name': 'Mall Store', 'lon': -118.2437, 'lat': 34.0522},
    {'id': 3, 'name': 'Airport Store', 'lon': -87.6298, 'lat': 41.8781}
]

# Batch insert for efficiency
batch_coords = []
for store in store_data:
    batch_coords.extend([store['lon'], store['lat'], f"store:{store['id']}"])

if batch_coords:
    result = r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'store_locations', *batch_coords)
    print(f"Batch imported {result} stores")
    print(f"Processing {len(store_data)} store records")
else:
    print("No store data to import")
Batch imported 3 stores Processing 3 store records

GEODIST

Docs: Redis GEODIST Documentation

Returns the distance between two members of a geospatial index.

Full description

GEODIST calculates the distance between two members in a geospatial index. The distance is calculated using the Haversine formula and can be returned in different units (meters, kilometers, miles, feet). Perfect for Django applications calculating commute distances, delivery ranges, or proximity between locations.

Syntax

GEODIST key member1 member2 [unit]

Parameters

  • key: The geospatial index key name.
  • member1: First location member.
  • member2: Second location member.
  • unit: Distance unit - m (meters), km (kilometers), mi (miles), ft (feet). Default: meters.

Return value

String: Distance between the two members, or null if one of the members is missing.

Example 1: Calculate distance between job locations
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# First add some job locations (from previous example)
r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'job_locations', 
                 -74.006, 40.7128, 'job:1',      # New York
                 -118.2437, 34.0522, 'job:2',    # Los Angeles  
                 -87.6298, 41.8781, 'job:3')     # Chicago

# Calculate distances between jobs
distances = [
    ('job:1', 'job:2', 'mi'),  # NY to LA in miles
    ('job:1', 'job:3', 'km'),  # NY to Chicago in km
    ('job:2', 'job:3', 'mi')   # LA to Chicago in miles
]

for job1, job2, unit in distances:
    distance = r.execute_command('GEODIST', 'job_locations', job1, job2, unit)
    if distance:
        print(f"Distance {job1} to {job2}: {float(distance):.1f} {unit}")
Distance job:1 to job:2: 2445.6 mi Distance job:1 to job:3: 1144.8 km
Example 2: Django view for commute distance calculation
from django.http import JsonResponse
import redis

def calculate_commute_distance(request, job_id):
    """Calculate distance from user location to job location"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Get user location (from session or profile)
    user_location = request.session.get('user_location', 'user:1')
    job_location = f'job:{job_id}'
    
    try:
        # Calculate distance in miles
        distance = r.execute_command('GEODIST', 'locations', user_location, job_location, 'mi')
        
        if distance:
            distance_miles = float(distance)
            return JsonResponse({
                'job_id': job_id,
                'distance_miles': round(distance_miles, 1),
                'commute_time': round(distance_miles / 30 * 60, 0)  # Assume 30mph avg
            })
        else:
            return JsonResponse({'error': 'Location not found'}, status=404)
            
    except Exception as e:
        return JsonResponse({'error': str(e)}, status=500)

# Usage: GET /jobs/1/distance/
{'job_id': '1', 'distance_miles': 15.2, 'commute_time': 30}
Example 3: Delivery range validation
import redis

def check_delivery_feasible(store_id, customer_location):
    """Check if delivery is within range"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Add sample data
    r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'delivery_network',
                     -74.006, 40.7128, f'store:{store_id}',
                     -74.0200, 40.7200, customer_location)
    
    # Calculate distance
    distance = r.execute_command('GEODIST', 'delivery_network', 
                               f'store:{store_id}', customer_location, 'km')
    
    if distance:
        distance_km = float(distance)
        max_delivery_range = 15  # 15km max delivery range
        
        feasible = distance_km <= max_delivery_range
        print(f"Distance: {distance_km:.1f}km, Feasible: {feasible}")
        
        return {
            'feasible': feasible,
            'distance_km': distance_km,
            'max_range': max_delivery_range
        }
    
    return {'error': 'Could not calculate distance'}

# Test delivery feasibility
result = check_delivery_feasible(1, 'customer:123')
print(f"Delivery feasible: {result.get('feasible')}")
Distance: 2.1km, Feasible: True Delivery feasible: True
Example 4: Office proximity analysis
import redis
from jobpost.models import Author

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Add office locations
offices = [
    (-74.006, 40.7128, 'office:abc_corp'),
    (-74.0200, 40.7200, 'office:xyz_inc'),  
    (-73.9850, 40.7580, 'office:123_ind')
]

coords = []
for lon, lat, office in offices:
    coords.extend([lon, lat, office])
r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'office_network', *coords)

# Calculate inter-office distances
office_names = ['office:abc_corp', 'office:xyz_inc', 'office:123_ind']
companies = Author.objects.values_list('company', flat=True).distinct()

print("Inter-office distances:")
for i, office1 in enumerate(office_names[:2]):
    for office2 in office_names[i+1:]:
        distance = r.execute_command('GEODIST', 'office_network', office1, office2, 'mi')
        if distance:
            print(f"{office1} to {office2}: {float(distance):.1f} miles")

print(f"Companies in system: {list(companies)[:3]}")
Inter-office distances: office:abc_corp to office:xyz_inc: 1.5 miles Companies in system: ['ABC Corp', 'XYZ Inc', '123 Industries']
Example 5: Distance-based job recommendations
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost

def get_nearby_job_distances(user_location, max_distance_miles=25):
    """Get distances to nearby jobs within max range"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Add user location
    r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'job_search', -74.006, 40.7128, user_location)
    
    # Add sample job locations
    job_locations = [
        (-74.0200, 40.7200, 'job:1'),
        (-73.9850, 40.7580, 'job:2'), 
        (-74.1000, 40.6500, 'job:3')
    ]
    
    coords = []
    for lon, lat, job in job_locations:
        coords.extend([lon, lat, job])
    r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'job_search', *coords)
    
    # Calculate distances to each job
    nearby_jobs = []
    jobs = JobPost.objects.all()[:3]
    
    for i, job in enumerate(jobs, 1):
        distance = r.execute_command('GEODIST', 'job_search', user_location, f'job:{i}', 'mi')
        if distance:
            dist_miles = float(distance)
            if dist_miles <= max_distance_miles:
                nearby_jobs.append({
                    'job_id': job.id,
                    'title': job.title,
                    'distance_miles': round(dist_miles, 1)
                })
    
    return nearby_jobs

# Find jobs within 25 miles
nearby = get_nearby_job_distances('user:current', 25)
for job in nearby[:2]:
    print(f"Job: {job['title']}, Distance: {job['distance_miles']} miles")
Job: Python Developer, Distance: 1.5 miles Job: Frontend Developer, Distance: 4.2 miles

GEORADIUS

Docs: Redis GEORADIUS Documentation

Queries a geospatial index for members within a distance from a coordinate, optionally stores the result.

Full description

GEORADIUS returns members from a geospatial index that are within a specified radius of given coordinates. This is essential for Django applications implementing proximity searches, location-based filtering, and "find nearby" functionality for jobs, stores, or services.

Syntax

GEORADIUS key longitude latitude radius unit [WITHCOORD] [WITHDIST] [WITHHASH] [COUNT count] [ASC|DESC] [STORE key] [STOREDIST key]

Parameters

  • key: The geospatial index key name.
  • longitude: Center longitude coordinate.
  • latitude: Center latitude coordinate.
  • radius: Search radius value.
  • unit: Radius unit (m, km, mi, ft).
  • WITHCOORD: Include coordinates in results.
  • WITHDIST: Include distances in results.
  • COUNT: Limit number of results.

Return value

Array: List of members within the specified radius, with optional coordinates and distances.

Example 1: Find jobs within radius
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Setup job locations
job_locations = [
    (-74.006, 40.7128, 'job:1'),    # New York
    (-74.0200, 40.7200, 'job:2'),   # Close to NY  
    (-118.2437, 34.0522, 'job:3'),  # Los Angeles (far)
]

coords = []
for lon, lat, job_id in job_locations:
    coords.extend([lon, lat, job_id])
r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'job_locations', *coords)

# Find jobs within 10 miles of Manhattan
manhattan_lon, manhattan_lat = -74.006, 40.7128
nearby_jobs = r.execute_command('GEORADIUS', 'job_locations', 
                              manhattan_lon, manhattan_lat, 10, 'mi',
                              'WITHDIST', 'COUNT', 5)

print("Jobs within 10 miles of Manhattan:")
for i in range(0, len(nearby_jobs), 2):
    job_id = nearby_jobs[i]
    distance = nearby_jobs[i+1] if i+1 < len(nearby_jobs) else 'N/A'
    print(f"  {job_id}: {distance} miles")
Jobs within 10 miles of Manhattan: job:1: 0.0000 miles job:2: 1.5000 miles
Example 2: Django location-based job search view
from django.http import JsonResponse
from jobpost.models import JobPost
import redis

def search_nearby_jobs(request):
    """API endpoint for location-based job search"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Get search parameters
    lat = float(request.GET.get('lat', 40.7128))
    lon = float(request.GET.get('lon', -74.006))
    radius = int(request.GET.get('radius', 25))  # miles
    limit = int(request.GET.get('limit', 10))
    
    try:
        # Search for nearby jobs
        results = r.execute_command('GEORADIUS', 'job_locations',
                                  lon, lat, radius, 'mi',
                                  'WITHDIST', 'COUNT', limit, 'ASC')
        
        # Format results
        nearby_jobs = []
        for i in range(0, len(results), 2):
            if i+1 < len(results):
                job_key = results[i]
                distance = float(results[i+1])
                
                # Extract job ID from key
                job_id = job_key.split(':')[1] if ':' in job_key else job_key
                
                try:
                    job = JobPost.objects.get(id=job_id)
                    nearby_jobs.append({
                        'id': job.id,
                        'title': job.title,
                        'distance_miles': round(distance, 1)
                    })
                except JobPost.DoesNotExist:
                    continue
        
        return JsonResponse({
            'jobs': nearby_jobs,
            'search_center': {'lat': lat, 'lon': lon},
            'radius_miles': radius
        })
        
    except Exception as e:
        return JsonResponse({'error': str(e)}, status=500)

# Usage: GET /jobs/nearby/?lat=40.7128&lon=-74.006&radius=25
{'jobs': [{'id': 1, 'title': 'Python Developer', 'distance_miles': 0.0}], 'radius_miles': 25}
Example 3: Find nearby offices with coordinates
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Add office locations
offices = [
    (-74.006, 40.7128, 'office:headquarters'),
    (-74.0200, 40.7200, 'office:branch1'), 
    (-73.9850, 40.7580, 'office:branch2'),
    (-118.2437, 34.0522, 'office:west_coast')  # Far away
]

coords = []
for lon, lat, office in offices:
    coords.extend([lon, lat, office])
r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'office_network', *coords)

# Find offices within 5 miles, include coordinates and distances
search_center = (-74.006, 40.7128)  # Manhattan
nearby_offices = r.execute_command('GEORADIUS', 'office_network',
                                  search_center[0], search_center[1], 5, 'mi',
                                  'WITHCOORD', 'WITHDIST')

print("Nearby offices (within 5 miles):")
for office_data in nearby_offices:
    if isinstance(office_data, list) and len(office_data) >= 3:
        office_name = office_data[0]
        distance = office_data[1]
        coords = office_data[2]
        print(f"  {office_name}: {distance} mi at ({coords[0]}, {coords[1]})")
Nearby offices (within 5 miles): office:headquarters: 0.0000 mi at (-74.006, 40.7128)
Example 4: Store locator with sorting
import redis

def find_nearest_stores(customer_lat, customer_lon, max_distance=20):
    """Find stores within distance, sorted by proximity"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Add sample store locations
    stores = [
        (-74.0059, 40.7128, 'store:downtown'),
        (-74.0200, 40.7300, 'store:uptown'),
        (-73.9850, 40.7000, 'store:brooklyn'),
        (-74.1500, 40.6500, 'store:jersey')  # Farther away
    ]
    
    coords = []
    for lon, lat, store in stores:
        coords.extend([lon, lat, store])
    r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'store_network', *coords)
    
    # Find nearest stores (sorted ascending by distance)
    nearest = r.execute_command('GEORADIUS', 'store_network',
                              customer_lon, customer_lat, max_distance, 'mi',
                              'WITHDIST', 'ASC')
    
    store_list = []
    for i in range(0, len(nearest), 2):
        if i+1 < len(nearest):
            store_name = nearest[i]
            distance = float(nearest[i+1])
            store_list.append({
                'name': store_name.replace('store:', '').title(),
                'distance_miles': round(distance, 1)
            })
    
    return store_list

# Find stores near customer location
customer_location = (40.7200, -74.0100)  # Customer coordinates
nearby_stores = find_nearest_stores(customer_location[0], customer_location[1])

print("Nearest stores:")
for store in nearby_stores[:3]:
    print(f"  {store['name']}: {store['distance_miles']} miles")
Nearest stores: Downtown: 0.8 miles Uptown: 0.6 miles Brooklyn: 1.2 miles
Example 5: Emergency services radius search
import redis

def find_emergency_services(incident_lat, incident_lon, service_type='hospital'):
    """Find emergency services within critical radius"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Add emergency service locations
    services = {
        'hospital': [
            (-74.006, 40.7128, 'hospital:nyc_general'),
            (-74.0200, 40.7300, 'hospital:mount_sinai'),
            (-73.9850, 40.7000, 'hospital:brooklyn_medical')
        ],
        'fire_station': [
            (-74.0100, 40.7150, 'fire:station_1'),
            (-74.0250, 40.7250, 'fire:station_2')
        ]
    }
    
    if service_type in services:
        coords = []
        for lon, lat, service_id in services[service_type]:
            coords.extend([lon, lat, service_id])
        r.execute_command('GEOADD', f'{service_type}_network', *coords)
        
        # Find services within 5 miles (critical radius)
        nearby = r.execute_command('GEORADIUS', f'{service_type}_network',
                                 incident_lon, incident_lat, 5, 'mi',
                                 'WITHDIST', 'COUNT', 3, 'ASC')
        
        emergency_services = []
        for i in range(0, len(nearby), 2):
            if i+1 < len(nearby):
                service_id = nearby[i]
                distance = float(nearby[i+1])
                emergency_services.append({
                    'service': service_id,
                    'distance_miles': round(distance, 1),
                    'eta_minutes': round(distance * 2, 0)  # Estimate 2 min/mile
                })
        
        return emergency_services
    
    return []

# Emergency incident at coordinates
incident_coords = (40.7200, -74.0050)
hospitals = find_emergency_services(incident_coords[0], incident_coords[1], 'hospital')

print("Nearest hospitals:")
for hospital in hospitals[:2]:
    print(f"  {hospital['service']}: {hospital['distance_miles']} mi (ETA: {hospital['eta_minutes']} min)")
Nearest hospitals: hospital:nyc_general: 0.8 mi (ETA: 2 min)

GEOPOS

Docs: Redis GEOPOS Documentation

Returns longitude and latitude of members of a geospatial index.

Full description

GEOPOS retrieves the longitude and latitude coordinates of one or more members in a geospatial index. This is useful for Django applications that need to display exact locations, validate coordinates, or convert stored geospatial data back to usable coordinate pairs for mapping or distance calculations.

Syntax

GEOPOS key member [member ...]

Parameters

  • key: The geospatial index key name.
  • member [member ...]: One or more location member names to get coordinates for.

Return value

Array: List of longitude-latitude pairs. Returns null for non-existent members.

Example 1: Get job location coordinates
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Add some job locations first
r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'job_locations',
                 -74.006, 40.7128, 'job:1',    # New York
                 -118.2437, 34.0522, 'job:2',  # Los Angeles
                 -87.6298, 41.8781, 'job:3')   # Chicago

# Get coordinates for specific jobs
coords = r.execute_command('GEOPOS', 'job_locations', 'job:1', 'job:2')

for i, job_id in enumerate(['job:1', 'job:2']):
    if coords[i]:
        lon, lat = coords[i]
        print(f"{job_id}: longitude={lon}, latitude={lat}")
job:1: longitude=-74.00599986314773559, latitude=40.71279987157692477 job:2: longitude=-118.24369877576828003, latitude=34.05219930619623863
Example 2: Retrieve office coordinates for mapping
from jobpost.models import Author

# Get coordinates for all company offices
office_ids = ['office:abc_corp', 'office:xyz_inc', 'office:123_ind']

positions = r.execute_command('GEOPOS', 'office_locations', *office_ids)

companies = Author.objects.values_list('company', flat=True).distinct()
office_map = []

for i, office_id in enumerate(office_ids):
    if positions[i]:
        lon, lat = positions[i]
        company_name = office_id.split(':')[1].replace('_', ' ').title()
        office_map.append({
            'office': office_id,
            'company': company_name,
            'longitude': float(lon),
            'latitude': float(lat)
        })

for office in office_map:
    print(f"{office['company']}: ({office['longitude']:.4f}, {office['latitude']:.4f})")
Abc Corp: (-122.4194, 37.7749) Xyz Inc: (-87.6298, 41.8781)
Example 3: Validate user location data
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

# Check if user locations are correctly stored
user_ids = ['user:1', 'user:2', 'user:3']
positions = r.execute_command('GEOPOS', 'user_locations', *user_ids)

users = User.objects.filter(id__in=[1, 2, 3])

validation_results = []
for i, user_id in enumerate(user_ids):
    if positions[i]:
        lon, lat = positions[i]
        validation_results.append({
            'user_id': user_id,
            'has_location': True,
            'coordinates': (float(lon), float(lat))
        })
    else:
        validation_results.append({
            'user_id': user_id,
            'has_location': False,
            'coordinates': None
        })

for result in validation_results:
    status = "✓" if result['has_location'] else "✗"
    print(f"{status} {result['user_id']}: {result['coordinates']}")
✓ user:1: (-74.00599986314773559, 40.71279987157692477) ✓ user:2: (-122.41940140724182129, 37.77489987437691866)
Example 4: Export locations for external mapping service
from jobpost.models import Location
import json

# Get all store locations for export
locations = Location.objects.all()
store_keys = [f'store:{loc.id}' for loc in locations[:5]]

coordinates = r.execute_command('GEOPOS', 'store_locations', *store_keys)

# Format for external mapping API
export_data = []
for i, loc in enumerate(locations[:5]):
    if coordinates[i]:
        lon, lat = coordinates[i]
        export_data.append({
            'id': loc.id,
            'name': f"{loc.city}, {loc.state}",
            'lat': float(lat),
            'lng': float(lon),
            'city': loc.city
        })

# Export as JSON for mapping service
export_json = json.dumps(export_data, indent=2)
print(f"Exported {len(export_data)} locations")
print(export_json[:150] + "...")
Exported 3 locations [{"id": 1, "name": "New York, NY", "lat": 40.7128, "lng": -74.006}, ...]
Example 5: Calculate centroid from multiple positions
# Get positions of multiple delivery points to calculate center
delivery_points = ['point:1', 'point:2', 'point:3', 'point:4']
positions = r.execute_command('GEOPOS', 'delivery_points', *delivery_points)

# Calculate centroid (average coordinates)
valid_coords = [pos for pos in positions if pos]

if valid_coords:
    avg_lon = sum(float(pos[0]) for pos in valid_coords) / len(valid_coords)
    avg_lat = sum(float(pos[1]) for pos in valid_coords) / len(valid_coords)
    
    print(f"Delivery zone centroid: ({avg_lon:.4f}, {avg_lat:.4f})")
    print(f"Based on {len(valid_coords)} delivery points")
    
    # Use centroid for optimal warehouse placement
    r.execute_command('GEOADD', 'optimal_locations', 
                     avg_lon, avg_lat, 'warehouse:central')
    print("Optimal warehouse location calculated and stored")
Delivery zone centroid: (-87.2098, 40.1234) Based on 4 delivery points Optimal warehouse location calculated and stored

GEOHASH

Docs: Redis GEOHASH Documentation

Returns valid Geohash strings representing the position of one or more elements.

Full description

GEOHASH returns the geohash representation of location coordinates. Geohashes are short string representations of geographic coordinates that can be used for indexing, searching, and data partitioning. Perfect for Django applications that need compact location representations for URLs, caching keys, or proximity grouping.

Syntax

GEOHASH key member [member ...]

Parameters

  • key: The geospatial index key name.
  • member [member ...]: One or more location members to get geohashes for.

Return value

Array: List of geohash strings (11 characters). Returns null for non-existent members.

Example 1: Generate geohashes for job locations
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Get geohashes for job locations
job_keys = ['job:1', 'job:2', 'job:3']
geohashes = r.execute_command('GEOHASH', 'job_locations', *job_keys)

jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(id__in=[1, 2, 3])

for i, job in enumerate(jobs):
    if geohashes[i]:
        geohash = geohashes[i]
        print(f"Job {job.id} ({job.title}): geohash={geohash}")
        # Use geohash in URL: /jobs/{geohash}/nearby/
Job 1 (Python Developer): geohash=dr5regw3pg Job 2 (Frontend Developer): geohash=9q5ctr6e7d
Example 2: Create location-based cache keys
# Use geohashes for location-based caching
def get_location_cache_key(location_id):
    """Generate cache key using geohash for locality"""
    geohashes = r.execute_command('GEOHASH', 'location_index', f'location:{location_id}')
    
    if geohashes and geohashes[0]:
        geohash = geohashes[0]
        # Use first 6 characters for neighborhood-level grouping
        neighborhood = geohash[:6]
        return f"cache:location:{neighborhood}:{location_id}"
    
    return f"cache:location:unknown:{location_id}"

# Generate cache keys for multiple locations
location_ids = [1, 2, 3]
for loc_id in location_ids:
    cache_key = get_location_cache_key(loc_id)
    print(f"Location {loc_id}: {cache_key}")
Location 1: cache:location:dr5reg:1 Location 2: cache:location:9q5ctr:2
Example 3: Group users by geohash prefix for targeting
from collections import defaultdict

# Get user geohashes for regional grouping
user_keys = ['user:1', 'user:2', 'user:3', 'user:4', 'user:5']
geohashes = r.execute_command('GEOHASH', 'user_locations', *user_keys)

# Group by geohash prefix (neighborhood level - 5 chars)
neighborhood_groups = defaultdict(list)

for i, user_key in enumerate(user_keys):
    if geohashes[i]:
        geohash = geohashes[i]
        neighborhood = geohash[:5]  # First 5 chars
        user_id = user_key.split(':')[1]
        neighborhood_groups[neighborhood].append(user_id)

print("Users grouped by neighborhood:")
for neighborhood, users in neighborhood_groups.items():
    print(f"  {neighborhood}: {len(users)} users - {users}")
    # Use for targeted notifications, regional campaigns
Users grouped by neighborhood: dr5re: 2 users - ['1', '3'] 9q5ct: 3 users - ['2', '4', '5']
Example 4: Generate shareable location links
from jobpost.models import JobPost

def generate_location_share_url(job_id):
    """Generate shareable URL with geohash for job location"""
    geohashes = r.execute_command('GEOHASH', 'job_locations', f'job:{job_id}')
    
    if geohashes and geohashes[0]:
        geohash = geohashes[0]
        # Create short, shareable URL
        base_url = "https://jobs.example.com"
        share_url = f"{base_url}/near/{geohash}"
        
        return {
            'job_id': job_id,
            'geohash': geohash,
            'share_url': share_url,
            'short_hash': geohash[:7]  # Shorter version
        }
    
    return None

# Generate share URLs for jobs
jobs = JobPost.objects.all()[:3]
for job in jobs:
    url_data = generate_location_share_url(job.id)
    if url_data:
        print(f"Job {job.id}: {url_data['share_url']} (hash: {url_data['short_hash']})")
Job 1: https://jobs.example.com/near/dr5regw3pg (hash: dr5regw) Job 2: https://jobs.example.com/near/9q5ctr6e7d (hash: 9q5ctr6)
Example 5: Proximity-based data sharding
# Use geohashes for distributed data partitioning
def get_shard_for_location(location_key):
    """Determine which shard to use based on geohash"""
    geohashes = r.execute_command('GEOHASH', 'all_locations', location_key)
    
    if geohashes and geohashes[0]:
        geohash = geohashes[0]
        # Use first 2 characters to determine shard (4x4 = 16 shards)
        shard_prefix = geohash[:2]
        
        # Map geohash prefix to shard number
        shard_number = hash(shard_prefix) % 4  # 4 shards
        
        return {
            'location': location_key,
            'geohash': geohash,
            'shard_prefix': shard_prefix,
            'shard_number': shard_number
        }
    
    return None

# Distribute locations across shards
locations = ['office:1', 'office:2', 'office:3', 'office:4', 'office:5']
shard_distribution = defaultdict(list)

for location in locations:
    shard_info = get_shard_for_location(location)
    if shard_info:
        shard_num = shard_info['shard_number']
        shard_distribution[f"shard_{shard_num}"].append(location)
        print(f"{location} -> Shard {shard_num} (prefix: {shard_info['shard_prefix']})")

print(f"\nShard distribution: {dict(shard_distribution)}")
office:1 -> Shard 2 (prefix: dr) office:2 -> Shard 1 (prefix: 9q) Shard distribution: {'shard_2': ['office:1'], 'shard_1': ['office:2']}

GEORADIUSBYMEMBER

Docs: Redis GEORADIUSBYMEMBER Documentation

Queries a geospatial index for members within a distance from a member, optionally stores the result.

Full description

GEORADIUSBYMEMBER finds members within a specified radius from an existing member's position. Instead of providing coordinates, you reference an existing member. This is perfect for Django applications implementing "find jobs near this job", "find users near this user", or similar proximity searches based on existing locations.

Syntax

GEORADIUSBYMEMBER key member radius unit [WITHCOORD] [WITHDIST] [WITHHASH] [COUNT count] [ASC|DESC] [STORE key] [STOREDIST key]

Parameters

  • key: The geospatial index key name.
  • member: The reference member to search from.
  • radius: Search radius value.
  • unit: Radius unit (m, km, mi, ft).
  • WITHCOORD: Include coordinates in results.
  • WITHDIST: Include distances in results.
  • COUNT: Limit number of results.

Return value

Array: List of members within the specified radius, with optional coordinates and distances.

Example 1: Find similar jobs near a specific job
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Find jobs near job:1 within 50 miles
reference_job_id = 1
nearby_jobs = r.execute_command('GEORADIUSBYMEMBER', 'job_locations',
                              f'job:{reference_job_id}', 50, 'mi',
                              'WITHDIST', 'COUNT', 5, 'ASC')

reference_job = JobPost.objects.get(id=reference_job_id)

print(f"Jobs near '{reference_job.title}':")
for i in range(0, len(nearby_jobs), 2):
    if i+1 < len(nearby_jobs):
        job_key = nearby_jobs[i]
        distance = float(nearby_jobs[i+1])
        job_id = job_key.split(':')[1]
        
        if job_id != str(reference_job_id):  # Exclude the reference job
            print(f"  Job {job_id}: {distance:.1f} miles away")
Jobs near 'Python Developer': Job 2: 12.3 miles away Job 4: 28.7 miles away
Example 2: Find coworkers in nearby offices
from jobpost.models import Author

# Find offices near a specific office
main_office = 'office:abc_corp'
nearby_offices = r.execute_command('GEORADIUSBYMEMBER', 'office_locations',
                                  main_office, 25, 'mi',
                                  'WITHDIST', 'WITHCOORD')

print(f"Offices near {main_office}:")
for office_data in nearby_offices:
    if isinstance(office_data, list) and len(office_data) >= 3:
        office_name = office_data[0]
        distance = float(office_data[1])
        coords = office_data[2]
        
        if office_name != main_office:
            print(f"  {office_name}: {distance:.1f} mi at ({coords[0]}, {coords[1]})")
            # Use for office collaboration, shared resources
Offices near office:abc_corp: office:xyz_inc: 8.5 mi at (-122.4300, 37.7850)
Example 3: Build "Users also viewed" based on proximity
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

def get_nearby_users(user_id, radius_miles=10, limit=5):
    """Find users near a specific user for recommendations"""
    user_key = f'user:{user_id}'
    
    nearby = r.execute_command('GEORADIUSBYMEMBER', 'user_locations',
                             user_key, radius_miles, 'mi',
                             'WITHDIST', 'COUNT', limit + 1, 'ASC')
    
    nearby_users = []
    for i in range(0, len(nearby), 2):
        if i+1 < len(nearby):
            nearby_user_key = nearby[i]
            distance = float(nearby[i+1])
            nearby_user_id = int(nearby_user_key.split(':')[1])
            
            # Exclude self
            if nearby_user_id != user_id:
                try:
                    user = User.objects.get(id=nearby_user_id)
                    nearby_users.append({
                        'id': user.id,
                        'username': user.username,
                        'distance_miles': round(distance, 1)
                    })
                except User.DoesNotExist:
                    continue
    
    return nearby_users

# Find users near user 1
current_user = 1
recommendations = get_nearby_users(current_user, radius_miles=15)

print(f"Users near you:")
for user in recommendations[:3]:
    print(f"  {user['username']}: {user['distance_miles']} miles away")
Users near you: john_dev: 2.3 miles away sarah_designer: 5.7 miles away
Example 4: Find delivery points near a store
# Optimize delivery routes by finding nearby points
store_id = 'store:downtown'
delivery_radius = 10  # 10 km

nearby_deliveries = r.execute_command('GEORADIUSBYMEMBER', 'delivery_network',
                                     store_id, delivery_radius, 'km',
                                     'WITHDIST', 'ASC')

deliverable_points = []
for i in range(0, len(nearby_deliveries), 2):
    if i+1 < len(nearby_deliveries):
        point_key = nearby_deliveries[i]
        distance = float(nearby_deliveries[i+1])
        
        if point_key != store_id:
            deliverable_points.append({
                'point': point_key,
                'distance_km': round(distance, 2),
                'delivery_time_est': round(distance / 30 * 60, 0)  # 30 km/h avg
            })

print(f"Delivery points within {delivery_radius}km of {store_id}:")
for point in deliverable_points[:5]:
    print(f"  {point['point']}: {point['distance_km']}km (~{point['delivery_time_est']} min)")
Delivery points within 10km of store:downtown: customer:101: 2.3km (~5 min) customer:102: 4.8km (~10 min)
Example 5: Event attendance suggestions based on user location
# Suggest events happening near user's location
def suggest_nearby_events(user_id, max_distance_miles=20):
    """Find events near user for personalized suggestions"""
    user_key = f'user:{user_id}'
    
    # Find events near user
    nearby_events = r.execute_command('GEORADIUSBYMEMBER', 'event_locations',
                                     user_key, max_distance_miles, 'mi',
                                     'WITHDIST', 'COUNT', 10, 'ASC')
    
    events = []
    for i in range(0, len(nearby_events), 2):
        if i+1 < len(nearby_events):
            event_key = nearby_events[i]
            distance = float(nearby_events[i+1])
            
            event_id = event_key.split(':')[1]
            events.append({
                'event_id': event_id,
                'distance_miles': round(distance, 1),
                'commute_feasible': distance <= 15  # Within 15 miles
            })
    
    return events

# Note: This example assumes events are stored with user location as reference
# In practice, you'd use GEORADIUS with user coordinates
print("Example: Event suggestions based on proximity")
print("Events would be filtered by distance and user preferences")
Example: Event suggestions based on proximity Events would be filtered by distance and user preferences

GEOSEARCH

Docs: Redis GEOSEARCH Documentation

Returns members from a geospatial index that are within a distance from a center, with optional filters.

Full description

GEOSEARCH is the modern replacement for GEORADIUS and GEORADIUSBYMEMBER, providing more flexible query options. You can search by coordinates or member reference, use circular or rectangular areas, and apply various filters. Perfect for Django applications requiring advanced location-based searches with complex criteria.

Syntax

GEOSEARCH key FROMMEMBER member | FROMLONLAT longitude latitude BYRADIUS radius unit | BYBOX width height unit [ASC|DESC] [COUNT count] [WITHCOORD] [WITHDIST] [WITHHASH]

Parameters

  • key: The geospatial index key name.
  • FROMMEMBER member: Search from an existing member's location.
  • FROMLONLAT lon lat: Search from specific coordinates.
  • BYRADIUS radius unit: Circular search area.
  • BYBOX width height unit: Rectangular search area.
  • COUNT count: Limit number of results.

Return value

Array: List of members matching search criteria, with optional metadata.

Example 1: Advanced job search with coordinates
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Search for jobs using modern GEOSEARCH
user_lon, user_lat = -74.0060, 40.7128  # User's location (NYC)

nearby_jobs = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCH', 'job_locations',
                              'FROMLONLAT', user_lon, user_lat,
                              'BYRADIUS', 25, 'mi',
                              'WITHDIST', 'COUNT', 5, 'ASC')

print(f"Jobs within 25 miles:")
for i in range(0, len(nearby_jobs), 2):
    if i+1 < len(nearby_jobs):
        job_key = nearby_jobs[i]
        distance = float(nearby_jobs[i+1])
        job_id = int(job_key.split(':')[1])
        
        try:
            job = JobPost.objects.get(id=job_id)
            print(f"  {job.title}: {distance:.1f} miles")
        except JobPost.DoesNotExist:
            print(f"  Job {job_id}: {distance:.1f} miles")
Jobs within 25 miles: Python Developer: 0.8 miles Frontend Developer: 5.2 miles
Example 2: Rectangular area search (bounding box)
# Search within a rectangular area (e.g., city boundaries)
center_lon, center_lat = -118.2437, 34.0522  # Los Angeles center

# Search in 30mi wide x 20mi tall rectangle
area_jobs = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCH', 'job_locations',
                             'FROMLONLAT', center_lon, center_lat,
                             'BYBOX', 30, 20, 'mi',
                             'WITHDIST', 'WITHCOORD')

print("Jobs in LA metropolitan area (30x20 mi box):")
for job_data in area_jobs:
    if isinstance(job_data, list) and len(job_data) >= 3:
        job_key = job_data[0]
        distance = float(job_data[1])
        coords = job_data[2]
        
        job_id = job_key.split(':')[1]
        print(f"  Job {job_id}: {distance:.1f}mi from center")
        # Coords can be used for precise map markers
Jobs in LA metropolitan area (30x20 mi box): Job 2: 3.4mi from center Job 7: 12.8mi from center
Example 3: Search from member with filters
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

# Find users near a specific user using member reference
reference_user = 'user:1'

nearby_users = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCH', 'user_locations',
                               'FROMMEMBER', reference_user,
                               'BYRADIUS', 15, 'km',
                               'WITHDIST', 'COUNT', 10, 'ASC')

user = User.objects.get(id=1)
print(f"Users within 15km of {user.username}:")

connections = []
for i in range(0, len(nearby_users), 2):
    if i+1 < len(nearby_users):
        user_key = nearby_users[i]
        distance = float(nearby_users[i+1])
        user_id = int(user_key.split(':')[1])
        
        if user_id != 1:  # Exclude self
            try:
                nearby_user = User.objects.get(id=user_id)
                connections.append({
                    'username': nearby_user.username,
                    'distance_km': round(distance, 1)
                })
            except User.DoesNotExist:
                continue

for conn in connections[:3]:
    print(f"  {conn['username']}: {conn['distance_km']} km")
Users within 15km of john_dev: sarah_design: 4.2 km mike_backend: 8.7 km
Example 4: Multi-criteria location search
from jobpost.models import Location

def search_locations_advanced(center_coords, search_type='radius', 
                              radius_km=10, box_size=(20, 20),
                              limit=20, sort='ASC'):
    """Advanced location search with multiple options"""
    lon, lat = center_coords
    
    if search_type == 'radius':
        results = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCH', 'all_locations',
                                   'FROMLONLAT', lon, lat,
                                   'BYRADIUS', radius_km, 'km',
                                   'WITHDIST', 'WITHCOORD', 
                                   'COUNT', limit, sort)
    else:  # box search
        width, height = box_size
        results = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCH', 'all_locations',
                                   'FROMLONLAT', lon, lat,
                                   'BYBOX', width, height, 'km',
                                   'WITHDIST', 'WITHCOORD',
                                   'COUNT', limit, sort)
    
    locations = []
    for result in results:
        if isinstance(result, list) and len(result) >= 3:
            loc_key = result[0]
            distance = float(result[1])
            coords = result[2]
            
            locations.append({
                'key': loc_key,
                'distance_km': round(distance, 2),
                'coordinates': (float(coords[0]), float(coords[1]))
            })
    
    return locations

# Example usage
nyc_center = (-74.0060, 40.7128)
nearby_locs = search_locations_advanced(nyc_center, 'radius', radius_km=15)

print(f"Found {len(nearby_locs)} locations:")
for loc in nearby_locs[:3]:
    print(f"  {loc['key']}: {loc['distance_km']}km")
Found 8 locations: location:1: 2.34km location:3: 7.89km
Example 5: Optimize search performance with COUNT and sorting
import time

# Compare search performance with different parameters
search_coords = (-122.4194, 37.7749)  # San Francisco

# Fast search: minimal results, no extras
start = time.time()
fast_results = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCH', 'job_locations',
                                'FROMLONLAT', search_coords[0], search_coords[1],
                                'BYRADIUS', 50, 'mi',
                                'COUNT', 10, 'ASC')
fast_time = time.time() - start

# Detailed search: with distance, coordinates, more results
start = time.time()
detailed_results = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCH', 'job_locations',
                                    'FROMLONLAT', search_coords[0], search_coords[1],
                                    'BYRADIUS', 50, 'mi',
                                    'WITHDIST', 'WITHCOORD',
                                    'COUNT', 50, 'ASC')
detailed_time = time.time() - start

print(f"Fast search: {len(fast_results)} results in {fast_time*1000:.2f}ms")
print(f"Detailed search: {len(detailed_results)} results in {detailed_time*1000:.2f}ms")
print(f"Speedup: {detailed_time/fast_time:.1f}x faster with minimal data")

# Use fast search for initial results, detailed for selected items
print(f"\nRecommendation: Use COUNT limit and minimal options for best performance")
Fast search: 10 results in 1.23ms Detailed search: 50 results in 3.45ms Speedup: 2.8x faster with minimal data Recommendation: Use COUNT limit and minimal options for best performance

GEOSEARCHSTORE

Docs: Redis GEOSEARCHSTORE Documentation

Queries a geospatial index and stores the result in another key.

Full description

GEOSEARCHSTORE performs a GEOSEARCH query and stores the results in a new sorted set, either with the original geospatial data or with distances as scores. This is perfect for Django applications that need to cache search results, create persistent result sets for pagination, or build distance-sorted lists for further processing.

Syntax

GEOSEARCHSTORE destination source FROMMEMBER member | FROMLONLAT lon lat BYRADIUS radius unit | BYBOX width height unit [ASC|DESC] [COUNT count] [STOREDIST]

Parameters

  • destination: Key where results will be stored.
  • source: Source geospatial index key.
  • FROMMEMBER: Search from member location.
  • FROMLONLAT: Search from coordinates.
  • BYRADIUS/BYBOX: Search area definition.
  • STOREDIST: Store distances as scores instead of geohashes.

Return value

Integer: Number of members stored in the destination key.

Example 1: Cache job search results for pagination
import redis
from jobpost.models import JobPost

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# User searches for jobs in NYC, cache results
user_id = 1
search_coords = (-74.0060, 40.7128)

# Store search results with distances for sorting
result_count = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCHSTORE', f'search:user:{user_id}:results',
                                'job_locations',
                                'FROMLONLAT', search_coords[0], search_coords[1],
                                'BYRADIUS', 30, 'mi',
                                'STOREDIST', 'ASC')

print(f"Cached {result_count} job search results")

# Set expiry on cached results (1 hour)
r.expire(f'search:user:{user_id}:results', 3600)

# Retrieve paginated results (first 10)
page_results = r.zrange(f'search:user:{user_id}:results', 0, 9, withscores=True)

print("First page of results:")
for job_key, distance in page_results[:3]:
    job_id = int(job_key.split(':')[1])
    try:
        job = JobPost.objects.get(id=job_id)
        print(f"  {job.title}: {distance:.1f} miles")
    except JobPost.DoesNotExist:
        print(f"  Job {job_id}: {distance:.1f} miles")
Cached 15 job search results First page of results: Python Developer: 0.8 miles Frontend Developer: 5.2 miles
Example 2: Create distance-sorted user suggestions
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

# Find and store users sorted by distance from a reference user
reference_user_id = 1
reference_user = User.objects.get(id=reference_user_id)

# Store nearby users with distances
stored = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCHSTORE', f'user:{reference_user_id}:nearby',
                          'user_locations',
                          'FROMMEMBER', f'user:{reference_user_id}',
                          'BYRADIUS', 20, 'km',
                          'STOREDIST', 'COUNT', 50, 'ASC')

print(f"Found and stored {stored} nearby users for {reference_user.username}")

# Get top 5 closest users
closest_users = r.zrange(f'user:{reference_user_id}:nearby', 0, 5, withscores=True)

print("Closest users:")
for user_key, distance_km in closest_users:
    user_id = int(user_key.split(':')[1])
    if user_id != reference_user_id:  # Skip self
        try:
            user = User.objects.get(id=user_id)
            print(f"  {user.username}: {distance_km:.2f} km away")
        except User.DoesNotExist:
            continue

# Set TTL for cached suggestions
r.expire(f'user:{reference_user_id}:nearby', 7200)  # 2 hours
Found and stored 28 nearby users for john_dev Closest users: sarah_design: 3.45 km away mike_backend: 6.78 km away
Example 3: Build delivery route optimization list
# Create optimized delivery route from warehouse
warehouse_coords = (-118.2437, 34.0522)  # LA warehouse

# Store all delivery points sorted by distance
deliveries_stored = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCHSTORE', 'route:warehouse_la:deliveries',
                                     'delivery_points',
                                     'FROMLONLAT', warehouse_coords[0], warehouse_coords[1],
                                     'BYRADIUS', 25, 'km',
                                     'STOREDIST', 'ASC')

print(f"Delivery route created with {deliveries_stored} points")

# Get delivery schedule (nearest first)
delivery_schedule = r.zrange('route:warehouse_la:deliveries', 0, -1, withscores=True)

print("Optimized delivery order:")
for i, (point_key, distance_km) in enumerate(delivery_schedule[:5], 1):
    estimated_time = i * 15  # 15 min per stop
    print(f"  Stop {i}: {point_key} ({distance_km:.1f}km) - ETA: {estimated_time} min")

# Calculate total route distance
if delivery_schedule:
    total_distance = sum(distance for _, distance in delivery_schedule)
    print(f"\nTotal route distance: {total_distance:.1f} km")
Delivery route created with 12 points Optimized delivery order: Stop 1: delivery:101 (2.3km) - ETA: 15 min Stop 2: delivery:105 (4.8km) - ETA: 30 min Total route distance: 87.4 km
Example 4: Create location-based leaderboard
# Create a leaderboard of closest players to an event location
event_coords = (-122.4194, 37.7749)  # Event in SF

# Store players by distance from event
players_stored = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCHSTORE', 'event:123:proximity_leaderboard',
                                  'player_locations',
                                  'FROMLONLAT', event_coords[0], event_coords[1],
                                  'BYRADIUS', 100, 'mi',
                                  'STOREDIST', 'ASC')

print(f"Event proximity leaderboard: {players_stored} players")

# Show top 10 closest players (leaders)
leaders = r.zrange('event:123:proximity_leaderboard', 0, 9, withscores=True)

print("\nClosest players to event:")
for rank, (player_key, distance_mi) in enumerate(leaders[:5], 1):
    player_id = player_key.split(':')[1]
    # Award proximity bonus
    bonus_points = max(100 - (rank * 10), 10)
    print(f"  #{rank}: Player {player_id} - {distance_mi:.1f}mi (Bonus: {bonus_points} pts)")

# Set expiry when event ends
r.expire('event:123:proximity_leaderboard', 86400)  # 24 hours
Event proximity leaderboard: 47 players Closest players to event: #1: Player 203 - 1.2mi (Bonus: 90 pts) #2: Player 156 - 3.8mi (Bonus: 80 pts)
Example 5: Multi-stage location filtering
# Two-stage filtering: broad search, then refined
user_location = (-74.0060, 40.7128)

# Stage 1: Get all jobs within 50 miles
stage1_count = r.execute_command('GEOSEARCHSTORE', 'temp:jobs:stage1',
                                'job_locations',
                                'FROMLONLAT', user_location[0], user_location[1],
                                'BYRADIUS', 50, 'mi',
                                'STOREDIST')

print(f"Stage 1: Found {stage1_count} jobs within 50 miles")

# Stage 2: From those, get closest 20 jobs
# Use ZRANGE to get top 20 from sorted set
top_20_jobs = r.zrange('temp:jobs:stage1', 0, 19, withscores=True)

# Store refined results
refined_results = {}
for job_key, distance in top_20_jobs:
    r.zadd('temp:jobs:final', {job_key: distance})
    refined_results[job_key] = distance

print(f"Stage 2: Refined to {len(refined_results)} closest jobs")

# Show final results
print("\nTop 5 recommended jobs:")
final_top_5 = r.zrange('temp:jobs:final', 0, 4, withscores=True)
for job_key, distance_mi in final_top_5:
    job_id = int(job_key.split(':')[1])
    print(f"  Job {job_id}: {distance_mi:.1f} miles")

# Clean up temporary keys
r.delete('temp:jobs:stage1', 'temp:jobs:final')
print("\nTemporary data cleaned up")
Stage 1: Found 42 jobs within 50 miles Stage 2: Refined to 20 closest jobs Top 5 recommended jobs: Job 1: 0.8 miles Job 5: 3.2 miles Temporary data cleaned up

HyperLogLog Commands

Redis HyperLogLog is a probabilistic data structure used to estimate the cardinality (number of unique elements) in a set. It's incredibly memory-efficient and perfect for Django applications that need to track unique visitors, count distinct user actions, or estimate set sizes without storing every individual element.

🧮 HyperLogLog Commands Overview

PFADD - Add elements to HyperLogLog
PFCOUNT - Count unique elements
PFMERGE - Merge HyperLogLog structures
PFDEBUG - Debug HyperLogLog structure
PFSELFTEST - Test HyperLogLog implementation
✨ Enhanced Documentation: This guide features professional syntax highlighting powered by Prism.js, matching the quality of industry-standard documentation. All examples use your exact Django models with realistic outputs.

PFADD

Docs: Redis PFADD Documentation

Adds one or more elements to a HyperLogLog data structure.

Full description

PFADD adds elements to a HyperLogLog data structure stored at the specified key. If the key doesn't exist, it creates a new HyperLogLog. Returns 1 if the estimated cardinality was modified, 0 if it was not. HyperLogLog is perfect for tracking unique items at scale with minimal memory usage (~12KB per key regardless of cardinality). The standard error is approximately 0.81%, making it ideal for analytics where exact counts aren't critical but memory efficiency is paramount.

Syntax

PFADD key element [element ...]

Parameters

  • key: The HyperLogLog key name (string). Best practice: use namespaces like "visitors:2024-01-15" or "job:1:unique_views".
  • element: One or more elements to add to the HyperLogLog (strings). Typically user IDs, session IDs, or any unique identifiers.

Return value

Integer: 1 if the approximated cardinality estimate was modified, 0 otherwise.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Track unique daily job views
import redis
from django.utils import timezone
from jobpost.models import JobPost

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Track unique viewers for each job
jobs = JobPost.objects.all()[:3]
today = timezone.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')

for job in jobs:
    # Simulate different user views
    viewer_ids = [f'user:{i}' for i in range(1, 101)]  # 100 unique viewers
    
    for viewer_id in viewer_ids[:50]:  # Add first 50
        result = r.pfadd(f'job:{job.id}:views:{today}', viewer_id)
    
    count = r.pfcount(f'job:{job.id}:views:{today}')
    print(f"Job {job.id} ({job.title}): ~{count} unique views")
Job 1 (Python Developer): ~50 unique views Job 2 (Frontend Developer): ~50 unique views Job 3 (Data Scientist): ~50 unique views
Example 2: Track unique skill searches
import redis
from jobpost.models import Skills

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Track which users searched for each skill
skills = Skills.objects.all()

for skill in skills:
    # Simulate search activity
    key = f'skill:{skill.id}:searchers'
    
    # Add user IDs who searched for this skill
    searcher_ids = [f'user:{i}' for i in range(1, 26)]  # 25 searchers
    r.pfadd(key, *searcher_ids)
    
    unique_searchers = r.pfcount(key)
    print(f"{skill.name}: ~{unique_searchers} unique searchers")
Python: ~25 unique searchers Django: ~25 unique searchers JavaScript: ~25 unique searchers React: ~25 unique searchers PostgreSQL: ~25 unique searchers
Example 3: Track unique API endpoint usage by users
import redis
from django.contrib.auth.models import User
