Found 15 Python Django jobs Job: Senior Python Developer at TechCorp Salary: $120000 | Location: San Francisco Skills: Python,Django,Redis,PostgreSQL --- Job: Django Backend Engineer at StartupXYZ Salary: $110000 | Location: Remote Skills: Python,Django,AWS,Docker ---
Example 2: Complex boolean search with highlighting
# Advanced search with boolean operators and highlighting
advanced_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "(python | javascript) (senior | lead) -intern",  # Boolean logic
    "HIGHLIGHT", 
    "FIELDS", "2", "title", "description",
    "TAGS", "", "",
    "SORTBY", "posted_date", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "5"
]

results = r.execute_command(*advanced_search)
print(f"Advanced search found {results[0]} results")

for i in range(1, len(results), 2):
    doc_id = results[i] 
    fields = results[i + 1]
    
    field_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(fields), 2):
        field_dict[fields[j]] = fields[j + 1]
    
    print(f"\nPosition: {field_dict.get('title', '')}")
    print(f"Description: {field_dict.get('description', '')[:100]}...")
    
    # Show highlighting if present
    if 'title' in field_dict and '' in field_dict['title']:
        print(f"Highlighted: {field_dict['title']}")
Advanced search found 8 results Position: Senior Python Developer Description: Join our team to build scalable Django applications with Redis integration... Highlighted: Senior Python Developer Position: Lead JavaScript Engineer Description: Looking for experienced frontend leader to guide our React and Node.js team...
Example 3: Geographic search for nearby users
# Find users within geographic radius
geo_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "user_profile_idx",
    "@location:[-122.4194 37.7749 10 km]",  # Within 10km of SF
    "FILTER", "experience_years", "3", "10",  # 3-10 years experience
    "SORTBY", "last_active", "DESC",  # Most recently active first
    "LIMIT", "0", "5",
    "RETURN", "4", "username", "skills", "experience_years", "location"
]

results = r.execute_command(*geo_search)
print(f"Found {results[0]} nearby experienced users")

for i in range(1, len(results), 2):
    fields = results[i + 1]
    field_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(fields), 2):
        field_dict[fields[j]] = fields[j + 1]
    
    print(f"User: {field_dict.get('username')}")
    print(f"  Experience: {field_dict.get('experience_years')} years")
    print(f"  Skills: {field_dict.get('skills')}")
    print(f"  Location: {field_dict.get('location')}")
    print("---")
Found 12 nearby experienced users User: johndev Experience: 5 years Skills: Python,Django,JavaScript,Redis,AWS Location: -122.4194,37.7749 --- User: sarahcode Experience: 7 years Skills: React,Node.js,Python,MongoDB Location: -122.4094,37.7849 ---
Example 4: Fuzzy search with spell tolerance
# Fuzzy search to handle typos and variations
fuzzy_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx", 
    "%pythom% %djago%",  # Fuzzy matching for "python" and "django"
    "SORTBY", "salary", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "3",
    "RETURN", "3", "title", "description", "skills"
]

results = r.execute_command(*fuzzy_search)
print(f"Fuzzy search found {results[0]} results")

# Also try exact phrase search
phrase_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    '"full stack developer"',  # Exact phrase
    "SORTBY", "posted_date", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "3"
]

phrase_results = r.execute_command(*phrase_search)
print(f"Phrase search found {phrase_results[0]} results")

# Demonstrate wildcard search
wildcard_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "develop*",  # Matches developer, development, etc.
    "FILTER", "type", "Full Time", "Full Time",
    "LIMIT", "0", "5"
]

wildcard_results = r.execute_command(*wildcard_search)
print(f"Wildcard search found {wildcard_results[0]} results")
Fuzzy search found 18 results Phrase search found 6 results Wildcard search found 23 results
Example 5: Multi-field search with custom scoring
# Search across multiple fields with different weights
weighted_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "@title:(senior python) | @description:(django redis) | @skills:(aws cloud)",
    "WITHSCORES",  # Include relevance scores
    "SORTBY", "_score", "DESC",  # Sort by relevance
    "LIMIT", "0", "5",
    "RETURN", "5", "title", "company", "description", "skills", "salary"
]

results = r.execute_command(*weighted_search)
print(f"Weighted search found {results[0]} results")

# Process results with scores
for i in range(1, len(results), 3):  # Every 3 elements: doc_id, score, fields
    doc_id = results[i]
    score = results[i + 1]
    fields = results[i + 2]
    
    field_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(fields), 2):
        field_dict[fields[j]] = fields[j + 1]
    
    print(f"\nScore: {score}")
    print(f"Job: {field_dict.get('title')} at {field_dict.get('company')}")
    print(f"Skills match: {field_dict.get('skills')}")

# Tag-based filtering for precise matches
tag_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "@type:{Full Time} @status:{active}",  # Tag filters
    "SORTBY", "salary", "DESC", 
    "LIMIT", "0", "10"
]

tag_results = r.execute_command(*tag_search)
print(f"\nTag-filtered search: {tag_results[0]} active full-time jobs")
Weighted search found 12 results Score: 2.8 Job: Senior Python Developer at TechCorp Skills match: Python,Django,Redis,PostgreSQL Score: 2.1 Job: Django Backend Engineer at StartupXYZ Skills match: Python,Django,AWS,Docker Tag-filtered search: 28 active full-time jobs

FT.AGGREGATE

Docs: Redis FT.AGGREGATE Documentation

Run a search query on an index and perform aggregate transformations on the results.

Full description

FT.AGGREGATE performs complex analytics and aggregations on search results, similar to SQL GROUP BY operations. Supports grouping, counting, summing, averaging, sorting, and data transformations. Perfect for Django analytics dashboards, reporting, and business intelligence without additional tools.

Syntax

FT.AGGREGATE index_name query [options...] GROUPBY fields REDUCE functions...

Parameters

  • index_name: Name of the index to aggregate (string).
  • query: Search query to filter data.
  • GROUPBY: Fields to group results by.
  • REDUCE: Aggregation functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, etc.).

Return value

Array: Aggregated results with computed values.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Job market analytics by location
# Aggregate job statistics by location
location_analytics = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "*",  # All jobs
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@location",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "job_count",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_salary", 
    "REDUCE", "MAX", "1", "@salary", "AS", "max_salary",
    "REDUCE", "MIN", "1", "@salary", "AS", "min_salary",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@job_count", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "10"
]

results = r.execute_command(*location_analytics)
print("Job Market Analytics by Location:")
print("=" * 50)

# Process aggregation results
for i in range(1, len(results)):  # Skip count at index 0
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    location = row_dict.get('location', 'Unknown')
    job_count = row_dict.get('job_count', 0)
    avg_salary = float(row_dict.get('avg_salary', 0))
    max_salary = row_dict.get('max_salary', 0)
    min_salary = row_dict.get('min_salary', 0)
    
    print(f"Location: {location}")
    print(f"  Jobs Available: {job_count}")
    print(f"  Avg Salary: ${avg_salary:,.0f}")
    print(f"  Salary Range: ${min_salary} - ${max_salary}")
    print("---")
Job Market Analytics by Location: ================================================== Location: San Francisco Jobs Available: 45 Avg Salary: $128,500 Salary Range: $85000 - $180000 --- Location: New York Jobs Available: 38 Avg Salary: $122,300 Salary Range: $90000 - $165000 --- Location: Remote Jobs Available: 52 Avg Salary: $115,700 Salary Range: $75000 - $155000 ---
Example 2: Skills demand analysis
# Analyze most in-demand skills across job postings
skills_analysis = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "@status:{active}",  # Only active jobs
    "APPLY", "split", "1", "@skills", "AS", "skill_list",  # Split skills by comma
    "APPLY", "substr", "2", "@skill_list", "0", "-1", "AS", "individual_skill",
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@individual_skill", 
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "skill_demand",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_salary_for_skill",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@skill_demand", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "10"
]

# Alternative approach using simpler aggregation
simple_skills_analysis = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "python OR django OR javascript OR react OR aws",
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@skills",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "jobs_with_skillset", 
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_salary",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@jobs_with_skillset", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "8"
]

results = r.execute_command(*simple_skills_analysis)
print("Skills Demand Analysis:")
print("=" * 40)

for i in range(1, len(results)):
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    skills = row_dict.get('skills', 'Unknown')
    job_count = row_dict.get('jobs_with_skillset', 0)
    avg_salary = float(row_dict.get('avg_salary', 0))
    
    print(f"Skillset: {skills}")
    print(f"  Jobs requiring: {job_count}")
    print(f"  Average salary: ${avg_salary:,.0f}")
    print("---")
Skills Demand Analysis: ======================================== Skillset: Python,Django,Redis,PostgreSQL Jobs requiring: 28 Average salary: $125,400 --- Skillset: JavaScript,React,Node.js Jobs requiring: 22 Average salary: $118,700 --- Skillset: Python,Django,AWS,Docker Jobs requiring: 19 Average salary: $132,100 ---
Example 3: Company hiring trends
# Analyze hiring patterns by company
company_trends = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "*",  # All jobs
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@company",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "total_positions",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_offer", 
    "REDUCE", "COUNT_DISTINCT", "1", "@type", "AS", "job_types_offered",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@total_positions", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "5"
]

results = r.execute_command(*company_trends)
print("Company Hiring Trends:")
print("=" * 35)

for i in range(1, len(results)):
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    company = row_dict.get('company', 'Unknown')
    positions = row_dict.get('total_positions', 0)
    avg_offer = float(row_dict.get('avg_offer', 0))
    job_types = row_dict.get('job_types_offered', 0)
    
    print(f"Company: {company}")
    print(f"  Open positions: {positions}")
    print(f"  Average offer: ${avg_offer:,.0f}")
    print(f"  Job types: {job_types}")
    print("---")
Company Hiring Trends: =================================== Company: TechCorp Open positions: 12 Average offer: $128,300 Job types: 2 --- Company: StartupXYZ Open positions: 8 Average offer: $115,600 Job types: 3 --- Company: MegaCorp Inc Open positions: 6 Average offer: $145,200 Job types: 1 ---
Example 4: Salary distribution analysis
# Create salary ranges and analyze distribution
salary_distribution = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "*",  # All jobs
    # Create salary ranges using APPLY
    "APPLY", "floor(@salary/20000)*20000", "AS", "salary_bucket",
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@salary_bucket",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "job_count",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_in_bucket",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@salary_bucket", "ASC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "10"
]

results = r.execute_command(*salary_distribution)
print("Salary Distribution Analysis:")
print("=" * 40)

for i in range(1, len(results)):
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    bucket = int(float(row_dict.get('salary_bucket', 0)))
    count = row_dict.get('job_count', 0)
    avg_in_bucket = float(row_dict.get('avg_in_bucket', 0))
    
    range_end = bucket + 19999
    print(f"Salary Range: ${bucket:,} - ${range_end:,}")
    print(f"  Job count: {count}")
    print(f"  Average: ${avg_in_bucket:,.0f}")
    print("---")
Salary Distribution Analysis: ======================================== Salary Range: $60,000 - $79,999 Job count: 8 Average: $72,500 --- Salary Range: $80,000 - $99,999 Job count: 15 Average: $91,200 --- Salary Range: $100,000 - $119,999 Job count: 22 Average: $108,700 --- Salary Range: $120,000 - $139,999 Job count: 18 Average: $128,400 ---
Example 5: Time-based posting trends
import time
from datetime import datetime

# Analyze job posting patterns over time
time_trends = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx", 
    "*",  # All jobs
    # Group by month (convert timestamp to month)
    "APPLY", "timefmt(@posted_date, '%Y-%m')", "AS", "posting_month",
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@posting_month",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "jobs_posted",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_monthly_salary",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@posting_month", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "6"
]

# Simpler version using numeric grouping
monthly_trends = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "@status:{active}",  # Active jobs only
    # Create monthly buckets from timestamps  
    "APPLY", "floor(@posted_date/2629746)*2629746", "AS", "month_bucket", # ~30 days
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@month_bucket", 
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "jobs_posted",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT_DISTINCT", "1", "@company", "AS", "unique_companies",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_salary",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@month_bucket", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "3"
]

results = r.execute_command(*monthly_trends)
print("Monthly Job Posting Trends:")
print("=" * 35)

for i in range(1, len(results)):
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    bucket_timestamp = int(float(row_dict.get('month_bucket', 0)))
    jobs_posted = row_dict.get('jobs_posted', 0)
    companies = row_dict.get('unique_companies', 0)
    avg_salary = float(row_dict.get('avg_salary', 0))
    
    # Convert timestamp to readable date
    date_str = datetime.fromtimestamp(bucket_timestamp).strftime('%Y-%m')
    
    print(f"Month: {date_str}")
    print(f"  Jobs posted: {jobs_posted}")
    print(f"  Companies hiring: {companies}")
    print(f"  Avg salary: ${avg_salary:,.0f}")
    print("---")
Monthly Job Posting Trends: =================================== Month: 2024-08 Jobs posted: 34 Companies hiring: 18 Avg salary: $124,500 --- Month: 2024-07 Jobs posted: 28 Companies hiring: 15 Avg salary: $119,200 --- Month: 2024-06 Jobs posted: 31 Companies hiring: 20 Avg salary: $126,800 ---

FT.INFO

Docs: Redis FT.INFO Documentation

Returns information and statistics about an index.

Full description

FT.INFO provides comprehensive details about a search index including field definitions, document count, indexing options, memory usage, and performance statistics. Essential for Django monitoring, troubleshooting search issues, and capacity planning for production search implementations.

Syntax

FT.INFO index_name

Parameters

  • index_name: Name of the index to inspect (string).

Return value

Array: Detailed index information including schema, statistics, and configuration.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Get job index statistics
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Get comprehensive index information
info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", "job_search_idx")

# Parse key statistics
print("Job Search Index Statistics:")
print("=" * 50)

# Convert array response to dictionary for easier access
info_dict = {}
for i in range(0, len(info), 2):
    key = info[i]
    value = info[i + 1] if i + 1 < len(info) else None
    info_dict[key] = value

print(f"Index name: job_search_idx")
print(f"Documents indexed: {info_dict.get('num_docs', 0):,}")
print(f"Index size (bytes): {info_dict.get('inverted_sz_mb', 0)} MB")
print(f"Number of fields: {len(info_dict.get('attributes', [])) // 2}")
print(f"Indexing: {info_dict.get('indexing', 'complete')}")
Job Search Index Statistics: ================================================== Index name: job_search_idx Documents indexed: 1,247 Index size (bytes): 3.2 MB Number of fields: 9 Indexing: complete
Example 2: Monitor Django search index health
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
from django.utils import timezone
import redis

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Monitor Redis search index health'
    
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        indexes = ['job_search_idx', 'user_profile_idx', 'company_idx']
        
        print(f"Search Index Health Check - {timezone.now()}")
        print("=" * 60)
        
        for idx_name in indexes:
            try:
                info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", idx_name)
                info_dict = dict(zip(info[::2], info[1::2]))
                
                num_docs = info_dict.get('num_docs', 0)
                indexing = info_dict.get('indexing', 'unknown')
                
                status = "✓ HEALTHY" if indexing == 0 else "⚠ INDEXING"
                
                print(f"\n{idx_name}:")
                print(f"  Status: {status}")
                print(f"  Documents: {num_docs:,}")
                print(f"  Memory: {info_dict.get('inverted_sz_mb', 0)} MB")
                
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"\n{idx_name}: ✗ ERROR - {str(e)}")
Search Index Health Check - 2024-08-15 14:23:45 ============================================================ job_search_idx: Status: ✓ HEALTHY Documents: 1,247 Memory: 3.2 MB user_profile_idx: Status: ✓ HEALTHY Documents: 8,542 Memory: 12.8 MB company_idx: Status: ✓ HEALTHY Documents: 234 Memory: 0.8 MB
Example 3: Analyze index field configuration
# Inspect field definitions for optimization
info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", "job_search_idx")
info_dict = dict(zip(info[::2], info[1::2]))

attributes = info_dict.get('attributes', [])

print("Job Index Field Configuration:")
print("=" * 50)

# Attributes come as nested arrays
for i in range(0, len(attributes), 2):
    field_info = attributes[i + 1]
    if isinstance(field_info, list):
        field_dict = dict(zip(field_info[::2], field_info[1::2]))
        
        field_name = field_dict.get('attribute', 'unknown')
        field_type = field_dict.get('type', 'unknown')
        sortable = 'SORTABLE' in str(field_info)
        
        print(f"\nField: {field_name}")
        print(f"  Type: {field_type}")
        print(f"  Sortable: {'Yes' if sortable else 'No'}")
        
        if field_type == 'TEXT':
            weight = field_dict.get('weight', 1.0)
            print(f"  Weight: {weight}")
Job Index Field Configuration: ================================================== Field: title Type: TEXT Sortable: Yes Weight: 2.0 Field: description Type: TEXT Sortable: No Weight: 1.0 Field: salary Type: NUMERIC Sortable: Yes Field: skills Type: TAG Sortable: No
Example 4: Performance metrics for Django optimization
import json

# Get performance-related metrics
info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", "job_search_idx")
info_dict = dict(zip(info[::2], info[1::2]))

performance_metrics = {
    'total_documents': info_dict.get('num_docs', 0),
    'index_size_mb': info_dict.get('inverted_sz_mb', 0),
    'num_terms': info_dict.get('num_terms', 0),
    'num_records': info_dict.get('num_records', 0),
    'max_doc_id': info_dict.get('max_doc_id', 0),
    'gc_stats': info_dict.get('gc_stats', {}),
}

print("Search Performance Metrics:")
print(json.dumps(performance_metrics, indent=2))

# Calculate efficiency ratio
if performance_metrics['total_documents'] > 0:
    mb_per_doc = performance_metrics['index_size_mb'] / performance_metrics['total_documents']
    print(f"\nEfficiency: {mb_per_doc:.4f} MB per document")
Search Performance Metrics: { "total_documents": 1247, "index_size_mb": 3.2, "num_terms": 8934, "num_records": 15623, "max_doc_id": 1250, "gc_stats": {} } Efficiency: 0.0026 MB per document
Example 5: Django admin monitoring integration
from django.contrib import admin
from django.utils.html import format_html
import redis

class SearchIndexAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    """Admin interface for monitoring search indexes"""
    
    def get_index_stats(self):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        try:
            info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", "job_search_idx")
            info_dict = dict(zip(info[::2], info[1::2]))
            
            return {
                'docs': info_dict.get('num_docs', 0),
                'size': info_dict.get('inverted_sz_mb', 0),
                'status': 'healthy' if info_dict.get('indexing') == 0 else 'indexing'
            }
        except Exception as e:
            return {'status': 'error', 'message': str(e)}
    
    def index_status_display(self, obj):
        stats = self.get_index_stats()
        
        if stats.get('status') == 'healthy':
            color = 'green'
            icon = '✓'
        elif stats.get('status') == 'indexing':
            color = 'orange'
            icon = '⚠'
        else:
            color = 'red'
            icon = '✗'
        
        return format_html(
            '{} {} | {} docs | {} MB',
            color, icon, stats.get('status', 'unknown').upper(),
            stats.get('docs', 0), stats.get('size', 0)
        )
    
    index_status_display.short_description = 'Search Index Status'

# Usage in admin
print("Search Index Dashboard:")
stats = SearchIndexAdmin().get_index_stats()
print(f"Status: {stats.get('status')}")
print(f"Documents: {stats.get('docs', 0):,}")
print(f"Size: {stats.get('size', 0)} MB")
Search Index Dashboard: Status: healthy Documents: 1,247 Size: 3.2 MB

FT.DROPINDEX

Docs: Redis FT.DROPINDEX Documentation

Deletes an index and optionally the indexed documents.

Full description

FT.DROPINDEX removes a search index from Redis. By default, it keeps the indexed documents (hashes/JSON) intact; use DD flag to delete documents as well. Essential for Django index management, schema migrations, and testing cleanup where you need to rebuild or remove search functionality.

Syntax

FT.DROPINDEX index_name [DD]

Parameters

  • index_name: Name of the index to delete (string).
  • [DD]: Optional flag to also delete the indexed documents.

Return value

String: "OK" if the index was successfully deleted.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Drop index for schema migration
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Drop old index before creating new one with updated schema
try:
    result = r.execute_command("FT.DROPINDEX", "job_search_idx")
    print(f"Old index dropped: {result}")
    print("Documents preserved for re-indexing")
    
    # Now create new index with updated schema
    # (documents are still in Redis, just need new index)
    print("\nReady to create new index with updated schema")
    
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error dropping index: {e}")
Old index dropped: OK Documents preserved for re-indexing Ready to create new index with updated schema
Example 2: Django test cleanup with DD flag
from django.test import TestCase
import redis

class JobSearchTestCase(TestCase):
    def setUp(self):
        self.redis = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        # Create test index
        self.redis.execute_command(
            "FT.CREATE", "test_job_idx",
            "ON", "HASH",
            "PREFIX", "1", "test:job:",
            "SCHEMA", "title", "TEXT", "salary", "NUMERIC"
        )
        print("Test index created")
    
    def tearDown(self):
        # Clean up completely: drop index AND delete documents
        try:
            result = self.redis.execute_command("FT.DROPINDEX", "test_job_idx", "DD")
            print(f"Test cleanup: {result}")
            print("Index and test documents deleted")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Cleanup note: {e}")

# Simulate test cleanup
test = JobSearchTestCase()
test.setUp()
test.tearDown()
Test index created Test cleanup: OK Index and test documents deleted
Example 3: Safe index replacement in Django
def rebuild_job_search_index(delete_data=False):
    """Safely rebuild search index with optional data preservation"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    index_name = "job_search_idx"
    
    print(f"Rebuilding index: {index_name}")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    # Check if index exists
    try:
        info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", index_name)
        print(f"✓ Existing index found")
        
        # Drop with appropriate flag
        if delete_data:
            r.execute_command("FT.DROPINDEX", index_name, "DD")
            print("✓ Index and documents deleted")
        else:
            r.execute_command("FT.DROPINDEX", index_name)
            print("✓ Index dropped (documents preserved)")
            
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"No existing index to drop: {e}")
    
    # Create new index
    r.execute_command(
        "FT.CREATE", index_name,
        "ON", "HASH", "PREFIX", "1", "job:",
        "SCHEMA", 
        "title", "TEXT", "SORTABLE",
        "salary", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
        "skills", "TAG"
    )
    print("✓ New index created successfully")

# Rebuild preserving data
rebuild_job_search_index(delete_data=False)
Rebuilding index: job_search_idx ================================================== ✓ Existing index found ✓ Index dropped (documents preserved) ✓ New index created successfully
Example 4: Django management command for index cleanup
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
import redis

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Drop Redis search indexes'
    
    def add_arguments(self, parser):
        parser.add_argument('index_name', type=str, help='Index to drop')
        parser.add_argument(
            '--delete-docs',
            action='store_true',
            help='Also delete indexed documents'
        )
    
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        index_name = options['index_name']
        delete_docs = options['delete_docs']
        
        self.stdout.write(f"Dropping index: {index_name}")
        
        try:
            if delete_docs:
                result = r.execute_command("FT.DROPINDEX", index_name, "DD")
                self.stdout.write(
                    self.style.WARNING(f"Index and documents deleted: {result}")
                )
            else:
                result = r.execute_command("FT.DROPINDEX", index_name)
                self.stdout.write(
                    self.style.SUCCESS(f"Index dropped: {result}")
                )
        except Exception as e:
            self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f"Error: {e}"))

# Simulate command execution
print("Django Management Command:")
print("python manage.py drop_search_index job_search_idx")
print("\nResult: Index dropped successfully (documents preserved)")
Django Management Command: python manage.py drop_search_index job_search_idx Result: Index dropped successfully (documents preserved)
Example 5: Conditional index drop with verification
def safe_drop_index(index_name, delete_documents=False, confirm=True):
    """Safely drop index with confirmation and verification"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Check if index exists
    try:
        info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", index_name)
        info_dict = dict(zip(info[::2], info[1::2]))
        num_docs = info_dict.get('num_docs', 0)
        
        print(f"Index: {index_name}")
        print(f"Documents: {num_docs:,}")
        print(f"Action: Drop index" + (" + DELETE documents" if delete_documents else ""))
        
        if confirm:
            # In real app, get user confirmation
            proceed = True  # input("Proceed? (yes/no): ") == 'yes'
        else:
            proceed = True
        
        if proceed:
            if delete_documents:
                result = r.execute_command("FT.DROPINDEX", index_name, "DD")
                print(f"✓ Index and {num_docs:,} documents deleted")
            else:
                result = r.execute_command("FT.DROPINDEX", index_name)
                print(f"✓ Index dropped, {num_docs:,} documents preserved")
            
            return True
        else:
            print("✗ Operation cancelled")
            return False
            
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"✗ Index not found or error: {e}")
        return False

# Example usage
safe_drop_index("old_job_idx", delete_documents=True, confirm=False)
Index: old_job_idx Documents: 847 Action: Drop index + DELETE documents ✓ Index and 847 documents deleted

FT.ALTER

Docs: Redis FT.ALTER Documentation

Adds a new field to the index schema.

Full description

FT.ALTER allows you to add new fields to an existing search index without rebuilding it. Existing documents won't have values for new fields until updated, but the field becomes searchable for future documents. Perfect for Django schema evolution where you need to add search capabilities to new model fields without downtime.

Syntax

FT.ALTER index_name SCHEMA ADD field_name field_type [options ...]

Parameters

  • index_name: Name of the index to modify (string).
  • field_name: Name of the new field to add (string).
  • field_type: Type of field (TEXT, NUMERIC, TAG, GEO).
  • [options]: Field-specific options (SORTABLE, WEIGHT, etc.).

Return value

String: "OK" if the field was successfully added.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Add new searchable field to job index
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Add new "remote_friendly" tag field to existing job index
result = r.execute_command(
    "FT.ALTER", "job_search_idx",
    "SCHEMA", "ADD",
    "remote_friendly", "TAG"
)

print(f"Schema updated: {result}")
print("\nNew field 'remote_friendly' added to job search")
print("Future job postings can now be filtered by remote work options")

# Update existing job with new field
r.hset("job:1001", "remote_friendly", "yes")
print("\nExisting job updated with remote_friendly=yes")
Schema updated: OK New field 'remote_friendly' added to job search Future job postings can now be filtered by remote work options Existing job updated with remote_friendly=yes
Example 2: Django model evolution with search
from django.db import models
from django.db.models.signals import post_migrate
from django.dispatch import receiver
import redis

# JobPost model evolved to include experience_level
class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    salary = models.IntegerField()
    experience_level = models.CharField(max_length=50)  # NEW FIELD
    
    class Meta:
        app_label = 'jobpost'

@receiver(post_migrate)
def update_search_schema(sender, **kwargs):
    """Auto-update search index when models change"""
    if sender.name == 'jobpost':
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        try:
            # Add new field to search index
            result = r.execute_command(
                "FT.ALTER", "job_search_idx",
                "SCHEMA", "ADD",
                "experience_level", "TAG", "SORTABLE"
            )
            print(f"Search index updated: {result}")
            print("New field 'experience_level' now searchable")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Schema update: {e}")

# Simulate migration
print("Running Django migrations...")
print("✓ Model schema updated")
update_search_schema(sender=type('jobpost', (), {'name': 'jobpost'}))
print("✓ Search schema synchronized")
Running Django migrations... ✓ Model schema updated Search index updated: OK New field 'experience_level' now searchable ✓ Search schema synchronized
Example 3: Add sortable numeric field
# Add view_count field for sorting popular jobs
result = r.execute_command(
    "FT.ALTER", "job_search_idx",
    "SCHEMA", "ADD",
    "view_count", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE"
)

print(f"Added sortable field: {result}")

# Now you can sort search results by popularity
search_popular = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "@title:Python",
    "SORTBY", "view_count", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "5"
]

print("\nSearching for popular Python jobs...")
print("Results will be sorted by view count (most popular first)")

# Update some jobs with view counts
jobs_to_update = [
    ("job:1001", 1250),
    ("job:1002", 890),
    ("job:1003", 2100)
]

for job_key, views in jobs_to_update:
    r.hset(job_key, "view_count", views)
    print(f"Updated {job_key}: {views} views")
Added sortable field: OK Searching for popular Python jobs... Results will be sorted by view count (most popular first) Updated job:1001: 1250 views Updated job:1002: 890 views Updated job:1003: 2100 views
Example 4: Add weighted text field for better relevance
# Add company_description with higher weight for better matching
result = r.execute_command(
    "FT.ALTER", "job_search_idx",
    "SCHEMA", "ADD",
    "company_description", "TEXT", "WEIGHT", "1.5"
)

print(f"Text field with weight added: {result}")
print("\nCompany descriptions now searchable with 1.5x relevance weight")

# Update existing jobs
company_info = {
    "job:1001": "Leading tech startup building innovative AI solutions",
    "job:1002": "Fortune 500 company with excellent benefits",
    "job:1003": "Fast-growing fintech disrupting the payment industry"
}

for job_key, description in company_info.items():
    r.hset(job_key, "company_description", description)
    print(f"Added description to {job_key}")

print("\n✓ Search relevance improved with company context")
Text field with weight added: OK Company descriptions now searchable with 1.5x relevance weight Added description to job:1001 Added description to job:1002 Added description to job:1003 ✓ Search relevance improved with company context
Example 5: Django management command for schema updates
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
import redis

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Add new field to search index'
    
    def add_arguments(self, parser):
        parser.add_argument('--index', type=str, default='job_search_idx')
        parser.add_argument('--field', type=str, required=True)
        parser.add_argument('--type', type=str, required=True)
        parser.add_argument('--sortable', action='store_true')
        parser.add_argument('--weight', type=float, default=1.0)
    
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        cmd = [
            "FT.ALTER", options['index'],
            "SCHEMA", "ADD",
            options['field'], options['type']
        ]
        
        if options['sortable']:
            cmd.append("SORTABLE")
        
        if options['type'] == 'TEXT' and options['weight'] != 1.0:
            cmd.extend(["WEIGHT", str(options['weight'])])
        
        try:
            result = r.execute_command(*cmd)
            self.stdout.write(
                self.style.SUCCESS(f"✓ Field added: {options['field']} ({result})")
            )
        except Exception as e:
            self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f"✗ Error: {e}"))

# Simulate command
print("Command: python manage.py add_search_field --field benefits --type TEXT")
print("\nResult: ✓ Field added: benefits (OK)")
Command: python manage.py add_search_field --field benefits --type TEXT Result: ✓ Field added: benefits (OK)

FT.ALIASADD

Docs: Redis FT.ALIASADD Documentation

Creates an alias for an index.

Full description

FT.ALIASADD creates an alias (alternative name) for an existing search index. This allows Django applications to reference indexes by stable names while the underlying index can be rebuilt or changed. Perfect for zero-downtime index updates where you create a new index, verify it, then switch the alias.

Syntax

FT.ALIASADD alias index_name

Parameters

  • alias: The alias name to create (string).
  • index_name: The actual index to alias (string).

Return value

String: "OK" if the alias was created successfully.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Create alias for job search
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Create alias for production job index
result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASADD", "jobs_current", "job_search_idx_v1")

print(f"Alias created: {result}")
print("\nDjango app can now use 'jobs_current' alias")
print("Underlying index can be updated without code changes")
Alias created: OK Django app can now use 'jobs_current' alias Underlying index can be updated without code changes
Example 2: Zero-downtime index rebuild
# Step 1: Create new index version
r.execute_command(
    "FT.CREATE", "job_search_idx_v2",
    "ON", "HASH", "PREFIX", "1", "job:",
    "SCHEMA", "title", "TEXT", "SORTABLE"
)
print("✓ New index version created: job_search_idx_v2")

# Step 2: Re-index data (happens in background)
print("✓ Re-indexing data into v2...")

# Step 3: Create alias to new version
try:
    r.execute_command("FT.ALIASADD", "jobs_prod", "job_search_idx_v2")
    print("✓ Alias 'jobs_prod' now points to v2")
except Exception as e:
    # If alias exists, use ALIASUPDATE instead
    print(f"Alias exists, use FT.ALIASUPDATE: {e}")

print("\n✓ Django app automatically using new index")
✓ New index version created: job_search_idx_v2 ✓ Re-indexing data into v2... ✓ Alias 'jobs_prod' now points to v2 ✓ Django app automatically using new index
Example 3: Environment-specific aliases
# Create aliases for different Django environments
environments = {
    'development': 'job_search_idx_dev',
    'staging': 'job_search_idx_staging',
    'production': 'job_search_idx_prod'
}

for env, index_name in environments.items():
    alias = f"jobs_{env}"
    try:
        result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASADD", alias, index_name)
        print(f"✓ {env.upper()}: {alias} → {index_name}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"✗ {env}: {e}")

print("\nDjango settings can reference environment-specific aliases")
✓ DEVELOPMENT: jobs_development → job_search_idx_dev ✓ STAGING: jobs_staging → job_search_idx_staging ✓ PRODUCTION: jobs_production → job_search_idx_prod Django settings can reference environment-specific aliases
Example 4: Django settings with aliases
from django.conf import settings
import redis

class JobSearchService:
    def __init__(self):
        self.redis = redis.Redis(
            host=settings.REDIS_HOST,
            port=settings.REDIS_PORT,
            decode_responses=True
        )
        # Use alias instead of hard-coded index name
        self.index_name = settings.SEARCH_INDEX_ALIAS  # "jobs_current"
    
    def search_jobs(self, query):
        """Search using alias - works regardless of underlying index version"""
        results = self.redis.execute_command(
            "FT.SEARCH", self.index_name, query, "LIMIT", "0", "10"
        )
        return results

# Example Django settings.py:
# SEARCH_INDEX_ALIAS = 'jobs_current'  # Points to whatever version is active

service = JobSearchService()
print(f"Search service using alias: {service.index_name}")
print("✓ Underlying index can be swapped without code deployment")
Search service using alias: jobs_current ✓ Underlying index can be swapped without code deployment
Example 5: Blue-green deployment pattern
def deploy_new_search_index(new_index_name, alias="jobs_live"):
    """Blue-green deployment for search indexes"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    print(f"Deploying new index: {new_index_name}")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    # Verify new index is ready
    try:
        info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", new_index_name)
        print(f"✓ New index verified: {new_index_name}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"✗ New index not ready: {e}")
        return False
    
    # Create or update alias
    try:
        r.execute_command("FT.ALIASADD", alias, new_index_name)
        print(f"✓ Alias created: {alias} → {new_index_name}")
    except:
        # Alias exists, will update in next step
        print(f"ℹ Alias exists, use FT.ALIASUPDATE")
    
    print(f"\n✓ Deployment complete")
    return True

# Deploy
deploy_new_search_index("job_search_idx_v3", "jobs_live")
Deploying new index: job_search_idx_v3 ================================================== ✓ New index verified: job_search_idx_v3 ✓ Alias created: jobs_live → job_search_idx_v3 ✓ Deployment complete

FT.ALIASDEL

Docs: Redis FT.ALIASDEL Documentation

Deletes an index alias.

Full description

FT.ALIASDEL removes an alias from a search index. The underlying index remains intact, only the alias reference is deleted. Useful for Django cleanup operations, removing deprecated aliases, or managing multi-environment index configurations.

Syntax

FT.ALIASDEL alias

Parameters

  • alias: The alias name to delete (string).

Return value

String: "OK" if the alias was successfully deleted.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Remove deprecated alias
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Delete old alias after migration
result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASDEL", "jobs_old")

print(f"Alias deleted: {result}")
print("Old alias removed, Django app using new alias")
Alias deleted: OK Old alias removed, Django app using new alias
Example 2: Django deployment cleanup
def cleanup_deployment_aliases():
    """Clean up temporary aliases after deployment"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    temporary_aliases = ['jobs_staging', 'jobs_test', 'jobs_canary']
    
    print("Cleaning up temporary aliases:")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    for alias in temporary_aliases:
        try:
            result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASDEL", alias)
            print(f"✓ Deleted: {alias}")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"ℹ {alias}: {e}")
    
    print("\n✓ Cleanup complete")

cleanup_deployment_aliases()
Cleaning up temporary aliases: ================================================== ✓ Deleted: jobs_staging ✓ Deleted: jobs_test ✓ Deleted: jobs_canary ✓ Cleanup complete
Example 3: Conditional alias removal
def safe_delete_alias(alias_name, verify=True):
    """Safely delete alias with verification"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    if verify:
        # Check alias usage before deletion
        try:
            # Try to get info through alias
            info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", alias_name)
            print(f"⚠ Warning: Alias '{alias_name}' is active")
            
            # In production, check application usage
            proceed = True  # input("Delete anyway? (yes/no): ") == 'yes'
        except:
            proceed = True
    else:
        proceed = True
    
    if proceed:
        try:
            result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASDEL", alias_name)
            print(f"✓ Deleted alias: {alias_name}")
            return True
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"✗ Error: {e}")
            return False
    else:
        print("✗ Deletion cancelled")
        return False

# Safe deletion
safe_delete_alias("jobs_deprecated", verify=False)
✓ Deleted alias: jobs_deprecated
Example 4: Django management command
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
import redis

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Delete Redis search index alias'
    
    def add_arguments(self, parser):
        parser.add_argument('alias', type=str, help='Alias to delete')
        parser.add_argument(
            '--force',
            action='store_true',
            help='Skip confirmation'
        )
    
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        alias = options['alias']
        force = options['force']
        
        if not force:
            self.stdout.write(f"About to delete alias: {alias}")
            # In real app: confirm = input("Proceed? (yes/no): ")
        
        try:
            result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASDEL", alias)
            self.stdout.write(
                self.style.SUCCESS(f"✓ Alias deleted: {alias}")
            )
        except Exception as e:
            self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f"✗ Error: {e}"))

# Simulate command
print("Command: python manage.py delete_search_alias jobs_old --force")
print("Result: ✓ Alias deleted: jobs_old")
Command: python manage.py delete_search_alias jobs_old --force Result: ✓ Alias deleted: jobs_old
Example 5: Batch alias cleanup
def cleanup_all_aliases_for_index(index_name):
    """Remove all aliases pointing to a specific index"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Get list of known aliases (tracked in Django)
    known_aliases = [
        'jobs_old_v1', 'jobs_old_v2', 'jobs_legacy',
        'jobs_deprecated', 'jobs_test_old'
    ]
    
    print(f"Removing aliases for index: {index_name}")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    deleted = []
    errors = []
    
    for alias in known_aliases:
        try:
            # Check if alias points to our index
            info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", alias)
            # If we get here, alias exists
            
            result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASDEL", alias)
            deleted.append(alias)
            print(f"✓ Deleted: {alias}")
        except Exception as e:
            errors.append((alias, str(e)))
    
    print(f"\n✓ Deleted {len(deleted)} aliases")
    if errors:
        print(f"ℹ {len(errors)} errors (aliases may not exist)")

cleanup_all_aliases_for_index("job_search_idx_v1")
Removing aliases for index: job_search_idx_v1 ================================================== ✓ Deleted: jobs_old_v1 ✓ Deleted: jobs_old_v2 ✓ Deleted: jobs_legacy ✓ Deleted 3 aliases ℹ 2 errors (aliases may not exist)

FT.ALIASUPDATE

Docs: Redis FT.ALIASUPDATE Documentation

Updates an existing alias to point to a different index.

Full description

FT.ALIASUPDATE changes an existing alias to point to a different index. This enables atomic index switching for Django applications, allowing zero-downtime updates where you build a new index version, then atomically switch the alias to point to it. Perfect for production deployments.

Syntax

FT.ALIASUPDATE alias index_name

Parameters

  • alias: The existing alias to update (string).
  • index_name: The new index to point to (string).

Return value

String: "OK" if the alias was successfully updated.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Switch to new index version
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Atomically switch alias to new index version
result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASUPDATE", "jobs_prod", "job_search_idx_v2")

print(f"Alias updated: {result}")
print("Django app instantly using new index version")
print("Zero downtime deployment complete!")
Alias updated: OK Django app instantly using new index version Zero downtime deployment complete!
Example 2: Zero-downtime index rebuild
def rebuild_search_index():
    """Rebuild index with zero downtime"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    current_alias = "jobs_live"
    old_index = "job_search_idx_v1"
    new_index = "job_search_idx_v2"
    
    print("Zero-Downtime Index Rebuild:")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    # Step 1: Create new index
    print(f"1. Creating new index: {new_index}...")
    r.execute_command(
        "FT.CREATE", new_index,
        "ON", "HASH", "PREFIX", "1", "job:",
        "SCHEMA", "title", "TEXT", "SORTABLE"
    )
    print("   ✓ New index created")
    
    # Step 2: Data re-indexes automatically
    print("2. Re-indexing data...")
    print("   ✓ Data indexed")
    
    # Step 3: Atomically switch alias
    print(f"3. Switching alias '{current_alias}' to {new_index}...")
    result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASUPDATE", current_alias, new_index)
    print(f"   ✓ Alias switched ({result})")
    
    # Step 4: Cleanup old index
    print(f"4. Old index {old_index} can be dropped when safe")
    
    print("\n✓ Rebuild complete with zero downtime!")

rebuild_search_index()
Zero-Downtime Index Rebuild: ================================================== 1. Creating new index: job_search_idx_v2... ✓ New index created 2. Re-indexing data... ✓ Data indexed 3. Switching alias 'jobs_live' to job_search_idx_v2... ✓ Alias switched (OK) 4. Old index job_search_idx_v1 can be dropped when safe ✓ Rebuild complete with zero downtime!
Example 3: Rollback capability
def rollback_index_deployment(alias, previous_index):
    """Quickly rollback to previous index version"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    print(f"ROLLBACK: {alias} → {previous_index}")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    try:
        # Verify old index still exists
        info = r.execute_command("FT.INFO", previous_index)
        print(f"✓ Previous index verified: {previous_index}")
        
        # Switch alias back
        result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASUPDATE", alias, previous_index)
        print(f"✓ Alias rolled back: {result}")
        print("\nDjango app instantly using previous index")
        
        return True
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"✗ Rollback failed: {e}")
        return False

# Example rollback
rollback_index_deployment("jobs_live", "job_search_idx_v1")
ROLLBACK: jobs_live → job_search_idx_v1 ================================================== ✓ Previous index verified: job_search_idx_v1 ✓ Alias rolled back: OK Django app instantly using previous index
Example 4: Django deployment script
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
from django.conf import settings
import redis
import time

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Deploy new search index with validation'
    
    def add_arguments(self, parser):
        parser.add_argument('new_index', type=str)
        parser.add_argument('--validate', action='store_true')
    
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        r = redis.Redis(host=settings.REDIS_HOST, decode_responses=True)
        
        alias = settings.SEARCH_INDEX_ALIAS  # "jobs_prod"
        new_index = options['new_index']
        
        self.stdout.write(f"Deploying: {new_index}")
        
        # Optional validation
        if options['validate']:
            self.stdout.write("Validating new index...")
            # Run test queries
            test_result = r.execute_command("FT.SEARCH", new_index, "*", "LIMIT", "0", "1")
            self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS("✓ Validation passed"))
        
        # Switch alias
        try:
            result = r.execute_command("FT.ALIASUPDATE", alias, new_index)
            self.stdout.write(
                self.style.SUCCESS(f"✓ Deployed: {alias} → {new_index}")
            )
        except Exception as e:
            self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f"✗ Deployment failed: {e}"))

# Simulate
print("Command: python manage.py deploy_search_index job_search_idx_v3 --validate")
print("\nResult:")
print("Validating new index...")
print("✓ Validation passed")
print("✓ Deployed: jobs_prod → job_search_idx_v3")
Command: python manage.py deploy_search_index job_search_idx_v3 --validate Result: Validating new index... ✓ Validation passed ✓ Deployed: jobs_prod → job_search_idx_v3
Example 5: Blue-green with health checks
def deploy_with_health_check(alias, new_index, old_index):
    """Deploy with automatic health checking and rollback"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    print(f"Blue-Green Deployment: {alias}")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    # Switch to new (green) index
    print(f"1. Switching to green index: {new_index}")
    r.execute_command("FT.ALIASUPDATE", alias, new_index)
    print("   ✓ Switched")
    
    # Health check
    print("2. Running health checks...")
    time.sleep(1)  # Simulate health check
    
    try:
        # Test search
        result = r.execute_command("FT.SEARCH", alias, "*", "LIMIT", "0", "1")
        doc_count = result[0] if result else 0
        
        if doc_count > 0:
            print(f"   ✓ Health check passed ({doc_count} docs)")
            print("\n✓ Deployment successful!")
            print(f"ℹ Old index {old_index} can be cleaned up")
            return True
        else:
            raise Exception("No documents in index")
            
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"   ✗ Health check failed: {e}")
        print("3. Rolling back to blue index...")
        r.execute_command("FT.ALIASUPDATE", alias, old_index)
        print("   ✓ Rolled back")
        return False

deploy_with_health_check("jobs_live", "job_search_idx_v2", "job_search_idx_v1")
Blue-Green Deployment: jobs_live ================================================== 1. Switching to green index: job_search_idx_v2 ✓ Switched 2. Running health checks... ✓ Health check passed (1247 docs) ✓ Deployment successful! ℹ Old index job_search_idx_v1 can be cleaned up

FT.SPELLCHECK

Docs: Redis FT.SPELLCHECK Documentation

Performs spelling correction on a query.

Full description

FT.SPELLCHECK analyzes a search query and suggests spelling corrections for misspelled terms. It uses the indexed terms and optional custom dictionaries to provide "Did you mean?" functionality. Essential for Django search interfaces to improve user experience by handling typos and suggesting correct spellings.

Syntax

FT.SPELLCHECK index query [DISTANCE dist] [TERMS INCLUDE|EXCLUDE dict [terms ...]]

Parameters

  • index: The index name to use for spell checking (string).
  • query: The query text to check (string).
  • [DISTANCE dist]: Maximum Levenshtein distance (default 1).
  • [TERMS ...]: Include or exclude terms from custom dictionaries.

Return value

Array: Suggested corrections with scores for each misspelled term.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Basic spell check for job search
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# User searches for "python devloper" (typo)
misspelled_query = "python devloper"

suggestions = r.execute_command(
    "FT.SPELLCHECK", "job_search_idx",
    misspelled_query
)

print(f"Original query: '{misspelled_query}'")
print("\nSuggestions:")

# Parse suggestions (format: [TERM, suggestions_array])
for i in range(0, len(suggestions), 3):
    if i < len(suggestions):
        term_type = suggestions[i]  # "TERM"
        original_term = suggestions[i + 1] if i + 1 < len(suggestions) else None
        corrections = suggestions[i + 2] if i + 2 < len(suggestions) else []
        
        if corrections:
            print(f"\n'{original_term}' → ", end="")
            for correction in corrections:
                if isinstance(correction, list) and len(correction) >= 2:
                    corrected_word = correction[1]
                    print(f"'{corrected_word}'", end=" ")
            print()
Original query: 'python devloper' Suggestions: 'devloper' → 'developer' 'developers'
Example 2: Django search view with spell check
from django.shortcuts import render
import redis

def job_search_view(request):
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    query = request.GET.get('q', '')
    
    # Perform spell check
    suggestions = r.execute_command(
        "FT.SPELLCHECK", "job_search_idx",
        query,
        "DISTANCE", "2"  # Allow up to 2 character difference
    )
    
    # Parse and extract corrected query
    corrected_terms = []
    has_corrections = False
    
    for i in range(0, len(suggestions), 3):
        if i + 2 < len(suggestions):
            original = suggestions[i + 1]
            corrections = suggestions[i + 2]
            
            if corrections:
                has_corrections = True
                # Use first suggestion
                if isinstance(corrections[0], list):
                    corrected_terms.append(corrections[0][1])
            else:
                corrected_terms.append(original)
    
    corrected_query = ' '.join(corrected_terms)
    
    context = {
        'original_query': query,
        'corrected_query': corrected_query if has_corrections else None,
        'has_suggestions': has_corrections
    }
    
    return context

# Example usage
request_mock = type('Request', (), {'GET': {'q': 'pythn engneer'}})()
result = job_search_view(request_mock)

print(f"User searched for: '{result['original_query']}'")
if result['has_suggestions']:
    print(f"Did you mean: '{result['corrected_query']}'?")
User searched for: 'pythn engneer' Did you mean: 'python engineer'?
Example 3: Custom dictionary for technical terms
# Add technical terms to custom dictionary
tech_dict_name = "tech_terms"

# Add common technical terms
tech_terms = ["django", "kubernetes", "postgresql", "reactjs", "typescript"]

for term in tech_terms:
    r.execute_command("FT.DICTADD", tech_dict_name, term)

print(f"Added {len(tech_terms)} technical terms to dictionary")

# Spell check with custom dictionary
query = "djang developer"

suggestions = r.execute_command(
    "FT.SPELLCHECK", "job_search_idx",
    query,
    "TERMS", "INCLUDE", tech_dict_name
)

print(f"\nQuery: '{query}'")
print("Using technical terms dictionary for better suggestions")
Added 5 technical terms to dictionary Query: 'djang developer' Using technical terms dictionary for better suggestions
Example 4: Auto-correct search with user confirmation
def auto_correct_search(query, auto_correct=False):
    """Search with spell checking and optional auto-correction"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Check spelling
    suggestions = r.execute_command(
        "FT.SPELLCHECK", "job_search_idx", query
    )
    
    corrected_query = query
    corrections_made = []
    
    for i in range(0, len(suggestions), 3):
        if i + 2 < len(suggestions):
            original = suggestions[i + 1]
            corrections = suggestions[i + 2]
            
            if corrections and isinstance(corrections[0], list):
                corrected = corrections[0][1]
                corrections_made.append((original, corrected))
                corrected_query = corrected_query.replace(original, corrected)
    
    if corrections_made:
        print(f"Original: '{query}'")
        print(f"Corrected: '{corrected_query}'")
        print("\nCorrections:")
        for orig, corr in corrections_made:
            print(f"  '{orig}' → '{corr}'")
        
        if auto_correct:
            print("\n✓ Auto-corrected and searching...")
            search_query = corrected_query
        else:
            print("\n? Suggest to user: 'Did you mean {corrected_query}?'")
            search_query = query
    else:
        print(f"No corrections needed for: '{query}'")
        search_query = query
    
    return search_query

# Example
auto_correct_search("senoir python devloper", auto_correct=False)
Original: 'senoir python devloper' Corrected: 'senior python developer' Corrections: 'senoir' → 'senior' 'devloper' → 'developer' ? Suggest to user: 'Did you mean senior python developer?'
Example 5: Spell check with distance threshold
def spell_check_with_confidence(query, distance=1):
    """Spell check with configurable confidence level"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    suggestions = r.execute_command(
        "FT.SPELLCHECK", "job_search_idx",
        query,
        "DISTANCE", str(distance)
    )
    
    print(f"Spell Check (distance={distance}):")
    print("=" * 50)
    print(f"Query: '{query}'")
    
    has_suggestions = False
    
    for i in range(0, len(suggestions), 3):
        if i + 2 < len(suggestions):
            original = suggestions[i + 1]
            corrections = suggestions[i + 2]
            
            if corrections:
                has_suggestions = True
                print(f"\nTerm: '{original}'")
                print("  Suggestions:")
                
                for correction in corrections[:3]:  # Top 3
                    if isinstance(correction, list) and len(correction) >= 2:
                        word = correction[1]
                        score = correction[0] if len(correction) > 0 else 0
                        print(f"    - {word} (confidence: {score})")
    
    if not has_suggestions:
        print("\n✓ No spelling corrections needed")
    
    return has_suggestions

# Test with different distance values
spell_check_with_confidence("pythoon", distance=1)
print("\n" + "-" * 50 + "\n")
spell_check_with_confidence("pyton", distance=2)
Spell Check (distance=1): ================================================== Query: 'pythoon' Term: 'pythoon' Suggestions: - python (confidence: 0.9) -------------------------------------------------- Spell Check (distance=2): ================================================== Query: 'pyton' Term: 'pyton' Suggestions: - python (confidence: 0.85) - cython (confidence: 0.75)

FT.DICTADD

Docs: Redis FT.DICTADD Documentation

Adds terms to a dictionary.

Full description

FT.DICTADD adds one or more terms to a custom dictionary that can be used with FT.SPELLCHECK for domain-specific spell checking. Perfect for Django applications to add industry-specific terminology, product names, or technical jargon that should be recognized as correct spellings.

Syntax

FT.DICTADD dict term [term ...]

Parameters

  • dict: Dictionary name (string).
  • term: One or more terms to add (strings).

Return value

Integer: Number of new terms added (excludes duplicates).

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Add technical skill terms
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Add programming skills to dictionary
skills = ["django", "fastapi", "kubernetes", "postgresql", "redis", "elasticsearch"]

count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTADD", "tech_skills", *skills)

print(f"Added {count} technical skills to dictionary")
print(f"Terms: {', '.join(skills)}")
Added 6 technical skills to dictionary Terms: django, fastapi, kubernetes, postgresql, redis, elasticsearch
Example 2: Django management command to populate dictionary
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
from jobpost.models import Skills
import redis

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Populate Redis spell check dictionary with skills'
    
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        # Get all unique skills from database
        skill_names = Skills.objects.values_list('name', flat=True).distinct()
        skill_list = [s.lower() for s in skill_names if s]
        
        if skill_list:
            count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTADD", "job_skills", *skill_list)
            self.stdout.write(
                self.style.SUCCESS(f"✓ Added {count} skills to dictionary")
            )
        else:
            self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING("No skills found"))

# Simulate
print("Running: python manage.py populate_spell_dict")
print("\n✓ Added 47 skills to dictionary")
Running: python manage.py populate_spell_dict ✓ Added 47 skills to dictionary
Example 3: Add company names from Django models
from jobpost.models import Author

# Get all unique company names
companies = Author.objects.values_list('company', flat=True).distinct()
company_list = [c.lower() for c in companies if c]

# Add to dictionary
count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTADD", "company_names", *company_list)

print(f"Dictionary populated with {count} company names")
print("\nExample companies:")
for company in list(company_list)[:5]:
    print(f"  - {company}")
Dictionary populated with 23 company names Example companies: - techcorp - innovateai - datastream - cloudnine - codeforge
Example 4: Batch add with duplicate handling
# Add terms in batches (handles duplicates automatically)
batch1 = ["python", "java", "golang", "rust"]
batch2 = ["python", "typescript", "kotlin"]  # 'python' is duplicate

count1 = r.execute_command("FT.DICTADD", "languages", *batch1)
count2 = r.execute_command("FT.DICTADD", "languages", *batch2)

print(f"Batch 1: Added {count1} terms")
print(f"Batch 2: Added {count2} terms (python already exists)")
print(f"\nTotal unique terms: {count1 + count2}")
Batch 1: Added 4 terms Batch 2: Added 2 terms (python already exists) Total unique terms: 6
Example 5: Django signal to auto-update dictionary
from django.db.models.signals import post_save
from django.dispatch import receiver
from jobpost.models import Skills
import redis

@receiver(post_save, sender=Skills)
def update_spell_check_dict(sender, instance, created, **kwargs):
    """Automatically add new skills to spell check dictionary"""
    if created and instance.name:
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        skill_term = instance.name.lower()
        
        try:
            count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTADD", "job_skills", skill_term)
            if count > 0:
                print(f"✓ Added '{skill_term}' to spell check dictionary")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"✗ Failed to add to dictionary: {e}")

# Simulate creating new skill
print("Creating new skill: 'WebAssembly'")
print("Django signal triggered:")
print("✓ Added 'webassembly' to spell check dictionary")
Creating new skill: 'WebAssembly' Django signal triggered: ✓ Added 'webassembly' to spell check dictionary

FT.DICTDEL

Docs: Redis FT.DICTDEL Documentation

Deletes terms from a dictionary.

Full description

FT.DICTDEL removes one or more terms from a custom dictionary. Useful for Django applications when terms become obsolete, when cleaning up deprecated technology names, or when removing inappropriate entries from spell check dictionaries.

Syntax

FT.DICTDEL dict term [term ...]

Parameters

  • dict: Dictionary name (string).
  • term: One or more terms to remove (strings).

Return value

Integer: Number of terms actually deleted.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Remove obsolete technology terms
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Remove deprecated technologies
obsolete_terms = ["flash", "silverlight", "ie6"]

count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDEL", "tech_skills", *obsolete_terms)

print(f"Removed {count} obsolete terms from dictionary")
print(f"Cleaned up: {', '.join(obsolete_terms)}")
Removed 3 obsolete terms from dictionary Cleaned up: flash, silverlight, ie6
Example 2: Django signal to remove deleted skills
from django.db.models.signals import post_delete
from django.dispatch import receiver
from jobpost.models import Skills
import redis

@receiver(post_delete, sender=Skills)
def remove_from_spell_dict(sender, instance, **kwargs):
    """Remove deleted skill from spell check dictionary"""
    if instance.name:
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        skill_term = instance.name.lower()
        
        try:
            count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDEL", "job_skills", skill_term)
            if count > 0:
                print(f"✓ Removed '{skill_term}' from dictionary")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"✗ Failed to remove: {e}")

# Simulate deleting skill
print("Deleting skill: 'jQuery' (no longer relevant)")
print("Django signal triggered:")
print("✓ Removed 'jquery' from dictionary")
Deleting skill: 'jQuery' (no longer relevant) Django signal triggered: ✓ Removed 'jquery' from dictionary
Example 3: Batch cleanup of inactive terms
# Clean up terms that are no longer in active use
inactive_skills = ["coldfusion", "perl", "vbscript", "delphi"]

print("Cleaning up inactive terms...")
print("=" * 50)

count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDEL", "tech_skills", *inactive_skills)

print(f"Attempted to remove: {len(inactive_skills)} terms")
print(f"Actually removed: {count} terms")
print(f"\nNote: {len(inactive_skills) - count} terms were not in dictionary")
Cleaning up inactive terms... ================================================== Attempted to remove: 4 terms Actually removed: 2 terms Note: 2 terms were not in dictionary
Example 4: Django management command for dictionary maintenance
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
import redis

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Remove terms from spell check dictionary'
    
    def add_arguments(self, parser):
        parser.add_argument('dict_name', type=str)
        parser.add_argument('terms', nargs='+', type=str)
    
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        dict_name = options['dict_name']
        terms = [t.lower() for t in options['terms']]
        
        try:
            count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDEL", dict_name, *terms)
            
            self.stdout.write(
                self.style.SUCCESS(f"✓ Removed {count} terms from {dict_name}")
            )
            
            if count < len(terms):
                self.stdout.write(
                    self.style.WARNING(
                        f"ℹ {len(terms) - count} terms were not found"
                    )
                )
        except Exception as e:
            self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f"✗ Error: {e}"))

# Simulate
print("Command: python manage.py remove_dict_terms tech_skills flash ie6")
print("\n✓ Removed 2 terms from tech_skills")
Command: python manage.py remove_dict_terms tech_skills flash ie6 ✓ Removed 2 terms from tech_skills
Example 5: Sync dictionary with Django model
def sync_dictionary_with_db():
    """Ensure dictionary only contains active skills from database"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    from jobpost.models import Skills
    
    # Get current active skills from DB
    active_skills = set(
        Skills.objects.filter(is_active=True)
        .values_list('name', flat=True)
    )
    active_skills = {s.lower() for s in active_skills if s}
    
    # Get all terms from dictionary (via DICTDUMP)
    dict_terms = set(r.execute_command("FT.DICTDUMP", "job_skills"))
    
    # Find terms to remove (in dict but not in DB)
    to_remove = dict_terms - active_skills
    
    if to_remove:
        count = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDEL", "job_skills", *list(to_remove))
        print(f"✓ Removed {count} inactive terms")
        print(f"  Examples: {', '.join(list(to_remove)[:5])}")
    else:
        print("✓ Dictionary is synchronized with database")

# Simulate
sync_dictionary_with_db()
✓ Removed 8 inactive terms Examples: flash, coldfusion, silverlight, vbscript, ie6

FT.DICTDUMP

Docs: Redis FT.DICTDUMP Documentation

Returns all terms in a dictionary.

Full description

FT.DICTDUMP retrieves all terms currently stored in a custom dictionary. Essential for Django admin interfaces, dictionary auditing, backup operations, and synchronizing dictionaries across environments. Useful for inspecting what terms are available for spell checking.

Syntax

FT.DICTDUMP dict

Parameters

  • dict: Dictionary name to dump (string).

Return value

Array: All terms in the dictionary.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: View all dictionary terms
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Get all terms from tech skills dictionary
terms = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDUMP", "tech_skills")

print(f"Tech Skills Dictionary ({len(terms)} terms):")
print("=" * 50)

# Display first 10 terms
for term in sorted(terms)[:10]:
    print(f"  - {term}")

if len(terms) > 10:
    print(f"  ... and {len(terms) - 10} more")
Tech Skills Dictionary (47 terms): ================================================== - angular - docker - django - elasticsearch - fastapi - golang - graphql - kubernetes - mongodb - nodejs ... and 37 more
Example 2: Django admin view for dictionary management
from django.contrib import admin
from django.http import HttpResponse
import redis
import json

class DictionaryAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    """Admin interface for viewing spell check dictionaries"""
    
    def get_dictionary_terms(self, dict_name):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        try:
            terms = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDUMP", dict_name)
            return sorted(terms)
        except Exception as e:
            return []
    
    def export_dictionary(self, request, dict_name):
        """Export dictionary as JSON"""
        terms = self.get_dictionary_terms(dict_name)
        
        data = {
            'dictionary': dict_name,
            'term_count': len(terms),
            'terms': terms
        }
        
        return HttpResponse(
            json.dumps(data, indent=2),
            content_type='application/json'
        )

# Example usage
admin_instance = DictionaryAdmin()
terms = admin_instance.get_dictionary_terms('tech_skills')

print(f"Dictionary Admin View:")
print(f"Found {len(terms)} terms in 'tech_skills'")
print(f"\nFirst 5 terms: {', '.join(terms[:5])}")
Dictionary Admin View: Found 47 terms in 'tech_skills' First 5 terms: angular, docker, django, elasticsearch, fastapi
Example 3: Backup dictionary to file
import json
from datetime import datetime

def backup_dictionary(dict_name, backup_dir='/tmp'):
    """Backup dictionary terms to JSON file"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    try:
        terms = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDUMP", dict_name)
        
        backup_data = {
            'dictionary': dict_name,
            'term_count': len(terms),
            'backup_time': datetime.now().isoformat(),
            'terms': sorted(terms)
        }
        
        filename = f"{backup_dir}/{dict_name}_backup.json"
        # In real app: write to file
        # with open(filename, 'w') as f:
        #     json.dump(backup_data, f, indent=2)
        
        print(f"Dictionary Backup: {dict_name}")
        print("=" * 50)
        print(f"Terms backed up: {len(terms)}")
        print(f"Backup file: {filename}")
        print(f"Timestamp: {backup_data['backup_time']}")
        
        return filename
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"✗ Backup failed: {e}")
        return None

backup_dictionary('tech_skills')
Dictionary Backup: tech_skills ================================================== Terms backed up: 47 Backup file: /tmp/tech_skills_backup.json Timestamp: 2024-08-15T14:23:45.123456
Example 4: Compare dictionaries across environments
def compare_dictionaries(dict_name, env1_redis, env2_redis):
    """Compare dictionary contents across environments"""
    
    # Get terms from both environments
    env1_terms = set(env1_redis.execute_command("FT.DICTDUMP", dict_name))
    env2_terms = set(env2_redis.execute_command("FT.DICTDUMP", dict_name))
    
    only_in_env1 = env1_terms - env2_terms
    only_in_env2 = env2_terms - env1_terms
    common = env1_terms & env2_terms
    
    print(f"Dictionary Comparison: {dict_name}")
    print("=" * 50)
    print(f"Environment 1: {len(env1_terms)} terms")
    print(f"Environment 2: {len(env2_terms)} terms")
    print(f"Common terms: {len(common)}")
    
    if only_in_env1:
        print(f"\nOnly in Env1 ({len(only_in_env1)}): {', '.join(list(only_in_env1)[:5])}")
    
    if only_in_env2:
        print(f"Only in Env2 ({len(only_in_env2)}): {', '.join(list(only_in_env2)[:5])}")
    
    return {
        'env1_only': only_in_env1,
        'env2_only': only_in_env2,
        'common': common
    }

# Simulate comparison
r1 = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
r2 = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

print("Simulated environment comparison:")
print("\nDictionary Comparison: tech_skills")
print("=" * 50)
print("Environment 1: 47 terms")
print("Environment 2: 42 terms")
print("Common terms: 40")
print("\nOnly in Env1 (7): kubernetes, typescript, webassembly")
print("Only in Env2 (2): flash, silverlight")
Simulated environment comparison: Dictionary Comparison: tech_skills ================================================== Environment 1: 47 terms Environment 2: 42 terms Common terms: 40 Only in Env1 (7): kubernetes, typescript, webassembly Only in Env2 (2): flash, silverlight
Example 5: Django management command to list dictionaries
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
import redis

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'List all terms in a spell check dictionary'
    
    def add_arguments(self, parser):
        parser.add_argument('dict_name', type=str)
        parser.add_argument('--export', type=str, help='Export to file')
    
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
        
        dict_name = options['dict_name']
        
        try:
            terms = r.execute_command("FT.DICTDUMP", dict_name)
            
            self.stdout.write(f"\nDictionary: {dict_name}")
            self.stdout.write(f"Total terms: {len(terms)}\n")
            
            for term in sorted(terms):
                self.stdout.write(f"  {term}")
            
            if options['export']:
                # Export logic here
                self.stdout.write(
                    self.style.SUCCESS(f"\n✓ Exported to {options['export']}")
                )
        except Exception as e:
            self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f"✗ Error: {e}"))

# Simulate
print("Command: python manage.py list_dict_terms tech_skills")
print("\nDictionary: tech_skills")
print("Total terms: 47")
print("\n  angular\n  django\n  docker\n  ...")
Command: python manage.py list_dict_terms tech_skills Dictionary: tech_skills Total terms: 47 angular django docker ...

FT.TAGVALS

Docs: Redis FT.TAGVALS Documentation

Returns all distinct values for a tag field.

Full description

FT.TAGVALS retrieves all unique values indexed in a TAG field. Perfect for Django applications to dynamically generate filter options, faceted search interfaces, or autocomplete suggestions based on actual indexed data rather than database queries.

Syntax

FT.TAGVALS index field_name

Parameters

  • index: The index name (string).
  • field_name: The TAG field name (string).

Return value

Array: All distinct values for the specified TAG field.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Get all job types for filters
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Get all job types from indexed data
job_types = r.execute_command("FT.TAGVALS", "job_search_idx", "type")

print("Available Job Types:")
for job_type in sorted(job_types):
    print(f"  - {job_type}")
Available Job Types: - Contract - Full Time - Internship - Part Time
Example 2: Django view for dynamic filter options
from django.shortcuts import render
import redis

def job_search_filters_view(request):
    """Generate filter options from indexed data"""
    r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
    
    # Get all possible filter values
    job_types = r.execute_command("FT.TAGVALS", "job_search_idx", "type")
    experience_levels = r.execute_command("FT.TAGVALS", "job_search_idx", "experience_level")
